title: Squad Community
heading: Squad Community
text:
# Squad Community
Squad is built by contributors who believe in democratizing multi-agent development. This page recognizes everyone who shapes the project — through code, feedback, ideas, and engagement.

title: Squad Community
heading: How to Contribute
text:
We welcome contributions in all forms:
For detailed contribution guidelines, including branch rules and PR process, see the Contributing Guide.

title: Squad Community
heading: Report Issues
text: Found a bug, have a feature request, or want to discuss an idea? Open an issue.

title: Squad Community
heading: Submit a Pull Request
text: Ready to contribute code? Check out the open issues and submit a PR. See the Contributing Guide for branch protection rules and the branch model.

title: Squad Community
heading: Join the Discussion
text: Have questions, want to share how you're using Squad, or discuss the roadmap? Start a discussion.

title: Squad Community
heading: External Deployments & Case Studies

title: Architecture
heading: Architecture
text:
# Architecture

⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.

How Squad works — one page, no handwaving.

title: Architecture
heading: Coordinator
text: The coordinator is Squad's routing engine. It reads your request, checks routing rules in , and decides which agents to spawn. If you say "team," it decomposes the work and launches multiple agents in parallel. If you name an agent, it routes directly to them.

title: Architecture
heading: Agents
text: Each agent is a specialist with a charter, role, and persistent memory. Agents are spawned as independent subprocesses with their own context windows and tools. They read  and their own history before working, then write results back. Agents never see each other's conversations — the coordinator orchestrates coordination.

title: Architecture
heading: Memory (.squad/)
text: All team state lives in . This includes the roster (), routing rules (), decisions (), agent charters and histories (), and ceremony schedules (). Agents read this before every spawn. You own these files — edit them anytime.

title: Architecture
heading: Routing
text: Routing rules in  define which agent handles which work. The coordinator reads these rules before spawning. You can override routing by naming an agent directly in your request.

title: Architecture
heading: Scribe
text: The Scribe is a silent agent that tracks decisions and logs sessions. Every team has a Scribe. You never talk to them directly — they work in the background, merging decisions from all agents into .

title: Architecture
heading: Ralph
text: Ralph is the work monitor. He watches your GitHub or GitLab issues, tracks work in progress, and alerts the team when something is ready. Every team has a Ralph. He's silent unless you ask him for status.

title: Architecture
heading: What happens when you say "Team, build X"?
text:
Coordinator reads the request and checks  for decomposition rules.
Coordinator spawns multiple agents in parallel — one for frontend, one for backend, one for tests, etc.
Each agent reads  and their own history (), then works independently.
Agents write results to their history files and propose decisions.
Scribe merges all decisions into .
Coordinator returns labeled results to you, tagged with each agent's name.

title: Architecture
heading: Learn more
text:
Work routing — How the coordinator decides which agents to spawn
Memory and knowledge — How decisions, skills, and history persist
Parallel work — How agents work simultaneously without conflicts

title: GitHub Integration
heading: GitHub Integration
text:
# GitHub Integration
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Squad plugs directly into your GitHub workflow — issues become branches, branches become PRs, PRs become merged code. No context-switching, no copy-paste, no ticket juggling. Just tell your squad what to build and watch the commits roll in.

title: GitHub Integration
heading: How It Works
text:
The lifecycle is simple: connect → backlog → work → PR → merge.
  You say   What happens  
 --------- ------------- 
     Stores issue source in  (once per project)  
     Fetches and displays open issues in a table  
     Agent creates branch, implements, opens PR  
     Parallel work — each issue gets its own branch and PR  
     Author agent reads comments and pushes fixes  
     Squash-merge, delete branch, close linked issue  
     Refreshes backlog, shows remaining open issues  
Prerequisite: Install and authenticate the  CLI (). Squad uses it for all GitHub operations.

title: GitHub Integration
heading: Working with your team
text: Squad is built for mixed teams — humans set direction, AI agents execute and report back. The Lead agent bridges between them, routing work and surfacing decisions when a human needs to act.

title: GitHub Integration
heading: Humans in the lifecycle
text:
The following is one example of how a mixed team might divide responsibilities. Your team decides its own process — use ceremonies and directives to shape the workflow that fits.
  Stage   Who acts   What happens  
 ------- ---------- -------------- 
  Triage   Human (or Lead)   Applies  /  — decides what's worth building  
  Design review   Human + team   Auto-triggered ceremony before multi-agent work; humans can participate or observe  
  Implementation   AI agents   Branch, build, test, open PRs — no human input required  
  PR review   Human   Reviews and approves (or requests changes); lockout protocol prevents conflicting edits  
  Merge   Human or Ralph   Squash-merge, branch cleanup, issue closed  
This is a starting point. Define your own checkpoints by configuring ceremonies and capturing directives.
For details on how work routes to humans, see Human team members.
For ceremony details, see Ceremonies.
For agent anatomy and how each team member (AI, human, @copilot) is structured, see your-team.md.

title: GitHub Integration
heading: Label Taxonomy
text:
Labels aren't just tags — they're Squad's state machine. Five namespaces drive workflow automation, routing, and lifecycle tracking.
  Namespace   Purpose   Example Values   Mutual Exclusivity  
 ----------- --------- ---------------- ------------------- 
     Verdict   , ,    ✅ One per issue  
     Release target   ,    ✅ One per issue  
     Issue category   , , , , ,    ✅ One per issue  
     Urgency   , ,    ✅ One per issue  
     Agent assignment   ,    ❌ Multiple OK (pair work)  
Within , , , and , applying a second label auto-removes the first. The  namespace allows multiple labels for collaborative work.

title: GitHub Integration
heading: How Labels Drive Automation
text:
Labels power four automation layers:
Enforcement —  watches for changes and removes duplicates within a namespace.
Sync — Cross-namespace cascading:  → auto-adds ;  → ensures .
Triage — Ralph uses labels to route work:  → Fenster picks it up; no  +  → routes based on .
Heartbeat —  runs every 30 minutes, auto-triaging unassigned issues and escalating stale research.

title: GitHub Integration
heading: State Machine Flow
text: Labels are created automatically during  or . Add custom labels with:

title: GitHub Integration
heading: Ralph — Work Monitor
text: Ralph is a built-in squad member who tracks the work queue, monitors CI status, and keeps the team moving. He's always on the roster — no casting required.

title: GitHub Integration
heading: Talking to Ralph
text:
You say   What happens  
 --------- ------------- 
     Activates the self-chaining work loop  
     Single check cycle, reports board state  
     Stops the loop  
     Monitors only issues, skips PRs/CI

title: GitHub Integration
heading: What Ralph Monitors
text:
Signal   Action  
 -------- -------- 
  Untriaged issues (no  label)   Lead triages and assigns  
  Assigned but unstarted issues   Spawns agent to pick it up  
  Draft PRs from squad members   Checks if agent is stalled  
  Review feedback on PRs   Routes to author agent  
  CI failures   Notifies agent to fix  
  Approved PRs   Merges and closes issue  
Ralph never stops on his own while work remains — he keeps cycling until the board clears, you say "idle", or the session ends. Every 3–5 rounds he posts a status update and keeps going.

title: GitHub Integration
heading: Three Layers of Ralph
text:
Layer   When   How  
 ------- ------ ----- 
  In-session   You're at the keyboard    — active loop  
  Local watchdog   You're AFK but machine is on     
  Cloud heartbeat   Fully unattended    GitHub Actions events  
The heartbeat workflow () is installed during  or . It runs on issue close, PR merge, and manual dispatch. Edit the workflow in  to customize triggers. For periodic polling without events, use  locally.
PAT requirement: Ralph needs  CLI authenticated with a Classic PAT (scopes:  and ). The default  doesn't have sufficient scopes.

title: GitHub Integration
heading: PRD Mode
text:
Got a product spec? Hand it to Squad and the Lead decomposes it into prioritized, dependency-tracked work items.
The Lead agent:
Decomposes the spec into discrete work items (WI-1, WI-2, etc.)
Assigns priorities: P0 (must-have), P1 (important), P2 (nice-to-have)
Routes items to agents based on domain expertise
Tracks dependencies — won't start WI-4 if it depends on WI-2
Independent items run in parallel. When requirements change, give Squad the updated PRD — the Lead diffs against existing items and adjusts the backlog without undoing completed work.

title: GitHub Integration
heading: Project Boards
text:
Squad integrates with GitHub Projects V2 for visual workflow tracking. Labels are the source of truth — boards are one-way projections that visualize the state machine.
  Board Column   Label State  
 -------------- ------------- 
  Backlog    or   
  Needs Research     
  Ready   , no   
  In Progress    +   
  Done   Issue closed  
Board sync runs on label changes, issue close, PR merge, and a 30-minute schedule. Dragging an issue on the board triggers a webhook that applies the corresponding label.
Status: Label-based state machine is fully implemented. Automated board sync workflows are in development for v0.4.0. You can use  commands now — full automation is coming.

title: GitHub Integration
heading: Notifications
text:
Your squad pings you when they need input, hit an error, or finish work. Squad uses MCP-based notification servers — you bring your own delivery channel.
See the Notifications Guide for platform setup (Teams, Discord, iMessage, webhooks), trigger configuration, and sample MCP configs.

title: GitHub Integration
heading: Tips
text:
You don't need to assign issues to agents — Squad routes based on domain expertise defined in charters and .
If  isn't authenticated, Squad will tell you. Run  first.
Use  to fast-track critical items — it auto-sets .
Combine PRD mode with GitHub Issues to auto-create issues from work items.
Ralph's in-session loop is session-scoped — state resets between sessions. Use  or the heartbeat for persistent monitoring.

title: GitHub Integration
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Links Squad to a GitHub repo and displays all open issues.
Processes multiple bug issues in parallel — each gets its own branch and PR.
Applies  and  labels, removing any conflicting labels.
Activates Ralph's self-chaining loop to continuously triage, assign, and process work.
Ingests a product spec and creates a prioritized, dependency-tracked backlog.
The author agent reads review comments and pushes fixes to the existing branch.
Queries issues with .
Starts persistent local polling — checks GitHub every 5 minutes for new work and triages automatically.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Memory & Knowledge
text:
# Memory & Knowledge
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Squad remembers everything — coding conventions, architecture decisions, deployment patterns, your personal preferences. Memory grows with every session, compounding across three layers so agents stop making the same mistakes and start anticipating your needs.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: How It Works
text: Memory lives in three layers, each serving a different purpose:

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: How Memory Compounds
text:
Stage   What Agents Know  
 ------- ----------------- 
  🌱 First session   Project description, tech stack, your name  
  🌿 After a few sessions   Conventions, component patterns, API design, test strategies  
  🌳 Mature project   Full architecture, tech debt map, regression patterns, performance conventions  
The first session is always the least capable. Give the team a few sessions to build up context — they'll stop asking questions they've already answered.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Personal Memory: `history.md`
text:
Each agent has its own history file at . After every session, agents append what they learned — architecture decisions, conventions, file paths, user preferences.
Only that agent reads its own history. This means each team member builds specialized knowledge about their domain. Kane learns the auth system inside and out. Dallas masters the component library. Lambert memorizes the test infrastructure.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Progressive Summarization
text: When an agent's  exceeds 12KB, older entries get archived into a summary section. Recent entries stay detailed; older entries condense. This keeps files within a useful context budget without losing accumulated knowledge.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Shared Decisions: `decisions.md`
text:
Team-wide decisions live in . Every agent reads this before working. This is the team's shared brain.
Decisions get captured three ways:
From agent work — agents write to 
From your directives — when you say "always…" or "never…" (see below)
From Scribe merges — the Scribe agent periodically consolidates inbox files into the canonical , deduplicating overlapping entries

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Decision Archiving
text:
As your project grows,  accumulates hundreds of blocks. Stale sprint artifacts and one-time planning fragments consume context without adding value. When this happens, old decisions archive to  — preserved for reference but no longer loaded into agent context.
Active decisions (ongoing policies, user preferences, current architecture) stay in . Agents always read the lean, current shared brain.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Directives
text: Directives are team rules that persist across sessions. Say "always" or "never" and Squad captures it permanently. Every agent reads directives before working.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Signal Word Detection
text:
The coordinator listens for these phrases and captures them as directives:
  Phrase   Example  
 -------- --------- 
     "Always use TypeScript strict mode"  
     "Never commit directly to main"  
     "From now on, prefix all commits with the issue number"  
     "Remember to run tests before pushing"  
     "Don't use var — only let and const"  
     "Make sure to document all public APIs"

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Directive Scope
text:
Directives can shape:
Coding style — formatting, naming conventions, language features
Tool preferences — linters, formatters, test runners
Workflow rules — branch naming, commit messages, PR templates
Scope constraints — "Don't touch legacy/ directory"
Review requirements — "Always have Lead review security changes"

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Directive Conflicts
text: When a new directive contradicts an existing one, the Scribe detects the overlap and asks you: "Replace, merge, or skip?" You decide, and  updates accordingly.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Viewing and Removing Directives
text: You can also edit  directly — it's plain Markdown.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Compliance
text: Directives are context-aware guidelines, not hard constraints. If an agent violates one, the reviewer protocol catches it during review, or you flag it directly.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Skills
text:
Skills are reusable knowledge files that live at . Unlike decisions (project policies like "use PostgreSQL"), skills are transferable techniques ("how to set up CI with GitHub Actions").
All agents can read any skill. Skills are team-wide knowledge, not per-agent.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Starter vs. Earned
text:
Type   Source   Example  
 ------ -------- --------- 
  Starter   Bundled at init, prefixed      
  Earned   Written by agents from real work     
Starter skills are overwritten on upgrade. Earned skills are never touched.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Confidence Lifecycle
text:
Earned skills have a confidence level reflecting how battle-tested they are:
  Level   Meaning  
 ------- --------- 
  Low   First written — based on a single experience  
  Medium   Applied successfully in multiple contexts  
  High   Well-established, consistently reliable  
Confidence only goes up, never down. A skill that reaches  stays there.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: How Skills Get Used
text:
Before working — agents read skill files relevant to the task
During routing — the coordinator checks skills when deciding who to spawn (an agent with a relevant earned skill may be preferred)
After working — agents may write new skills or update existing ones based on what they learned

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Portability
text: Skills export and import with your team. Move a trained team to a new repo, and all their earned knowledge comes along. This makes skills the most portable form of team intelligence.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Knowledge persistence
text:
Not all knowledge in  lasts forever. When files grow large, Squad compacts them to keep performance fast. Here's what persists and what gets summarized:
  What   File   Compacted?   Where old content goes   Who reads it  
 ------ ------ ----------- ---------------------- ------------- 
  Personal history      Yes, at 12 KB    (preserved, read-only)   Owning agent  
  Shared decisions      Yes, when large    (preserved, not loaded)   All agents  
  Skills      Never   Grows indefinitely   All agents  
  Directives   In    With decisions   Archived with decisions   All agents  
  Wisdom      Never   Permanent   All agents  
  Casting registry      Never   Permanent   Coordinator  
  Session logs      Never edited   Append-only archive   Read-only  
  Orchestration logs      Never edited   Append-only archive   Read-only  
Knowledge that needs to survive compaction belongs in skills. Reusable patterns, code conventions, and technical techniques live here because they grow without limits. Team rules and preferences go in directives (stored in ) — they persist through compaction cycles because directives are preserved when decisions get summarized.
💡 Where to store permanent knowledge: Put reusable patterns and techniques in . Put team rules and preferences in directives (they persist in ). For org-wide knowledge that multiple teams need, use upstream inheritance to share a skills library.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Tips
text:
Commit  — anyone who clones the repo gets the team with all their accumulated knowledge.
Directives ("always…", "never…") are the fastest way to shape team behavior. Use them liberally.
If an agent keeps making the same mistake, check  — the relevant convention might be missing.
You can edit , , and skill files directly. They're all plain Markdown.
Manually seed skills by pasting your existing conventions into a  — instant team knowledge.

title: Memory & Knowledge
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Creates a coding style directive all agents will follow.
Sets a workflow directive — agents format commits as , , , etc.
Queries Kane's personal  for relevant context.
Searches  for a particular topic.
Manually creates a new skill file and guides you through documenting the pattern.
Finds recently-created skills that haven't been validated across multiple contexts yet.
Establishes a type safety directive. Agents will avoid  and use proper types.
Finds historical decisions related to a specific topic or keyword.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Parallel Work & Models
text:
# Parallel Work & Models
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Squad launches independent work in parallel by default — multiple agents working simultaneously, no waiting. It also picks the right AI model for each agent based on what they're doing, so you get quality where it counts and speed everywhere else.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: How Parallel Execution Works
text:
When the coordinator receives a multi-part task, it follows a fan-out pattern:
Dependency Analysis — Check if tasks have data dependencies (A needs output from B).
Fan-Out — Launch all independent agents in parallel using background mode.
Wait — Coordinator polls agent status until all complete.
Collect — Aggregate results, check for errors, route to next step.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Example
text:
"Implement user authentication: API endpoints, frontend form, tests, and documentation"
Coordinator spawns 4 agents in parallel:
Backend → API endpoints
Frontend → Login/signup form
Tester → Integration tests
DevRel → Auth documentation
All work simultaneously. No agent waits unless there's a code dependency.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Background vs. Sync
text:
Mode   When Used   Behavior  
 ------ ----------- ---------- 
  Background   Independent work, no data dependencies   Agents run in parallel, coordinator polls for completion  
  Sync   One agent needs another's output   Agents run sequentially, coordinator waits  
  Sync   Reviewer gate (Lead must approve first)   Agent runs, coordinator waits for review decision

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Background (Fan-Out)
text: Agents don't see each other's output until the coordinator collects and synthesizes.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Sync (Dependencies & Gates)
text: Each step blocks until the previous completes.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Eager Execution
text:
Squad's default is eager parallelism — launch everything that can run, let the coordinator handle synchronization.
Faster throughput — no artificial sequencing
Better utilization — multiple agents saturate available compute
Resilient — if one agent stalls, others keep working
Trade-off: increased API cost. If cost is a concern:

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Deadlock Avoidance
text: When agents have circular dependencies (A needs B, B needs A), the coordinator detects the cycle and asks you to pick a resolution: run A first, run B first, or redesign.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Concurrency Limits
text:
Default: 5 agents in parallel
Adjustable:  → Coordinator batches work accordingly

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Model Selection
text: Squad routes each agent to the right AI model based on what they're doing — not a one-size-fits-all default.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Selection Layers
text:
First match wins:
  Layer   How It Works  
 ------- ------------- 
  1. User Override   You said  or  — done, session-wide  
  2. Charter Preference   Agent's charter has a  section  
  3. Task-Aware Auto   Coordinator checks what the agent is actually doing (see table below)  
  4. Default    — cost wins when in doubt

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Task-Aware Defaults
text:
Task Output   Model   Tier  
 ------------- ------- ------ 
  Writing code (implementation, refactoring, tests, bug fixes)      Standard  
  Writing prompts or agent designs      Standard  
  Non-code work (docs, planning, triage, changelogs)      Fast  
  Visual/design work requiring image analysis      Premium

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Role-to-Model Mapping
text:
Role   Default Model   Why  
 ------ -------------- ----- 
  Core Dev / Backend / Frontend      Writes code — quality first  
  Tester / QA      Writes test code  
  Lead / Architect   auto (per-task)   Mixed: code review vs. planning  
  Prompt Engineer   auto (per-task)   Prompt design is like code  
  DevRel / Writer      Docs — not code  
  Scribe / Logger      Mechanical file ops  
  Git / Release      Changelogs, tags, version bumps  
  Designer / Visual      Vision capability required

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Model Catalog (16 models)
text:
Squad supports models across three tiers:
Premium: claude-opus-4.6, claude-opus-4.6-fast, claude-opus-4.5
Standard: claude-sonnet-4.5, gpt-5.2-codex, claude-sonnet-4, gpt-5.2, gpt-5.1-codex, gpt-5.1, gpt-5, gemini-3-pro-preview
Fast/Cheap: claude-haiku-4.5, gpt-5.1-codex-mini, gpt-4.1, gpt-5-mini, gpt-5.1-codex-mini

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Fallback Chains
text: If a model is unavailable (plan restriction, rate limit, deprecation), Squad silently retries with the next in chain. Never falls back up in tier — a fast task won't land on a premium model.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
text: Add the GitHub Copilot coding agent to your Squad as an autonomous team member. It picks up issues, creates branches, and opens PRs — all without a chat session.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Prerequisites
text:
Copilot coding agent enabled on the repo (Settings → Copilot → Coding agent)
 exists in 
GitHub Actions enabled on the repo

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: How @copilot Differs
text:
AI Agent   Human Member   @copilot  
 --- ---------- ------------- ---------- 
  Badge   ✅ Active   👤 Human   🤖 Coding Agent  
  Charter   ✅   ❌   ❌ (uses )  
  Works in session   ✅   ❌   ❌ (async via issue assignment)  
  Creates PRs   Via session   Outside Squad   Autonomously

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Capability Profile
text:
The profile in  controls what @copilot handles:
  Tier   Meaning   Examples  
 ------ --------- ---------- 
  🟢 Good fit   Route automatically   Bug fixes, test coverage, lint fixes, dependency updates, small features, docs  
  🟡 Needs review   Route but flag for review   Medium features with specs, refactoring with tests, API additions  
  🔴 Not suitable   Route to a squad member   Architecture, multi-system design, security-critical, ambiguous requirements

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Auto-Assign Flow
text:
When the  label is added to an issue:
Workflow posts a routing comment
Workflow assigns  to the issue
Coding agent creates a  branch and opens a draft PR
Auto-assign requires a classic PAT stored as  (fine-grained PATs return 403 for this endpoint).

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Git Worktrees
text: Squad supports git worktrees with two strategies for teams working across multiple branches simultaneously.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Worktree-Local (Independent State)
text:
Each worktree gets its own  directory. Agents in one worktree don't see state from another.
Best for: multiple features with different teams, experimental branches, different compositions per worktree.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Main-Checkout (Shared State)
text:
All worktrees share  from the main checkout via symlink.
Best for: same team on multiple branches, coordinated parallel development, solo dev with multiple branches.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Which Strategy?
text:
Scenario   Strategy  
 ---------- ---------- 
  Parallel features, same team   Main-checkout  
  Experimental branch, isolated team   Worktree-local  
  Hotfix + feature branch   Main-checkout  
  Multiple teams in same repo   Worktree-local  
Setup is one command:  (creates symlink) or  (creates independent ).
Squad uses  for append-only log files to avoid conflicts across worktrees.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Tips
text:
Eager parallelism is the default. Only switch to sequential if cost is a real concern.
Start conservative with @copilot's capability profile and expand as you see what it handles well.
Use  labels with issue-driven development for fully autonomous processing.
Fallback chains are silent — you won't notice model switches unless you ask .
For worktrees, main-checkout is usually the right choice unless you need truly isolated teams.

title: Parallel Work & Models
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Coordinator spawns all relevant agents (Frontend, Backend, Tester, DevRel) simultaneously.
Spawns 3 agents in parallel, one per issue.
Forces sync mode: Backend completes, then Tester starts.
Sets concurrency limit. Coordinator batches work in groups.
One-off override to premium model for a high-stakes task.
Session-wide preference for the cheapest model tier.
Adds @copilot to the roster and configures automatic issue assignment.
Creates a symlink so this worktree shares the main checkout's  state.

title: Portability & Extensions
heading: Portability & Extensions
text:
# Portability & Extensions
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Your squad isn't locked to one repo, one editor, or one set of tools. Export a trained team and import it somewhere else. Install plugins for instant expertise. Inherit org-wide practices from upstream repos. Wire up MCP servers so agents can talk to anything.

title: Portability & Extensions
heading: How It Works
text:
Squad is designed to be portable by default. Four systems make this possible:
  System   What It Does  
 -------- ------------- 
  Export/Import   Snapshot your entire team to a JSON file, import it anywhere  
  Plugins   Install community bundles of agent templates, skills, and best practices  
  Upstream Inheritance   Inherit skills, decisions, and routing from other repos  
  MCP Servers   Extend agents with external services (GitHub, Trello, notifications)

title: Portability & Extensions
heading: Export & Import
text: Squad teams are portable. Export your trained agents, casting state, skills, and decisions to a single JSON file.

title: Portability & Extensions
heading: What's Included
text:
Data   Included  
 ------ ---------- 
  Agent charters   ✅  
  Agent histories   ✅ (split into portable vs project-specific)  
  Casting state   ✅  
  Skills   ✅ All earned skills from   
  Decisions   ✅  
Skills are fully portable — they export and import with perfect fidelity.

title: Your Team
heading: Your Team
text:
# Your Team
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Squad builds you a team of AI specialists that live in your repo. Tell it what you're working on, and it proposes a roster — backend devs, testers, writers, a lead — each with their own personality, expertise, and memory. Your team grows smarter every session.

title: Your Team
heading: How It Works
text:
When you first run Squad in a repository, it walks through a four-step init flow:
Discovery — Squad scans your repo: languages, file structure, test frameworks, dependencies, existing workflows.
Proposal — It suggests a roster with 3–7 members tailored to what it found.
Confirmation — You review and customize: accept as-is, add roles, remove roles, rename members.
Creation — Squad writes the  directory, creates charters, and sets up the coordinator.

title: Your Team
heading: Default Team Composition
text:
Role   When Included  
 ------ -------------- 
  Lead   Always — triages, reviews, unblocks  
  Core Dev   Always — main implementation  
  Tester   If tests exist or test deps detected  
  DevRel   If README exists or  present  
  Frontend   If React/Vue/Svelte/Angular detected  
  Backend   If API routes, database code, or server framework detected  
  Scribe   Always — silent decision logger

title: Your Team
heading: Human Team Members
text:
Not every team member needs to be AI. Add real people for decisions that need a human — design sign-off, security review, product approval.
Sarah appears on the roster with a 👤 Human badge.
    AI Agent   Human Member  
 --- ---------- ------------- 
  Badge   Role-specific emoji   👤 Human  
  Charter   ✅   ❌  
  History   ✅   ❌  
  Spawned as sub-agent   ✅   ❌  
  Can review work   ✅   ✅  
When work routes to a human, Squad pauses and tells you someone needs to act. You relay the task outside of Squad, then report back what happened. Stale reminders keep things moving.
Not sure whether someone should be a roster member or just a normal GitHub collaborator? See When to add a human member for a decision framework.

title: Your Team
heading: Work Routing
text:
The coordinator routes work automatically using three strategies. First match wins:
  Strategy   How It Works   Example  
 ---------- ------------- --------- 
  Named   You say who does it     
  Domain   Pattern matching in     → Backend  
  Skill-aware   Capability check in    Auth expertise → Backend or Lead  
Routing priority: Named > Domain > Skill-aware. If nothing matches, the Lead triages.

title: Your Team
heading: Sample Routing Table
text: GitHub issues with  labels route directly —  goes to Fenster, no triage needed.

title: Your Team
heading: Multi-Agent Work
text:
Some tasks need multiple agents:
The coordinator spawns both in parallel. They work independently and coordinate through shared  state. See Parallel Work & Models for details.

title: Your Team
heading: Reviewer Protocol
text:
When a reviewer (Lead, Tester) rejects work, the original agent gets locked out — no self-revision allowed. This prevents endless fix-retry loops.
  Outcome   What Happens  
 --------- ------------- 
  Approve   PR merges, issue closes, agent unlocked  
  Request changes   Author locked out, work reassigned or escalated

title: Your Team
heading: Lockout Details
text:
Task-specific — locked out of that PR/issue, not all work
Session-persistent — survives restarts (stored in )
Clearable —

title: Your Team
heading: Reviewer Authority
text:
Reviewer   Scope  
 ---------- ------- 
  Lead   Code quality, architecture, security — all submissions  
  Tester   Correctness, test coverage — test-related changes  
  You   Final arbiter — can override any decision

title: Your Team
heading: Deadlock Handling
text: If all capable agents are locked out, the coordinator escalates to you with options: manual fix, unlock with guidance, or close as won't-fix.

title: Your Team
heading: Ceremonies
text:
Structured team meetings that trigger at key moments — automatically or on demand.
  Ceremony   Auto-Triggers When   What Happens  
 ---------- ------------------- ------------- 
  Design Review   Multi-agent task with 2+ agents modifying shared systems   Lead facilitates; agents weigh in on interfaces, risks, contracts  
  Retrospective   Build failures, test failures, reviewer rejections   Lead runs root-cause analysis; decisions written to   
Run either manually anytime:
You can also create custom ceremonies, disable auto-triggers, or skip a ceremony for a single task. Config lives in .

title: Your Team
heading: Response Modes
text:
Squad auto-selects the right level of effort for each request:
  Mode   Time   What Happens   Triggered By  
 ------ ------ ------------- ------------- 
  Direct   2–3s   Coordinator answers from memory, no agent spawned   Status checks, factual questions  
  Lightweight   8–12s   One agent, minimal prompt — skips charter/history/decisions   Small fixes, typos, quick follow-ups  
  Standard   25–35s   Full agent spawn with charter, history, and decisions   Normal work requests  
  Full   40–60s   Multi-agent parallel spawn, may trigger design review   Complex multi-domain tasks  
Pro tip:  prompts trigger Full mode. Named agent prompts () trigger Standard. Quick questions get Direct automatically.

title: Your Team
heading: Customizing After Init
text:
What You Say   What Happens  
 -------------- ------------- 
     Coordinator casts a new member, creates charter, updates routing  
     Archives agent directory to , updates team.md  
     Updates the tester's charter and expertise  
     Adds a rule to   
     Creates routing rule + directive  
Running  on an existing Squad repo automatically offers upgrade mode.

title: Your Team
heading: Planning your team
text:
Before running , think through these decisions. Squad will scan your repo and propose a team — having answers ready makes setup faster.
What does your project do? Have a 1–2 sentence description of the language, stack, and purpose ready — Squad uses this to pick roles
What roles do you need? The default composition covers common cases, or let Squad propose custom roles based on your repo
How many agents? Typical teams are 3–7 agents. Scribe (memory) is always included
Will humans join the team? Human members can serve as reviewers or domain experts alongside AI agents
Will @copilot participate? The GitHub Copilot coding agent can pick up issues autonomously — see Agent anatomy
How will you track work? GitHub Issues with  labels, or conversational tasking via named prompts
Do you want review gates? Reviewers can approve or reject work before it proceeds
What ceremonies matter? Design reviews and retrospectives can auto-trigger or run on demand
What model preferences? Default is automatic selection, or specify preferred models per agent — see Parallel Work & Models
How many squads, and where do they live? One squad per repo is the default — your  directory lives alongside your code. For multi-repo projects, you can run one squad per repo (each with its own team) or share a single squad across repos using a personal squad or linked team repo. Start with one squad in one repo and expand as needed.

title: Your Team
heading: Agent anatomy
text:
An agent is a directory at . The contents depend on the member type.
For how humans differ from AI agents, see Human team members above.
    @copilot (🤖)  
 --- --- 
  Directory   None  
     ❌ Uses   
     ❌  
  Spawnable   ❌ Works via issue assignment  
AI agents have a  (identity, expertise, voice — compiled into the system prompt at spawn time) and an optional  (append-only cross-session learnings).
@copilot (🤖) appears on the roster and works via GitHub issue assignment. It reads  instead of a charter.
Retired agents move to  — charter preserved as a read-only archive, not spawnable.

title: Your Team
heading: Cross-agent context
text:
Agents don't share memory directly. Context flows through explicit shared files:
 — who's on the team and what they do
 — work assignment rules the coordinator reads on every request
 — canonical team memory: directives, patterns, learnings
 — agents drop decision files here; the Scribe merges them into 
Each agent's  is personal — only that agent reads it at spawn time. For the full picture on knowledge flow, see Memory and knowledge.

title: Your Team
heading: Hiring an agent
text:
To add a new AI agent to your team:
[ ] Create  directory
[ ] Write  — start from 
[ ] Add to  roster with status 
[ ] Add to  with work type assignments
[ ] (Optional) Create  for persistent memory
[ ] (Optional) Allocate a name via the casting system
To add a human member, skip the directory — just add them to . See Human team members for badge and routing details.

title: Your Team
heading: Tips
text:
Commit  to version control — anyone who clones the repo gets the full team with all accumulated knowledge.
Use human members for approval gates: design review, compliance, final sign-off.
Design reviews prevent agents from building conflicting implementations — let them run on multi-agent tasks.
Retros produce decisions that improve future work, not just diagnose the current failure.
You're the relay for human members. Squad can't message them directly — it tells you, and you coordinate.

title: Your Team
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Triggers init mode — Squad analyzes the repo and proposes a team.
Named routing to two agents. Both spawn in parallel.
Adds a human team member with a specific review responsibility.
Adds a domain routing rule to .
Triggers review — Lead evaluates and either approves (merge) or rejects (lockout author).
Clears lockout so Fenster can revise the PR with your additional context.
Starts a retrospective ceremony to analyze failures and capture learnings.
Coordinator checks routing and skills, reports the responsible agent(s).

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
text:
# Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
A compact cookbook of prompts, patterns, and power moves. Each recipe is a prompt you can paste straight into Squad.

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: New Project from Scratch
text:
"Set up Squad for a new React + Node.js app"
Create a repo, run , describe your project, and agents assemble a team based on your stack. Say "team" to trigger parallel work from the start. First session is the slowest — after 2–3 sessions, agents know your conventions.

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Joining Mid-Project
text:
"This project is already in progress — catch me up on what's been built and what's in the backlog"
Never too late. Run , describe the project as it is today, and let agents explore the codebase before giving tasks. Feed conventions they can't discover from code alone. After 2–3 sessions, agents are fully up to speed.

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Large Codebase (200K+ Lines)
text:
"This is a 200k line codebase — help me understand the architecture before we start making changes"
Each agent gets its own 200K context window. Routing ensures only the right agent looks at relevant code. Use routing rules in  to keep agents focused on their domain. Be explicit about scope on monorepos.

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Running Multiple Squads
text:
"Export skills from my React project and import them into this new project"
Import one full squad, then cherry-pick skills from others. Skills are standalone markdown files — just copy them into . Best practice: merge knowledge, don't run parallel squads.

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Moving a Team Between Repos
text:
"Export my team from project-a so I can import it into project-b"
Agents carry skills and portable knowledge. Project-specific details stay tagged so they don't bleed into the new project.

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Where to Store `.squad/`
text:
"Keep .squad/ out of my main branch"
Six storage options: committed (default), gitignored, separate branch, submodule, symlink, or dev-branch-only. Solo devs: just commit it. Enterprise: gitignore or submodule. Check the decision matrix above for your setup.

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Keeping Your Squad Across Projects
text:
"I want to keep my current team — don't cast a new one for this project"
Export often — at the end of each project or after a major milestone. Your squad gets smarter over time. Generic skills carry forward; project-specific details are stripped on export.

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Release Process
text:
"We're ready to ship v1.2.0 — run the release process: changelog, tags, and publish"
Squad uses a three-branch model: dev → preview → main. The guard workflow blocks  from reaching production. Tag from main only. Full lifecycle: prepare on dev, validate on preview, merge to main, tag, release.

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Open Source Maintainer
text:
"Enable auto-triage for incoming issues on my OSS repo"
Ralph triages issues every 6 hours via the heartbeat workflow. Skills become living contributor docs. Export your squad for forks.  labels + auto-assign = autonomous issue processing with human approval on merge.

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Private Repos & Security
text:
"I need to know Squad's data security model"
Squad runs entirely in your Copilot session. Nothing leaves your machine beyond Copilot's standard operation. Skills are generic and safe to share. Review agent histories before exporting — they may contain project-specific details.

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Switching Models
text:
"Switch everyone to Haiku — I'm trying to save costs this sprint"
Squad supports 17 models across three tiers. Budget mode:  for everything. Quality mode:  for the Lead,  for everyone else. Fallback chains handle unavailability automatically.

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Client Compatibility
text:
"Does Squad work in VS Code?"
CLI is the primary platform with full features. VS Code works with conditional support — parallel subagents, workspace-scoped file access, session model selection. JetBrains and GitHub.com are untested. Both CLI and VS Code share the same  state.

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Disaster Recovery
text:
"My .squad/ directory was deleted — help me recover the team state"
If committed: . If not: rebuild with  or import from a previous export. Override bad decisions with directives. Archive confused agent histories. Upgrades never touch .

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Pro Tips
text:
Patterns from real usage that make Squad click.
Be specific about scope. Describe the boundary, not just the task:
Say "team" for parallel work. Naming a specific agent sends work to just them:
Stack decisions in your prompt. Early conventions prevent agents from asking questions later:
Use bullet points for multi-part tasks. Agents process lists better than paragraphs.
Don't interrupt parallel work. Let agents chain their own follow-ups. Check the work log after, not during.
Let Ralph grind the backlog. Say  and Ralph triages, assigns, spawns agents, and reports every 3–5 rounds. You focus on critical-path work.
Decision first, implementation second. Before agents write code, have the team agree on the design:
Spike → Decision → Build. For hard problems, have the Lead do a spike first:

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: Power Prompts
text: Copy these directly into Squad.

title: Recipes & Advanced Scenarios
heading: See Also
text:
CLI Reference — Every command and config file
SDK Reference — Programmatic API
Migration & Troubleshooting — Upgrades and fixes

title: Built-in Base Roles
heading: Built-in Base Roles
text: # Built-in Base Roles

title: Built-in Base Roles
heading: Overview
text:
Squad ships with 20 curated base roles covering software development and business operations.
Base roles provide deep, substantive charter content out of the box — no LLM generation needed.
During team casting, base roles serve as starting points that get refined for your project context.
Role content is adapted from agency-agents by AgentLand Contributors (MIT License).

title: Built-in Base Roles
heading: Why Base Roles?
text:
Faster setup — deterministic role selection instead of LLM improvisation.
Lower token cost — base content provides 90% of the charter; only project-specific refinement needs the LLM.
Higher quality — curated expertise, boundaries, and voice instead of generic boilerplate.
Broad coverage — not just software dev; marketing, sales, product, game dev, and more.

title: Built-in Base Roles
heading: Software Development Roles (12)
text:
Emoji   ID   Title   Vibe  
 ------- ---- ------- ------ 
  🏗️      Lead / Architect   Designs systems that survive the team that built them. Every decision has a trade-off — name it.  
  ⚛️      Frontend Developer   Builds responsive, accessible web apps with pixel-perfect precision.  
  🔧      Backend Developer   Designs the systems that hold everything up — databases, APIs, cloud, scale.  
  💻      Full-Stack Developer   Sees the full picture — from the database to the pixel.  
  👁️      Code Reviewer   Reviews code like a mentor, not a gatekeeper. Every comment teaches something.  
  🧪      Test Engineer   Breaks your API before your users do.  
  ⚙️      DevOps Engineer   Automates infrastructure so your team ships faster and sleeps better.  
  🔒      Security Engineer   Models threats, reviews code, and designs security architecture that actually holds.  
  📊      Data Engineer   Thinks in tables and queries. Normalizes first, denormalizes when the numbers demand it.  
  📝      Technical Writer   Turns complexity into clarity. If the docs are wrong, the product is wrong.  
  🤖      AI / ML Engineer   Builds intelligent systems that learn, reason, and adapt.  
  🎨      UI/UX Designer   Pixel-aware and user-obsessed. If it looks off by one, it is off by one.

title: Built-in Base Roles

Emoji   ID   Title   Vibe  
 ------- ---- ------- ------ 
  📣      Marketing Strategist   Drives growth through content and channels — every post has a purpose.  
  💼      Sales Strategist   Closes deals with strategic precision — understand the buyer before pitching the solution.  
  📋      Product Manager   Shapes what gets built and why — every feature earns its place.  
  📅      Project Manager   Keeps the train on the tracks — scope, schedule, and sanity.  
  🎧      Support Specialist   First line of defense for users — solve fast, document everything.

title: Built-in Base Roles
heading: Customization

Override expertise, style, voice, or boundaries via  options.
Add extra ownership or approach items with /.
Base roles are starting points — the coordinator refines them for your project context during casting.

title: Capability Routing
heading: Capability Routing
text:
# Capability Routing

⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.

Try this to declare machine capabilities:


Try this to route an issue to a capable machine:


Squad discovers what each machine can do and routes issues only to machines that meet the requirements. No manual assignment needed for hardware- or OS-specific work.

title: Capability Routing
heading: What Are Capabilities?
text:
A capability is a label that describes what a machine can do — hardware, OS, or environment attributes that not every runner has. You declare capabilities in  at the project root or home directory; Squad reads them when routing issues.

Examples: , , , , , .

title: Capability Routing
heading: Declaring Capabilities
text:
Add a  array to  at the project root or home directory on each machine:



Squad reads this file at startup. The declared capabilities are available to the routing system immediately.

title: Capability Routing
heading: The `needs:*` Label Pattern
text:
Apply a  label to any GitHub issue to require a specific capability:

  Label   Meaning  
 ------- --------- 
     Must run on a machine with GPU  
     Must run on Windows  
     Must run on macOS  
     Must run on ARM64 architecture  
     Must run where Docker is available  

You can combine multiple  labels — all must match.

title: Capability Routing
heading: How Routing Works
text:
When Ralph picks up an issue:

It reads all  labels on the issue.
It compares them against the current machine's declared capabilities.
If the machine satisfies all requirements, it proceeds. If not, it skips the issue and leaves it for a capable machine to claim.

No central scheduler needed. Each machine self-selects based on what it can do.

title: Capability Routing
heading: See Also
text:
Work Routing — pattern-based and skill-aware routing
Ralph — Work Monitor — how Ralph polls and claims issues

title: Ceremonies
heading: Ceremonies
text:
# Ceremonies
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to schedule team ceremonies:
Try this to trigger a pre-work design review:
Ceremonies are structured team meetings that happen at key moments — before multi-agent work begins, or after something goes wrong. Squad runs them automatically when needed.

title: Ceremonies
heading: Design Review
text:
Triggers automatically before multi-agent tasks involving 2+ agents modifying shared systems.
The Lead facilitates. Each relevant agent is spawned to weigh in on interfaces, risks, and contracts before work begins.

title: Ceremonies
heading: Retrospective
text:
Triggers automatically after build failures, test failures, or reviewer rejections.
The Lead facilitates a focused root-cause analysis.

text: Run any ceremony on demand:

Action   Prompt  
 -------- --------

title: Ceremonies
heading: Tips
text:
Design reviews prevent agents from building conflicting implementations. Let them run on multi-agent tasks.
Retros produce decisions that get written to  — they improve future work, not just diagnose the current failure.
Ceremony config lives in . You can edit it directly if you prefer.
Ceremonies work well with human team members — add a human as a participant for approval gates.

title: Ceremonies

title: Consult Mode
heading: Consult Mode
text:
# Consult Mode
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Consult mode lets you bring your personal squad to projects you don't own — OSS contributions, client work, temporary collaborations — without leaving any trace. Your team consults, does the work, learns things, and returns home with only the generic learnings you approve.

title: Consult Mode
heading: The Problem
text:
You have a personal squad at your global path (e.g.,  on Linux) with agents, skills, and decisions refined over time. When you contribute to someone else's project, you face a dilemma:
Pollute the project? Running  creates a  folder they didn't ask for
Pollute your squad? Project-specific knowledge bleeds into your global squad
Work without your team? Lose the productivity benefits you've built up

title: Consult Mode
heading: The Solution
text:
Your team consults on a project. They bring their expertise, do the work, and learn things. When done, they extract what's reusable and return home. The project never knows Squad was there.
  Aspect   Normal Mode   Consult Mode  
 -------- ------------- -------------- 
  Squad location    in project   Copy of personal squad into project   
  Git visibility   Committed or    Invisible via

title: Context Hygiene: Nap, Reskill, and Compact
heading: Context Hygiene: Nap, Reskill, and Compact
text:
# Context Hygiene: Nap, Reskill, and Compact

⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.


Try this to compact your team's memory:


Try this to refresh agent skills:


Try this to do both and report results:


Over multiple sessions, Squad's  files grow — agent histories, decisions, skill files. Context hygiene commands let you actively manage that growth so agents stay fast and focused.

title: Context Hygiene: Nap, Reskill, and Compact
heading: Nap
text:
What it does: Summarizes accumulated work into smaller, more efficient memory files. This is the same as running  in the CLI or  from the command line.

When you tell the team to "take a nap," each agent:

Reviews its  and other state files
Compresses older entries into concise summaries
Archives verbose detail while preserving key decisions and learnings
Reports how much context was reclaimed

title: Context Hygiene: Nap, Reskill, and Compact
heading: Nap ≠ Shutting Down
text:
This is the most common misconception:

  Action   What happens to  files  
 -------- ------------------------------- 
  Shutting down Squad (closing the CLI, killing the process)   Files stay exactly as they are. Nothing is summarized or compacted.  
  Nap ( or )   Files are actively summarized and compacted. Older entries are archived, working context gets leaner.  

Shutting down Squad every night does not perform context hygiene. You must explicitly tell the team to take a nap.

title: Context Hygiene: Nap, Reskill, and Compact
heading: CLI equivalents
text: In the interactive shell, use  for the same effect.

title: Context Hygiene: Nap, Reskill, and Compact
heading: Reskill
text:
What it does: Tells agents to re-examine their skills, validate them against the current codebase, and potentially discover new patterns.

When you tell the team to "reskill," agents:

Review existing skill files in 
Validate that documented patterns still apply
Look for new reusable patterns from recent work
Update skill confidence levels based on current evidence

title: Context Hygiene: Nap, Reskill, and Compact
heading: Availability
text: Note: As of now, reskill requires running Squad from source (via symlink). It is not yet available through . This will change in a future release.

title: Context Hygiene: Nap, Reskill, and Compact
heading: Combined Commands
text:
You can trigger nap and reskill together in a single prompt:



This runs both behaviors and gives you a report on how much context was reduced — useful for understanding how lean your team's working memory is before the next session.

title: Context Hygiene: Nap, Reskill, and Compact
heading: When to Use These
text:
Situation   Command  
 ----------- --------- 
  After several work sessions, agents feel slow or unfocused     
  Codebase has changed significantly and skills may be stale     
  Before a major new phase of work   Combine both  
  End of sprint / milestone

title: Context Hygiene: Nap, Reskill, and Compact
heading: Tips
text:
Nap regularly. A few sessions of heavy work can bloat history files. Napping keeps context budgets in check.
Don't rely on shutdown. Closing the CLI preserves files as-is — it does not compact anything.
Reskill after refactors. If you've restructured the codebase, agent skills may reference outdated patterns.
Check the dry run first. Use  to preview cleanup actions before committing to them.

title: Context Hygiene: Nap, Reskill, and Compact
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Compacts and summarizes all agent memory files, reclaiming context space.



Agents re-examine and validate their skills against the current codebase.



Combines both behaviors and reports back on total context reduction.



Previews what a nap would clean up without making any changes.

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
text:
# Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Add the GitHub Copilot coding agent to your Squad as an autonomous team member. It picks up issues, creates branches, and opens PRs — all without a Copilot chat session.

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: Prerequisites
text:
Before enabling @copilot on your Squad, ensure:
Copilot coding agent is enabled on the repository (Settings → Copilot → Coding agent)
 exists in  (defines the agent's environment)
GitHub Actions are enabled on the repository

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: Quick Start
text: Why can't I use ? Bot accounts cannot be assigned via the GitHub CLI the same way as human users. Use label-based assignment instead. See FAQ: Why doesn't gh issue edit --add-assignee "@copilot" work? for details.

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: In conversation (recommended)
text:
Say something like:
"I want to add copilot to the squad"
"hire copilot to the squad"
"add team member copilot"
The coordinator will add @copilot to the roster and ask about auto-assign.
Note: If your project has features named "copilot" (e.g., a Copilot extension), the coordinator may misinterpret the phrase as project work. Use the CLI fallback in that case.

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: During team setup (new projects)
text: Squad asks if you want to include the coding agent during . Say yes and it's added to the roster with a default capability profile.

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: COPILOT_ASSIGN_TOKEN (required for auto-assign)
text: The  workflow needs a classic Personal Access Token to assign  to issues. The default  cannot do this.

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: Create the token
text:
Go to https://github.com/settings/tokens/new
Note: 
Expiration: 90 days (or your preference)
Scopes: check  (full control of private repositories)
Click Generate token

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: Add as repo secret
text: Why a classic PAT? Fine-grained PATs return  for this endpoint. The REST API for assigning  requires a classic PAT with  scope. The  silently ignores the assignment.

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: How @copilot Differs from Other Members
text:
AI Agent   Human Member   @copilot  
 --- ---------- ------------- ---------- 
  Badge   ✅ Active   👤 Human   🤖 Coding Agent  
  Name   Cast from universe   Real name   Always "@copilot"  
  Charter   ✅   ❌   ❌ (uses )  
  Works in session   ✅   ❌   ❌ (asynchronous via issue assignment)  
  Spawned by coordinator   ✅   ❌   ❌  
  Creates PRs   Via session commands   Outside Squad   Autonomously

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: Capability Profile
text:
The capability profile in  defines what @copilot should and shouldn't handle:
  Tier   Meaning   Examples  
 ------ --------- ---------- 
  🟢 Good fit   Route automatically   Bug fixes, test coverage, lint fixes, dependency updates, small features, docs  
  🟡 Needs review   Route to @copilot but flag for PR review   Medium features with specs, refactoring with tests, API additions  
  🔴 Not suitable   Route to a squad member instead   Architecture, multi-system design, security-critical, ambiguous requirements  
The profile is editable. The Lead can suggest updates based on experience:

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: Auto-Assign Flow
text:
When the  label is added to an issue:
Step 1 — Workflow posts a routing comment (uses )
Step 2 — Workflow assigns  to the issue (uses )
Step 3 — Coding agent picks up the issue, creates a  branch, and opens a draft PR
The workflow automatically detects the repo's default branch (, , etc.).

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: Lead Triage
text:
The Lead evaluates every issue against @copilot's capability profile during triage:
Good fit? → Routes to @copilot with reasoning
Needs review? → Routes to @copilot, flags for squad member PR review
Not suitable? → Routes to the right squad member, explains why not @copilot
The Lead can also suggest reassignment in either direction:

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: Labels
text:
When @copilot is on the team, the  workflow creates:
  Label   Color   Purpose  
 ------- ------- --------- 
     🟢 Green   Assigned to @copilot for autonomous work  
This works alongside the existing  (triage) and  labels.

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: copilot-instructions.md
text:
The  file gives the coding agent context about your Squad when it works autonomously. It tells @copilot to:
Read  for roster and capability profile
Read  for work routing rules
Check its capability profile before starting (and request reassignment if the issue doesn't match)
Follow the  branch naming convention
Write decisions to the inbox for the Scribe to merge
This file is upgraded automatically when you run  and  is on your team — even if Squad is already up to date. If @copilot is not enabled, the file is left untouched.

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: Tips
text:
Start conservative with the capability profile and expand as you see what @copilot handles well.
Use auto-assign for repos where you want fully autonomous issue processing.
The coding agent works great alongside issue-driven development — label issues  and the Lead + @copilot handle the rest.
@copilot's PRs go through normal review — treat them like any team member's work.

title: Copilot Coding Agent (@copilot)
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Adds @copilot to the roster and configures automatic issue assignment.
Shows which task types are marked as good fit, needs review, or not suitable for @copilot.
Routes an issue away from @copilot to a different squad member.
Updates the capability profile to mark refactoring tasks as automatically routable to @copilot.
Spawns the appropriate squad member to review @copilot's pull request.
