title: Directives
heading: Directives
text:
# Directives
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to establish team coding standards:
Try this to set formatting rules:
Try this to enforce workflow policies:
Directives are team rules that persist across sessions. When you say "always" or "never", Squad captures it and writes it to the team's permanent memory. Every agent reads these before working.

title: Directives
heading: How Directives Work
text: A directive is a preference, rule, or constraint the team remembers across sessions. When you say "always do X" or "never do Y", Squad captures it as a directive, writes it to the decisions inbox, and the Scribe merges it into  — the team's permanent memory.

title: Directives
heading: How Directives Work
text:
Signal Word Detection — The coordinator listens for: "always", "never", "from now on", "remember to", "don't", "make sure to".
Capture — Directive is written to .
Scribe Merge — Scribe consolidates inbox files into  during the next coordination cycle.
Agent Awareness — All agents read  before starting work. Directives shape behavior.

title: Directives
heading: Signal Words
text:
Phrase   Example  
 -------- --------- 
  "always"   "Always use TypeScript strict mode"  
  "never"   "Never commit directly to main"  
  "from now on"   "From now on, prefix all commits with the issue number"  
  "remember to"   "Remember to run tests before pushing"  
  "don't"   "Don't use var — only let and const"  
  "make sure to"   "Make sure to document all public APIs"

title: Directives
heading: Directive Scope
text:
Directives can apply to:
Coding style — Formatting, naming conventions, language features
Tool preferences — Linters, formatters, test runners
Workflow rules — Branch naming, commit messages, PR templates
Scope constraints — "Don't touch legacy/ directory", "Only work on v2 features"
Review requirements — "Always have Lead review security changes"

title: Directives
heading: Examples
text:
"Always use single quotes for strings in TypeScript"
Captured:
"Never deploy on Fridays"
Captured:
"From now on, all API endpoints need integration tests"
Captured:

title: Directives
heading: Decisions Inbox
text:
New directives land in  as individual files:
The Scribe periodically consolidates these into :
`markdown
# Team Decisions

title: Directives
heading: Single Quotes for Strings
text: Use single quotes for string literals in TypeScript. Avoid double quotes unless escaping is required.

title: Directives
heading: API Integration Test Coverage
text: Every new API endpoint requires at least one integration test covering the happy path and one error case.

title: Directives
heading: No Friday Deploys
text:
Do not trigger production deploys on Fridays. Schedule for Monday-Thursday only.
`

title: Directives
heading: Directive Conflicts
text:
When a new directive contradicts an existing one:
Scribe detects conflict — Checks for semantic overlap during merge.
User prompt — "New directive conflicts with existing rule: {old rule}. Replace, merge, or skip?"
Resolution — Scribe updates  based on your choice.

title: Directives
heading: Viewing Directives
text:
"Show me the team directives"
Coordinator displays  content.
"What's our rule on testing?"
Coordinator searches  for testing-related directives.

title: Directives
heading: Removing Directives
text:
"Remove the no-Friday-deploy rule"
Scribe edits  and removes that section.
Or edit  directly.

title: Directives
heading: Agent Directive Compliance
text:
Agents are not hard-constrained by directives — they're context-aware guidelines. If an agent violates a directive:
Reviewer rejection — Lead or Tester flags it during review.
User feedback — You say "this violates our style rule" and the agent revises.
Directives shape behavior but don't replace code review or linting.

title: Directives
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Creates a coding style directive. All agents will format code accordingly.
Establishes a type safety directive. Agents will avoid  and use proper types.
Sets a workflow directive. Agents will format commits as , , , etc.
Creates a release process directive. Agents will add changelog entries when appropriate.
Establishes a review requirement. Coordinator will route security PRs to Lead for approval.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: Distributed Mesh
text:
# Distributed Mesh
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to coordinate squads across machines:
Try this to sync remote squad state:
The distributed mesh lets squads on different machines coordinate through git and HTTP. Local squads read remote squad state after syncing it locally.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: What Is the Distributed Mesh?
text:
One sentence:
"The filesystem is the mesh, and git is how the mesh crosses machine boundaries."
Squad agents always read local files. When squads live on different machines, you need to materialize remote state locally before agents can see it. The distributed mesh does this through simple sync scripts — no servers, no federation protocols, no real-time messaging.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: Three Zones
text:
Zone   Description   Transport   Complexity  
 ------ ------------- ----------- ------------ 
  1 — Local   Same host/filesystem   Direct file read   Zero  
  2 — Remote-Trusted   Different host, same org    from shared repo   Zero new (git exists)  
  3 — Remote-Opaque   Different org, no shared auth    / HTTP fetch   15 lines of shell  
Zone 1 (Local):  works because the file is on your disk.
Zone 2 (Remote-Trusted): Squads push their state to a shared git repo. You pull from that repo to materialize their state locally.
Zone 3 (Remote-Opaque): A remote organization publishes their squad's  at an HTTPS URL. You curl it to materialize locally.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: Agent Lifecycle with Sync
text: Two new steps (SYNC, PUBLISH). Both are transport only — they move files, not change them.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: What Doesn't Change
text:
Agents read local files
Write partitioning (each squad owns its directory)
Pull-based coordination
Eventual consistency
LLMs as the relevance engine

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: What Changes
text: Remote files need to arrive locally before agents can read them.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: The `mesh.json` File
text: One JSON file lists where to find each squad:

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: Sync Scripts
text:
Bash (requires  and ):
PowerShell (requires  only):
Both scripts read , pull from remote-trusted repos, curl from remote-opaque URLs, and materialize everything into .

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: Prerequisites
text:
Git (with SSH or HTTPS auth configured)
A shell (bash/zsh) or PowerShell
 (github.com/jqlang/jq) for the bash sync script (PowerShell script has no external dependencies)

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: 1. Create the Mesh State Repo
text: The mesh state repo is a shared git repository where squads publish their current state. Nothing more — no code, no automation, no agents.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: 2. Directory Structure
text: One directory per squad, each with a :

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: 3. Register Your Squad
text: Create your directory, write initial state, push:

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: 4. Configure `mesh.json`
text: Point at the shared repo:

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: 5. Run Your First Sync
text: Does the mesh state repo need its own Squad? No. It's a shared data directory — a dumb pipe. No agents, no  folder, no automation. Each squad pushes its own state via write partitioning. The repo is just a git-based rendezvous point. If you later want a "mesh observer" that monitors all squads, THAT would be its own Squad project — but it's not required and shouldn't be the state repo itself.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: Cross-Org Setup (Zone 3)
text: Remote org publishes  at a URL. Add an HTTP entry to :

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: SubSquads (Streams)
text:
SubSquads partition work within a single repo using GitHub labels (e.g., , ). Each SubSquad runs in its own Codespace but shares the same git repository.
Distributed mesh coordinates across repos and machines — different organizations, different git repos, potentially no shared authentication.
SubSquads solve "one repo, many teams." Distributed mesh solves "many repos, many machines, crossing org boundaries."
See SubSquads for within-repo partitioning.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: Export & Import
text:
Export/import is a snapshot-based knowledge transfer. You export a trained squad from one repo and import it into another. It's a one-time copy.
Distributed mesh is continuous coordination. Remote squads keep working; you sync their latest state every time your agents wake up.
Use export/import when you want to clone a team. Use distributed mesh when you want live coordination.
See Multiple Squads scenario for when to use each approach.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: Upstream inheritance
text: The upstream module and the distributed mesh serve different coordination needs. They're complementary, not competing.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: Upstream: top-down inheritance
text: The  module (configured in ) is for hierarchical inheritance. An organization-level or team-level squad pushes skills, decisions, wisdom, casting policy, and routing rules down to project squads. The consuming squad treats upstream content as read-only — it inherits conventions but doesn't write back.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: Mesh: peer coordination
text: The distributed mesh (configured in ) is for peer-to-peer coordination. Squads on equal footing share their current state with each other. Each squad publishes its own state (SUMMARY.md, billboards) and reads everyone else's. It's read-write for each squad's own directory.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: Use them together
text:
A squad can have both an upstream (inheriting org conventions) and mesh peers (coordinating with sibling squads). For example:
Your project squad inherits security policies and routing rules from the org-level squad via 
The same squad coordinates with other project squads (auth, ci, data) via

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: Comparison
text:
Upstream   Mesh  
 --- --- --- 
  Direction   Top-down (parent → child)   Peer-to-peer (squad ↔ squad)  
  Write model   Read-only for consumer   Read-write (own directory)  
  What flows   Skills, decisions, wisdom, casting, routing   Current state (SUMMARY.md, billboards)  
  Config file        
  Transport   Local path / git clone / export JSON   Local path / git pull / HTTP curl  
  Use case   Org policies flowing into team projects   Sibling squads keeping each other informed

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: What neither does
text: Neither upstream nor mesh is about agent-to-agent communication within a single squad. That's the drop-box pattern — agents write to , read from , and coordinate asynchronously within one  directory.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: Skill scope
text:
When you ask an agent to set up a distributed mesh, the skill produces three things:
 config file — defines squads, zones, and sync sources
A decision entry — records why you configured the mesh this way
Sync scripts — copies pre-built  and  from the skill's bundled resources
The skill does not generate:
❌ Code (validators, helpers, utilities)
❌ Tests (the sync scripts are pre-tested templates)
❌ Custom sync scripts (bundled scripts are copied, not regenerated)
Why this matters: Deterministic skills give you consistent results. The sync scripts are bundled with the distributed-mesh skill. Agents shouldn't waste time generating validators or rewriting sync logic from scratch — they should copy the bundled scripts and configure your .
If you need to customize the sync behavior, edit the copied scripts in your project root. The mesh skill's job ends at configuration.

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: What We're NOT Building
text:
❌ Federation protocol (git push/pull IS federation)
❌ Discovery service (mesh.json IS discovery)
❌ Auth system (git auth IS the auth system)
❌ A2A endpoints (no running servers)
❌ Schema versioning (markdown; LLM reads it)
❌ Real-time sync (agents are async; eventual consistency is correct)
❌ Message queues (agents aren't persistent; nobody's listening)
❌ CRDTs/conflict resolution (write partitioning; no conflicts possible)

title: Distributed Mesh
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Creates a  entry for a remote-trusted squad and runs the first sync.
Runs the sync script to materialize the latest state from all configured remote squads.
Adds a remote-opaque Zone 3 entry to  for cross-org coordination.
Lists all squads in  and their zones.

title: Enterprise Platforms
heading: Enterprise Platforms
text:
# Enterprise Platforms
Squad supports Azure DevOps and Microsoft Planner in addition to GitHub. When your git remote points to Azure DevOps, Squad automatically detects the platform and adapts its commands. For work-item tracking, Squad also supports a hybrid model where code lives in one platform and tasks live in Microsoft Planner.

title: Enterprise Platforms
heading: Prerequisites
text:
Azure CLI — Install from https://aka.ms/install-az-cli
Azure DevOps extension — 
Login — 
Set defaults — 
Verify setup:

title: Enterprise Platforms
heading: How It Works
text:
Squad auto-detects the platform from your git remote URL:
  Remote URL pattern   Detected platform  
 --- --- 
     GitHub  
     Azure DevOps  
     Azure DevOps  
     Azure DevOps

title: Enterprise Platforms
heading: Work Items vs Issues
text:
GitHub   Azure DevOps  
 --- --- 
  Issues   Work Items  
  Labels (e.g., )   Tags (e.g., )  
     WIQL query via

title: Enterprise Platforms
heading: Pull Requests
text:
GitHub   Azure DevOps  
 --- --- 
       
       
       
  Review: Approved / Changes Requested   Vote: 10 (approved) / -10 (rejected)

title: Enterprise Platforms
heading: Branch Operations
text: Branch operations use the same  commands on both platforms. Squad creates branches with the naming convention .

title: Enterprise Platforms
heading: Ralph on Azure DevOps
text:
Ralph works identically on ADO — he scans for untriaged work items using WIQL queries instead of GitHub label filters:
Tag assignment uses the same  convention, stored as ADO work item tags separated by .

title: Enterprise Platforms
heading: Configuration
text: Squad auto-detects ADO from the git remote URL. For basic use, no extra configuration is needed.

title: Enterprise Platforms
heading: Work Item Configuration
text:
When your ADO environment has custom work item types, area paths, iterations, or when work items live in a different project or org than the git repo, configure the  section in :
  Field   Default   Description  
 ------- --------- ------------- 
     (from git remote)   ADO org for work items — set when work items are in a different org than the repo  
     (from git remote)   ADO project for work items — set when work items are in a different project  
        Default type for new work items. Some orgs use , , or custom types  
     (project default)   Area path for new work items — controls which team's backlog they appear in  
     (project default)   Iteration/sprint path — controls which sprint board work items appear on  
All fields are optional. Omitted fields use the defaults shown above.

title: Enterprise Platforms
heading: Authentication
text:
Squad uses the Azure CLI for ADO authentication — no Personal Access Tokens (PATs) needed. Run  once, and Squad agents use your authenticated session for all operations.
For GitHub repositories, Squad uses the  CLI for authentication. When working across multiple GitHub accounts (e.g., personal GitHub and Enterprise Managed Users), use  to toggle between accounts. See Cross-organization authentication for detailed multi-account setup.
Alternatively, if the Azure DevOps MCP server is configured in your environment, Squad will use it automatically for richer API access. Add it to :
Squad prefers MCP tools when available, falling back to  CLI when not.
To explicitly check which platform Squad detects:

title: Enterprise Platforms
heading: Microsoft Planner Support (Hybrid Model)
text: Squad supports a hybrid model where your repository lives in GitHub or Azure DevOps, but work items are tracked in Microsoft Planner. This is common in enterprise environments where project management uses Planner while engineering uses ADO or GitHub for code.

title: Enterprise Platforms
heading: How It Works
text:
Planner buckets map to squad assignments: , , , etc.
Moving a task between buckets = reassigning to a team member
Task completion = 100% complete or move to "Done" bucket
PRs and branches still go through the repo adapter (GitHub or Azure DevOps)

title: Export & Import
heading: Export & Import
text:
# Export & Import
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to make your team portable:
Try this to bring a trained team to a new repo:
Squad teams are portable. Export your trained agents, casting state, skills, and decisions to a single JSON file. Import them into any repo and they bring all their knowledge with them.

title: Export & Import
heading: Export
text: Creates  in the current directory — a portable snapshot of your entire team: agents, casting state, skills, and decisions.

title: Export & Import
heading: What's included
text:
Data   Included  
 ------ ---------- 
  Agent charters   ✅  
  Agent histories   ✅ (split into portable vs project-specific)  
  Casting state   ✅  
  Skills   ✅ All earned skills export with the team  
  Decisions   ✅  
Skills are portable: When you export a team, all earned skills from  are included in the JSON manifest. After importing, skills are immediately available to all agents — no loss of knowledge.

title: Export & Import
heading: Import
text: Imports the snapshot into the current repo's  directory.

title: Export & Import
heading: Collision detection
text:
If  already exists, Squad warns you and stops. To archive the existing team and replace it:
The  flag moves your current team to an archive before importing. Nothing is deleted.

title: Export & Import
heading: History splitting
text:
During import, agent histories are split into two categories:
Portable knowledge — general learnings, conventions, and patterns that transfer across projects
Project-specific learnings — context-tagged entries tied to the original repo
Imported agents bring their skills and general knowledge without assuming your project works the same way.

title: Export & Import
heading: Use Cases
text:
Scenario   Command  
 ---------- --------- 
  Back up before a major refactor     
  Share a trained team with a colleague   Export, send the JSON, they import — skills included  
  Move a team to a different repo   Export from old repo, import into new repo — skills travel with agents  
  Reset and start fresh   Export as backup, delete , re-init

title: Export & Import
heading: Tips
text:
Export before running  if you want a rollback point.
The export file is JSON — you can inspect it to see exactly what your team knows.
Imported agents retain their names and universe. They won't be renamed.
Commit your  directory after importing so the team is available to everyone who clones the repo.
Skills are fully portable — all earned skills export and import with perfect fidelity. No manual copying needed.

title: Export & Import
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Creates a  snapshot of the entire team in the current directory.
Imports a team snapshot into the current project's  directory.
Shows a summary of what data was captured in the most recent export file.
Creates a lightweight export with agent charters and skills but minimal history.
Overwrites the existing  directory after archiving it as a backup.

heading: Single issue
text:
The coordinator routes the issue to the best-fit agent. That agent:
Creates a branch (e.g., )
Implements the work
Opens a PR linked to the issue

title: GitHub Issues Mode
heading: Multiple issues
text: Agents work in parallel — each issue gets its own branch and PR.

title: GitHub Issues Mode
heading: Handle PR Review Feedback
text: The agent who opened the PR reads the review comments and addresses them. Commits are pushed to the existing branch.

title: GitHub Issues Mode
heading: Merge Completed Work
text: Squad squash-merges the PR, deletes the branch, and closes the linked issue.

title: GitHub Issues Mode
heading: Check Remaining Work
text: Squad refreshes the backlog and shows remaining open issues.

title: GitHub Issues Mode
heading: Workflow Summary
text:
You say   What happens  
 --------- ------------- 
     Stores issue source  
     Lists open issues  
     Agent branches, implements, opens PR  
     Parallel agent work on both issues  
     Agent addresses review comments  
     Squash merge, delete branch, close issue  
     Refreshes and shows remaining issues

title: GitHub Issues Mode
heading: Tips
text:
You don't need to assign issues to specific agents — Squad routes based on domain expertise.
If  isn't authenticated, Squad will tell you. Run  first.
For detailed GitHub workflow, see GitHub Workflow.

title: Human Team Members
heading: Human Team Members
text:
# Human Team Members
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to add a human specialist:
Try this to add a human reviewer:
Not every team member needs to be AI. Add real people to the roster for decisions that require a human — design sign-off, security review, product approval.

title: Human Team Members
heading: Adding a Human
text: Sarah appears in the team roster with a 👤 Human badge, distinct from AI agents.

title: Human Team Members
heading: How Humans Differ from AI Agents
text:
AI Agent   Human Member  
 --- ---------- ------------- 
  Badge   Role-specific emoji   👤 Human  
  Charter   ✅   ❌  
  History   ✅   ❌  
  Spawned as sub-agent   ✅   ❌  
  Can review work   ✅   ✅  
Human team members have no charter, no history file, and are never spawned as sub-agents. They exist on the roster as routing targets.

title: Human Team Members
heading: What Happens When Work Routes to a Human
text:
When the coordinator determines that a task should go to a human team member:
Squad pauses and tells you that a human needs to act
You relay the task to the person outside of Squad
When they respond, you tell Squad what happened
If the human hasn't responded after a while, Squad sends stale reminders prompting you to follow up.

title: Human Team Members
heading: Humans as Reviewers
text:
Human team members can serve as reviewers in the reviewer protocol. This is useful when you want a real person to sign off before work is considered done.
When work requires security review, Squad routes it to Jordan and waits.

title: Human Team Members
heading: Removing a Human
text:
Same as removing any team member — they move to alumni:
Their entry moves to . They can be re-added later.

title: Human Team Members
heading: When to add a human member
text:
Not every collaborator needs a roster entry. Use this table to decide:
  Scenario   Add to roster?   Why  
 ---------- --------------- ----- 
  Approves architecture decisions before implementation   ✅ Yes   Decision gate — agents route and wait  
  Reviews all docs PRs as a standing reviewer   ✅ Yes   Recurring review gate  
  Makes the final ship/no-ship call   ✅ Yes   Approval gate  
  Occasionally reviews PRs when tagged   ❌ No   Use @mention on the PR instead  
  Files issues and contributes code   ❌ No   Normal GitHub collaboration  
Litmus test: If you want agents to stop and wait for someone's input before proceeding, add them. If they review asynchronously through normal GitHub flows, a roster entry adds no value.
You don't need to add yourself. Squad reads  every session, so the team always knows who's driving. Adding yourself to the roster is optional — it formalizes routing and review tracking but isn't required for day-to-day interaction.

title: Human Team Members
heading: Tips
text:
Use human members for approval gates — design review, compliance, final sign-off.
Human members work well alongside ceremonies — add a human as a required participant in a design review ceremony.
You're the relay. Squad can't message humans directly — it tells you, and you coordinate.

title: Human Team Members
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Adds a human team member with a specific review responsibility.
Assigns a task to a human team member for external handling.
Unblocks work that was waiting on human input.
Shows all team members including both AI agents and human members.
Moves a human team member to the alumni list.

# Issue Templates for Squad

⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.


Try this after setting up templates:


Then watch Ralph auto-triage based on labels.

When GitHub Issues are your work queue, creating tasks should be frictionless. Issue templates pre-fill labels, structure task descriptions, and work beautifully on mobile — making it possible to add tasks in 10 seconds from anywhere.

title: Issue Templates for Squad
heading: Why Issue Templates Matter for Squad
text:
GitHub provides Issue Templates — a platform feature that pre-fills labels, fields, and structure when creating new issues. This guide shows how to configure templates that work smoothly with Squad's label-based routing.

Squad operates best when work is captured as GitHub Issues. But creating an issue from scratch takes time: you need to remember the right labels, format the description consistently, and ensure the structure matches what agents expect.

Issue templates solve this:

Pre-filled labels —  label applied automatically
Structured format — Task description, acceptance criteria, priority fields
Mobile-friendly — Works in the GitHub mobile app
Fast task creation — Add work while walking the dog, waiting for coffee, or during a meeting

With templates, creating a Squad task takes 10 seconds instead of 2 minutes.

text: Create  in your repository:

heading: What This Template Does
text:
Applies  label — Ralph sees it in the untriaged queue
Structured sections — Description, acceptance criteria, priority
Markdown support — Use checklists, code blocks, links
Works on mobile — GitHub app renders forms beautifully

heading: Custom Labels for Routing
text:
Ralph uses  to route work to agents. Add  labels to your template for pre-triaging:



When Ralph scans the board, this issue is already labeled  — no triage needed, work goes straight to PAO.

title: Issue Templates for Squad
heading: Mobile Workflow
text:
GitHub Issues + templates work from anywhere:

On your phone:
Open GitHub app
Navigate to repository
Tap Issues → New Issue
Select template
Fill form (voice-to-text works!)
Tap Submit new issue

10 seconds later:
Issue created with  label
Ralph sees it in the next scan
Agent picks it up autonomously

This workflow enables "capture anywhere, process later" — add tasks while commuting, exercising, or in meetings without context-switching to a laptop.

title: Issue Templates for Squad
heading: Template Configuration
text:
GitHub supports multiple templates. Create a config file to customize the issue creation experience:

:



This disables blank issues (forcing template use) and provides helpful links when users click "New Issue."

title: Issue Templates for Squad
heading: Template Best Practices
text:
Keep templates short — Long forms reduce completion rates
Make most fields optional — Only require what's absolutely necessary
Use placeholders — Show examples of good descriptions
Pre-fill smart defaults — Priority: Medium, Type: Task
Test on mobile — Ensure forms render well in the GitHub app
Use dropdown for enums — Priority, Type, Severity (reduces typos)
Add markdown help — Link to GitHub markdown guide in template

title: Issue Templates for Squad
heading: Integration with Ralph
text:
Ralph's heartbeat workflow () scans for untriaged issues:

Issue created with  label (from template)
Heartbeat workflow runs (every 30 min or on issue create)
Ralph reads  to determine agent
Ralph adds  label
Next heartbeat run (or in-session Ralph) assigns agent

If your template pre-fills , Ralph skips triage and goes straight to assignment.

Use  or  extension (both work)
Test templates by creating issues yourself before announcing to the team
Mobile workflow requires GitHub app (iOS or Android) — works on tablets too

# KEDA Autoscaling

⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.

Try this to understand your scaling needs:


KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) is an open-source component that scales Kubernetes workloads based on external event sources. Squad ships an external scaler template that scales agent pods up and down based on the depth of your GitHub issue queue.

title: KEDA Autoscaling
heading: When to Use This
text:
Use KEDA autoscaling when:

Squad agents run as Kubernetes pods (not local machines)
Issue volume is unpredictable — bursts of work should spawn more agents automatically
You want zero-agent idle cost when there is no work

title: Label Taxonomy
heading: Label Taxonomy
text:
# Label Taxonomy
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to apply workflow labels:
Try this to filter by priority:
Try this to route work to a specific agent:
Squad uses structured, namespaced labels as the state machine. Labels drive workflow automation — not just tags. Five namespaces control lifecycle, priority, ownership, and release targeting.

title: Label Taxonomy
heading: The Five Namespaces
text:
Namespace   Purpose   Values   Mutual Exclusivity  
 ----------- --------- -------- ------------------- 
     Verdict — yes/no/needs-research   , ,    ✅ One per issue  
     Release target   , ,    ✅ One per issue  
     Issue category   , , , , ,    ✅ One per issue  
     Urgency level   , ,    ✅ One per issue  
     Agent assignment   , ,    ❌ Can have multiple (pair work)

title: Label Taxonomy
heading: Mutual Exclusivity Rules
text:
Within , , , and  namespaces, only one label is allowed. Applying a second label in the same namespace auto-removes the first.
Example:
Issue has 
You apply 
Result:  removed,  applied
The  namespace allows multiple labels for collaborative work:
 +  = pair programming or handoff

title: Label Taxonomy
heading: Workflow Automation
text: Labels drive four automation layers:

title: Label Taxonomy
heading: 1. Enforcement (Mutual Exclusivity)
text: GitHub Actions workflow  watches for label changes. If multiple labels from the same namespace are applied, it removes all but the most recent.

title: Label Taxonomy
heading: 2. Sync (Cross-Namespace Consistency)
text:
Some label changes trigger cascading updates:
 applied → auto-adds , removes other release targets
 applied → ensures  is set (p0 implies approved)

title: Label Taxonomy
heading: 3. Triage (Auto-Assignment)
text:
Ralph (work monitor) uses labels to route work:
 → Fenster picks it up
No  +  → Routes to Tester or Lead based on routing.md
 → Routes to Lead for investigation

title: Label Taxonomy
heading: 4. Heartbeat (Periodic Check)
text:
The  workflow runs every 30 minutes and:
Finds issues with  label but no  → auto-triages
Finds  +  but no assignee → spawns agent
Finds stale  (>7 days) → escalates to Lead

heading: Adding Labels
text:
Labels are created automatically during  or . To add custom labels:
Or via the GitHub UI: Issues → Labels → New label

heading: Label Colors
text:
Squad uses a consistent color scheme:
  Namespace   Color   Hex  
 ----------- ------- ----- 
     Green (yes), Red (no), Yellow (research)   , ,   
     Blue     
     Purple     
     Orange (p0), Yellow (p1), Gray (p2)   , ,   
     Teal

title: MCP Setup Guide for Squad
heading: How Agents Discover and Use MCP Tools
text:
Agents don't need special setup to discover tools. Here's the flow:
At spawn time, the agent receives the MCP configuration
Agent lists available tools — it reads what's configured and knows what's available
Agent uses tools naturally — when working, if a tool matches the task (e.g., "create a GitHub issue"), agents call it automatically
Tools return results — the agent receives structured data back (e.g., issue ID, status, etc.) and continues working
See also: Skills System — how agents learn reusable patterns for complex MCP workflows.

title: Memory System
heading: Memory System
text:
# Memory System
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to query team decisions:
Try this to establish a new rule:
Try this to check agent knowledge:
Squad remembers everything — decisions, conventions, architecture patterns, and individual agent learnings. Memory grows with every session, making agents smarter over time.

title: Memory System
heading: Memory Layers
text: Squad's memory is layered. Each layer serves a different purpose, and knowledge grows with every session.

title: Memory System
heading: Personal Memory: `history.md`
text:
Each agent has its own history file at . After every session, agents append what they learned — architecture decisions, conventions, file paths, user preferences.
Only that agent reads its own history. This means each team member builds specialized knowledge about their domain.
After a few sessions, agents stop asking questions they've already answered.

title: Memory System
heading: Progressive summarization
text: Histories grow over time. When an agent's  exceeds 12KB, older entries are archived into a summary section. Recent entries stay detailed; older entries are condensed. This keeps the file within a useful context budget without losing accumulated knowledge.

title: Memory System
heading: Shared Memory: `decisions.md`
text:
Team-wide decisions live in . Every agent reads this before working. This is the team's shared brain.
Decisions are captured three ways:

title: Memory System
heading: 1. From agent work
text: When an agent makes a decision during a task, it writes to the inbox:

title: Memory System
heading: 2. From user directives
text:
When you say "always..." or "never...", it's captured as a directive:
These go directly into .

title: Memory System
heading: 3. Scribe merges
text:
The Scribe agent (a silent team member) periodically:
Reads all entries from 
Merges them into the canonical 
Deduplicates overlapping decisions
Propagates updates to affected agents

title: Memory System
heading: Decision archiving
text:
As your project grows,  accumulates hundreds of blocks. Stale sprint artifacts, completed analysis docs, and one-time planning fragments consume context window space without adding value. When this happens, old decisions are archived to  — preserved for reference but no longer loaded into agent context.
Active decisions (ongoing policies, user preferences, current architecture) stay in . Agents always read the lean, current shared brain.

title: Memory System
heading: Skills
text:
Reusable knowledge files at . See Skills System for details.
Skills differ from decisions — decisions are project policies ("use PostgreSQL"), while skills are transferable techniques ("how to set up CI with GitHub Actions").

title: Memory System
heading: How Memory Compounds
text:
Stage   What agents know  
 ------- ----------------- 
  🌱 First session   Project description, tech stack, your name  
  🌿 After a few sessions   Conventions, component patterns, API design, test strategies  
  🌳 Mature project   Full architecture, tech debt map, regression patterns, performance conventions

title: Memory System
heading: Tips
text:
Commit  — anyone who clones the repo gets the team with all their accumulated knowledge.
Directives ("always...", "never...") are the fastest way to shape team behavior. Use them liberally.
If an agent keeps making the same mistake, check  — the relevant convention might be missing.
You can edit  and  files directly. They're plain Markdown.
The first session is always the least capable. Give the team a few sessions to build up context.

title: Memory System
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Queries a specific agent's personal history for relevant context.
Searches the shared decisions.md file for a particular topic.
Reviews session history and recent agent learnings.
Adds a directive to the shared decisions that all agents will follow.
Finds historical decisions related to a specific topic or keyword.

title: Per-Agent Model Selection
heading: Per-Agent Model Selection
text:
# Per-Agent Model Selection
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to set a persistent preference (survives across sessions):
Try this to prioritize quality for the session only:
Try this to optimize costs:
Try this to balance quality and budget:
Try this to go back to automatic selection:
Squad adjusts model selection based on your directive. Agents writing code get quality models (Sonnet/Opus), agents doing docs/logs get cost-optimized models (Haiku). You can override anytime — and persistent overrides survive across sessions.

title: Per-Agent Model Selection
heading: How It Works
text: Squad routes each agent to the right model based on what they're doing — not a one-size-fits-all default. The governing principle: cost first, unless code is being written — but your preferences always take priority.

title: Per-Agent Model Selection
heading: 5-Layer Model Resolution
text:
Model selection uses a layered system. First match wins:
Persistent Config () — You said "always use opus"? It's saved to disk. Every session, every agent, until you change it. Per-agent overrides () take priority over the global .
Session Directive — You said "use opus for this session"? Done. Applies until the session ends.
Charter Preference — The agent's charter specifies a  section with a preferred model.
Task-Aware Auto-Selection — The coordinator checks what the agent is actually doing:
  Task Output   Model   Tier  
 ------------- ------- ------ 
  Writing code (implementation, refactoring, tests, bug fixes)      Standard  
  Writing prompts or agent designs      Standard  
  Non-code work (docs, planning, triage, changelogs)      Fast  
  Visual/design work requiring image analysis      Premium  
Default — If nothing matched, . Cost wins when in doubt.

title: Per-Agent Model Selection
heading: Persistent Model Preferences
text:
Squad stores your model preferences in :
 — applies to ALL agents unless overridden. Set with "always use X".
 — per-agent overrides. Set with "use X for {agent}".
Clear with "switch back to automatic" — removes , returns to auto-selection.

title: Per-Agent Model Selection
heading: Role-to-Model Mapping
text:
Role   Default Model   Why  
 ------ -------------- ----- 
  Core Dev / Backend / Frontend      Writes code — quality first  
  Tester / QA      Writes test code  
  Lead / Architect   auto (per-task)   Mixed: code review vs. planning  
  Prompt Engineer   auto (per-task)   Prompt design is like code  
  DevRel / Writer      Docs — not code  
  Scribe / Logger      Mechanical file ops  
  Git / Release      Changelogs, tags, version bumps  
  Designer / Visual      Vision capability required

title: Per-Agent Model Selection
heading: 18-Model Catalog
text:
Squad supports 18 models across three tiers:
Premium: claude-opus-4.6, claude-opus-4.6-fast, claude-opus-4.5
Standard: claude-sonnet-4.6, gpt-5.4, gpt-5.3-codex, gpt-5.2-codex, claude-sonnet-4, gpt-5.2, gpt-5.1-codex, gpt-5.1, gpt-5, gemini-3-pro-preview
Fast/Cheap: claude-haiku-4.5, gpt-5.1-codex-mini, gpt-4.1, gpt-5-mini

title: Per-Agent Model Selection
heading: Fallback Chains
text:
If a model is unavailable (plan restriction, rate limit, deprecation), Squad silently retries with the next in chain:
Never falls back UP in tier — a fast task won't land on a premium model.

title: Per-Agent Model Selection
heading: User Overrides
text:
Tell the coordinator what you want:
 — one-off premium for current task
 — persistent preference saved to  (survives sessions)
 — persistent per-agent override
 — clears persistent preference

title: Per-Agent Model Selection
heading: Economy Mode
text:
Economy mode automatically falls back to cheaper models when rate limits are approaching or when you want to cap spend. It is opt-in — enable it per session or persistently.
Enable economy mode:
Disable economy mode:
When economy mode is active, Squad remaps models using the :
  Normal Tier   Economy Model  
 ------------- -------------- 
  Standard (Sonnet)     
  Fast (Haiku)     
Fallback chains in economy mode run the same logic as normal fallback chains, but start one tier lower. A code task that would normally use  uses  instead.
Cost tradeoffs: Economy mode trades output quality for lower cost and reduced rate limit pressure. Use it for bulk triage, log analysis, or changelog generation — not for architecture work or complex refactors where quality matters.
Persistent economy mode saves to :
Economy mode is also triggered automatically by the rate limiting system when headroom drops to Amber state — you do not have to enable it manually for rate limit protection.

title: Per-Agent Model Selection
heading: Sample Prompts
text:

Set session-wide preference for the cheapest model tier.
Check which model was actually used for a completed task.
Set a specific model for tasks in a particular domain.
Clear any session-wide overrides and return to task-aware auto-selection.

title: Squad pings you
heading: Squad pings you
text:
# Squad pings you
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to get notified on completion:
Try this to stay in the loop:
Your squad sends you instant messages when they need input, hit an error, or complete work. Works with Teams, Discord, Slack, webhooks — whatever you configure.

title: Squad pings you
heading: How It Works
text: Your squad can send you instant messages when they need your input. Leave your terminal, get pinged on your phone.

title: Squad pings you
heading: How It Works
text:
Squad ships zero notification infrastructure. Instead, it uses skills — reusable knowledge files — to teach agents when and how to ping you. You bring your own notification delivery by configuring an MCP notification server in your Copilot environment.
The flow:
Skill () tells agents when to ping — blocked waiting for input, decision needed, error hit, work complete
Agent calls the skill, which invokes your configured MCP server
Your MCP server (Teams, iMessage, Discord, webhook, etc.) sends the actual message to your device
This means Squad works with any notification service. Pick your favorite messaging platform, configure it once, and your squad has a direct line to you.

title: Squad pings you
heading: What you need to know
text: Squad doesn't ship a Teams MCP server. You bring your own — either a community implementation or one you build yourself. Squad agents discover the configured MCP server at spawn time and call it automatically when they need to notify you.

title: Squad pings you
heading: What Triggers a Notification
text:
Agents ping you when:
  Trigger   Example  
 --------- --------- 
  Blocked on input   "Keaton needs your decision on which API approach to use (Issue #42)"  
  Decision needed   "Verbal hit a design choice and needs your call on error handling strategy"  
  Error hit   "McManus got an authentication error and needs credentials for the staging API"  
  Work complete   "Fenster finished the test suite — 142 tests passing, 3 flaky (check the logs)"  
  Review feedback   "Your PR review on #78 needs a response before Keaton can merge"  
You control which triggers send notifications (see Configuration below).

title: Parallel Execution
heading: Parallel Execution
text:
# Parallel Execution
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to launch concurrent work streams:
Try this to work multiple issues simultaneously:
Try this to control concurrency for cost savings:
Squad launches independent work in parallel by default — multiple agents work simultaneously, no waiting. You control concurrency limits and can force sequential execution when needed.

title: Parallel Execution
heading: How Parallel Execution Works
text: Squad runs agents in parallel whenever possible. The fan-out pattern launches all independent agents simultaneously, waits for results, then proceeds — no sequential bottlenecks unless data dependencies or reviewer gates require them.

title: Parallel Execution
heading: How Parallel Execution Works
text:
When the coordinator receives work:
Dependency Analysis — Check if tasks have data dependencies (A needs output from B).
Fan-Out — Launch all independent agents in parallel using .
Wait — Coordinator polls agent status until all complete.
Collect — Aggregate results, check for errors, route to next step.

title: Parallel Execution
heading: Example: Feature Implementation
text:
"Implement user authentication: API endpoints, frontend form, tests, and documentation"
Coordinator spawns 4 agents in parallel:
Backend → API endpoints
Frontend → Login/signup form
Tester → Integration tests
DevRel → Auth documentation
All work simultaneously. No agent waits for another unless there's a code dependency.

title: Parallel Execution
heading: Background vs Sync Mode
text:
Mode   When to Use   Behavior  
 ------ ------------- ---------- 
     Independent work, no data dependencies   Agent runs in parallel, coordinator polls for completion  
     Data dependency (one agent needs output from another)   Agent runs sequentially, coordinator waits  
     Reviewer gate (Lead must approve before continuing)   Agent runs, coordinator waits for review decision

title: Parallel Execution
heading: Background Mode
text:
Used for fan-out parallelism:
Agents don't see each other's output until the coordinator collects and synthesizes.

title: Parallel Execution
heading: Sync Mode
text:
Used for dependencies and gates:
Each step blocks until the previous completes.

title: Parallel Execution
heading: Eager Execution Philosophy
text:
Squad's default is eager parallelism — launch everything that can run, let the coordinator handle synchronization. Benefits:
Faster throughput — No artificial sequencing.
Better resource utilization — Multiple agents saturate available compute.
Resilient to blocking — If one agent stalls, others keep working.
Trade-off: Increased API cost (multiple agents running simultaneously). If cost is a concern, tell the coordinator:
"Work sequentially to save costs"
Coordinator switches to sync mode for all agents.

title: Parallel Execution
heading: Deadlock Avoidance
text:
When agents have circular dependencies:
Agent A needs output from Agent B
Agent B needs output from Agent A
The coordinator detects the cycle during dependency analysis and prompts:

title: Parallel Execution
heading: Reviewer Gates
text:
Some tasks require sequential review:
Agent writes code → Draft PR
Lead reviews → Approves or rejects
If approved → Merge and close
If rejected → Reassign or escalate (agent is locked out)
This is a sync gate — the next step cannot proceed until the reviewer completes.

title: Parallel Execution
heading: Parallel Execution Logs
text: The coordinator logs parallel execution in :

title: Parallel Execution
heading: Parallel Limits
text:
The coordinator respects concurrency limits to avoid rate limits or resource exhaustion:
Default: 5 agents in parallel
Adjustable:  → Coordinator batches work in groups of 3

title: Parallel Execution
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Coordinator spawns all relevant agents (Frontend, Backend, Tester, DevRel) simultaneously.
Forces sync mode: Backend runs, completes, then Tester starts.
Spawns 3 agents in parallel, one per issue. Assumes no dependencies between issues.
Sets concurrency limit. Coordinator batches work: runs 2, waits for completion, runs next 2.
Coordinator explains why Tester is blocked (e.g., waiting for Backend to finish implementation).

title: Plugin Marketplace Guide
heading: What Are Plugins?
text: Your Squad can discover and install plugins — curated collections of agent templates, skills, and instructions from community repositories. Plugins solve recurring problems: Azure cloud development, React patterns, security hardening, testing strategies, and more.

title: Plugin Marketplace Guide
heading: What Are Plugins?
text:
Plugins are community-curated bundles of reusable knowledge. Each plugin contains:
Agent templates — specialized role charters for common needs (e.g., "AWS DevOps", "Python Data Science")
Skills — reusable  files encoding patterns and best practices
Instructions —  snippets for conventions and routing rules
Sample prompts — ready-to-use prompts that activate plugin capabilities
A plugin marketplace is a repository that hosts these bundles. Squad ships with community marketplaces pre-configured. You can add your own.

title: Plugin Marketplace Guide
heading: Available Marketplaces
text:
Marketplace   URL   What's Inside  
 ------------- ----- ----------- 
  awesome-copilot      Frontend frameworks (React, Vue, Svelte), backend stacks (Node, Python, Go), deployment patterns  
  anthropic-skills      Claude-optimized patterns, prompt engineering, token efficiency, RAG patterns  
  azure-cloud-dev      Azure VMs, App Service, Cosmos DB, DevOps with GitHub Actions, infrastructure-as-code  
  security-hardening      OWASP, input validation, secrets management, cryptography, compliance patterns

text:
# PRD Mode
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to generate a requirements document:
Try this to break down product specs into work items:
Give Squad a product requirements document and the Lead breaks it into prioritized work items, assigns them to the team, and tracks progress with dependency management.

title: PRD Mode
heading: How to Use
text: Give Squad a product requirements document. The Lead agent breaks it into work items, assigns them to the team, and tracks progress.

title: PRD Mode
heading: How to Use
text:
Paste a PRD directly into the chat:
Or reference a file:

title: PRD Mode
heading: How It Works
text:
Lead decomposes the spec into discrete work items (WI-1, WI-2, WI-3, etc.)
Each work item gets a priority: P0 (must-have), P1 (important), P2 (nice-to-have)
Work items are assigned to agents based on domain expertise
Dependencies are tracked — Squad won't start WI-4 if it depends on WI-2 finishing first
Parallel work happens where possible — independent work items run simultaneously

title: PRD Mode
heading: Mid-Project PRD Updates
text:
Requirements change. When they do, give Squad the updated PRD:
The Lead agent:
Re-reads the PRD
Diffs against the existing work items
Adjusts the backlog — adds new items, re-prioritizes, or marks items as obsolete
Work already completed isn't undone. Only the remaining backlog changes.

title: PRD Mode
heading: Tips
text:
P0 work items are tackled first. Use priority levels to control sequencing.
The Lead handles decomposition — you don't need to break down the spec yourself.
Dependencies are respected automatically. You won't see an agent start on a dependent task before its prerequisite is done.
Combine with GitHub Issues Mode to create GitHub issues from work items.

title: PRD Mode
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Ingests a product requirements document and creates a prioritized, dependency-tracked backlog.
Displays the current backlog with priorities, assignments, and dependencies.
Re-ingests the PRD and adjusts the backlog based on changes without undoing completed work.
Begins parallel execution of all high-priority work items with no blockers.
Shows which tasks are waiting on dependencies or other blocking conditions.

title: Project Boards
heading: Project Boards
text:
# Project Boards
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to visualize workflow tracking:
Try this to link issues to a board:
Squad integrates with GitHub Projects V2 for visual workflow tracking. Labels are the source of truth, boards are one-way projections that visualize the state machine.

title: Project Boards
heading: Prerequisites
text:
GitHub Projects V2 access requires the  scope:
Verify:
You should see  (or similar).

title: Project Boards
heading: How It Works
text:
Squad treats labels as the state machine and boards as a read-mostly visualization:
Labels drive state — Issue gets  +  → state changes to "In Progress".
Board updates — Squad syncs label changes to the project board automatically.
Board changes propagate — If you drag an issue to "Done" on the board, Squad applies the corresponding label ().
Labels are authoritative. Boards reflect labels, not the other way around.

title: Project Boards
heading: Board Structure
text:
Squad's default board has 5 columns matching issue lifecycle:
  Column   Label State   Description  
 -------- ------------- ------------- 
  Backlog    or    Not approved or deferred  
  Needs Research      Lead is investigating feasibility  
  Ready   , no    Approved, awaiting assignment  
  In Progress    +    Agent actively working  
  Done   Issue closed   Completed and merged

On label change — Issue labeled  → moves to "Ready" column
On issue close → moves to "Done" column

Example:

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Ralph — Work Monitor
text:
# Ralph — Work Monitor
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to see active work:
Try this to identify blockers:
Try this to auto-assign work:
Ralph tracks the work queue, monitors CI status, and ensures the team never sits idle when there's work to do. He's always on the roster and requires GitHub CLI access.

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: What Ralph Does
text:
Ralph is a built-in squad member whose job is keeping tabs on work. Like Scribe tracks decisions, Ralph tracks and drives the work queue. He's always on the roster — not cast from a universe — and has one job: make sure the team never sits idle when there's work to do.
Ralph uses intelligent routing to match work to the right agent. Rather than simple keyword matching against role titles, Ralph reads  — your team's work-type definitions and module ownership — to make smart triage and dispatch decisions. This is the same intelligence the in-session coordinator uses.

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Prerequisites
text: Ralph requires access to GitHub Issues and Pull Requests via the  CLI. A GitHub PAT (Personal Access Token) with Classic scope is required.

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Why PAT Classic?
text:
The default  provided by Copilot does not have sufficient scopes to read and write GitHub Issues and PRs. Ralph needs to:
List and read issues
Create and update issue labels and assignments
Read and interact with pull requests
Report on CI status

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Setup
text:
Create a PAT Classic token:
   - Go to https://github.com/settings/tokens
   - Click "Generate new token (classic)"
   - Select scopes:  and  (full access to repositories and projects)
   - Copy the token
Authenticate with :
   
   - Select "GitHub.com"
   - Select "HTTPS" for protocol
   - When asked "Authenticate Git with your GitHub credentials?", answer "Yes"
   - Choose "Paste an authentication token" and paste your PAT Classic token
Verify authentication:
   
Once authenticated, Ralph can monitor your repository's issues and PRs.

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: How It Works
text: Once activated, Ralph continuously checks for pending work — open issues, draft PRs, review feedback, CI failures — and keeps the squad moving through the backlog without manual nudges. Ralph's behavior is built on three layers: in-session coordinator, watch mode for local polling, and cloud heartbeat for fully unattended monitoring.

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Routing-Aware Triage
text:
Ralph doesn't rely on dumb keyword matching. He reads your  file to understand:
Work types — categories like "Core runtime", "Docs & messaging", "Tests & quality"
Agent assignments — which agent owns each domain
Module ownership — which files belong to which agent (e.g.,  → Baer)
When triaging an issue, Ralph uses this priority order:
Module path match — If the issue mentions a file in , it routes to Baer (primary owner)
Routing rule keywords — If the issue mentions "docs" or "messaging", Ralph looks up those work types and assigns the matching agent (McManus for "Docs & messaging")
Role keywords — If no module or routing rule matches, Ralph scans the issue for role titles ("test", "security", "performance")
Lead fallback — If still no match, escalate to the team Lead for manual review
This ensures Ralph makes intelligent decisions based on your team's actual structure, not generic heuristics.

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: In-Session (Copilot Chat)
text:
When you're in a Copilot session, Ralph self-chains the coordinator's work loop:
Agents complete a batch of work
Ralph checks GitHub for more: untriaged issues, assigned-but-unstarted items, draft PRs, failing CI
Work found → triage, assign, spawn agents
Results collected → Ralph checks again immediately — no pause, no asking permission
Board clear → Ralph idles (use  for persistent polling)
Ralph never stops on his own while work remains. He keeps cycling through the backlog until every issue is closed, every PR is merged, and CI is green. When the board clears, Ralph idles — run  in a separate terminal for persistent polling, or use the cloud heartbeat for fully unattended monitoring. The only things that stop Ralph's active loop: the board is clear, you say "idle"/"stop", or the session ends.

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Between Sessions (GitHub Actions Heartbeat)
text:
When no one is at the keyboard, the  workflow runs on event-based triggers (issue close, PR merge, manual dispatch). It:
Finds untriaged -labeled issues
Auto-triages based on your routing.md — matching issues to the right agent by work type and module ownership
Assigns  labels
For  (if enabled with auto-assign): assigns  so the coding agent picks up work autonomously
This creates a fully autonomous loop for  — heartbeat triages → assigns → agent works → issue closed → heartbeat finds next issue → repeat. For continuous periodic monitoring, use  locally.

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Work-in-Progress Monitoring
text:
Ralph doesn't just dispatch work and forget about it. Once an issue is assigned or a PR is created, Ralph watches the work — tracking its lifecycle from assigned → PR created → review requested → CI running → approved → merged. Each completed step triggers a re-scan:
Assigned but no PR: Ralph checks if the assigned agent has started work
PR created: Ralph monitors for review feedback and CI status
Changes requested: Ralph routes the feedback back to the author agent
CI passing: Ralph marks as ready to merge
PR merged: Ralph closes the corresponding issue and picks up the next work item
This continuous watch prevents work from getting stuck in intermediate states — Ralph catches stalled PRs, failed CI, and review bottlenecks automatically.

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Board State
text:
Ralph maintains an internal view of the work board. Work items flow through these categories:
  Category   Meaning   Label(s)  
 ---------- --------- ---------- 
  Untriaged   Issue has  label but no  assignment    only  
  Assigned   Issue assigned to a squad member, awaiting agent start     
  In Progress   Agent has started work (draft PR exists or assignee begun)    + issue assigned  
  Needs Review   PR created, awaiting review feedback or approval    + PR open  
  Changes Requested   PR review came back with feedback    +   
  CI Failure   PR checks are failing    +   
  Ready to Merge   PR approved, all checks passing    +   
  Done   PR merged, issue closed   (removed from board)  
Ralph uses these categories internally to decide what action to take next. When you ask for status, Ralph reports the current board state across all these categories.

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: What Wakes Ralph Up
text:
Ralph monitors work at three different layers, each with different wake-up triggers:
In-Session (Copilot Chat):
Agent completes work → Ralph immediately checks for next item (no delay)
You say "Ralph, go" or "Ralph, status" → Ralph starts active loop
You say "Ralph, idle" → Ralph stops checking
Watch Mode ( CLI):
Poll interval expires (default 10 min) → Ralph checks GitHub
You press Ctrl+C → Ralph stops
Cloud Heartbeat (GitHub Actions events):
Issue close event → Ralph checks for next item
PR merge event → Ralph checks for next item
Manual dispatch via GitHub Actions UI → Ralph checks GitHub
In all three layers, when Ralph wakes up, he scans the board, triages any untriaged items using routing.md, dispatches work to the right agent, watches in-flight items for progress, and reports results.

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Talking to Ralph
text:
What you say   What happens  
 --- --- 
  "Ralph, go" / "Ralph, start monitoring"   Activates the work-check loop  
  "Keep working" / "Work until done"   Activates Ralph  
  "Ralph, status" / "What's on the board?"   Runs one check cycle, reports results  
  "Ralph, idle" / "Take a break"   Stops the loop  
  "Ralph, scope: just issues"   Monitors only issues, skips PRs/CI

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: What Ralph Monitors
text:
Category   Signal   Action  
 --- --- --- 
  Untriaged issues    label, no  label   Lead triages and assigns  
  Assigned issues    label, no assignee/PR yet   Spawn agent to pick it up  
  Draft PRs   Squad member PR still in draft   Check if agent is stalled  
  Review feedback   Changes requested on PR   Route to author agent  
  CI failures   PR checks failing   Notify agent to fix  
  Approved PRs   Ready to merge   Merge and close issue

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Periodic Check-In
text:
Ralph doesn't run silently forever. Every 3-5 rounds, Ralph reports and keeps going:
Ralph does not ask permission to continue — he keeps working. The only things that stop Ralph: the board is clear, you say "idle"/"stop", or the session ends.

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Watch Mode (`squad watch`)
text:
Ralph's in-session loop processes work while it exists, then idles. For persistent polling when you're away from the keyboard, run the  command in a separate terminal:
This runs as a standalone local process (not inside Copilot) that:
Checks GitHub every N minutes for untriaged squad work
Auto-triages issues based on team roles and keywords
Assigns @copilot to  issues (if auto-assign is enabled)
Runs until Ctrl+C

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Three layers of Ralph
text:
Layer   When   How  
 ------- ------ ----- 
  In-session   You're at the keyboard   "Ralph, go" — active loop while work exists  
  Local watchdog   You're away but machine is on     
  Cloud heartbeat   Fully unattended    GitHub Actions events (issue close, PR merge, manual dispatch)

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Ralph's Board View
text: When you ask for status:

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Heartbeat Workflow Setup
text:
The heartbeat workflow () is automatically installed during  or . It runs:
On issue close: Checks for next item in backlog
On PR merge: Checks for follow-up work
On manual dispatch: Trigger via GitHub Actions UI
For persistent polling when you're away, use  locally — it polls at your chosen interval without consuming GitHub Actions minutes.

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Notes
text:
Ralph is session-scoped — his state (active/idle, round count, stats) resets each session
Ralph appears on the roster like Scribe: 
Ralph is exempt from universe casting — always "Ralph"
The heartbeat workflow is the between-session complement to in-session Ralph

title: Ralph — Work Monitor
heading: Sample Prompts
text:

title: Rate Limiting
heading: Rate Limiting
text:
# Rate Limiting

⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.

Try this to check rate limit status:


Try this to adjust pacing:


Squad monitors API rate limit headroom in real time and pauses work before limits are reached — not after. This prevents cascading failures across concurrent agents.

title: Rate Limiting
heading: How It Works
text:
Squad tracks the rate limit headers returned by every API call. Before dispatching the next request, it checks remaining headroom against a configurable threshold. If headroom is below the threshold, it pauses and waits for the window to reset.

This is cooperative: agents yield voluntarily rather than hammering the API and hitting hard errors.

title: Rate Limiting
heading: The RAAS Traffic-Light Pattern
text:
Squad uses a three-state model for rate limit health:

  State   Meaning   Behavior  
 ------- --------- ---------- 
  🟢 Green   Headroom is healthy   Proceed normally  
  🟡 Amber   Headroom is low (below threshold)   Slow down, reduce concurrency  
  🔴 Red   At or near limit   Pause all requests, wait for reset  

The system transitions between states automatically as headroom changes. You do not need to configure thresholds manually — defaults are tuned for typical LLM API quotas.

title: Rate Limiting
heading: When It Engages
text:
Rate limiting engages when:

Remaining requests in the current window drop below 20% of the quota
A  response is received (reactive fallback)
Concurrent agent count is high and projected usage exceeds headroom

title: Rate Limiting
heading: Recovery Behavior
text:
When the circuit is paused (🔴 Red):

All pending requests queue in memory.
Squad polls the rate limit reset timestamp from the API response headers.
At reset, Squad resumes from the queue — oldest requests first.
State transitions back to 🟢 Green automatically.

No work is dropped. Queued tasks resume without requiring user intervention.

title: Rate Limiting
heading: Concurrency and Pacing
text: In Amber state, Squad reduces the number of agents dispatching simultaneously. This distributes the remaining quota across a longer window rather than exhausting it instantly.

title: Rate Limiting
heading: See Also
text:
Model Selection — economy mode for cost and rate limit management
Parallel Execution — how concurrent agents share API quota
Cost Tracking — monitor spend alongside rate limit usage

title: Squad Remote Control
heading: Security Model
text: Remote access has 7 layers of security:

title: Squad Remote Control
heading: 1. **devtunnel Private Auth**
text:
URL requires  header (devtunnel access token)
Tunnel is private by default — no public discovery

title: Squad Remote Control
heading: 2. **Session Token (UUID, 4-hour TTL)**
text:
Each session gets a unique token, valid for 4 hours
Token embedded in QR code or shown as connection string
Expires automatically

title: Squad Remote Control
heading: 3. **Ticket-Based WebSocket Auth**
text:
First request exchanges session token for single-use ticket
Ticket valid 60 seconds, single use only
Prevents token replay attacks

title: Squad Remote Control
heading: 4. **HTTP Rate Limiting**
text:
30 requests per minute per IP address
Blocks brute-force connection attempts
Rate limit resets hourly

title: Squad Remote Control
heading: 5. **Environment Variable Blocklist**
text:
27 common secret patterns redacted from output
Blocks: , , , , , , , etc.
ANSI escape sequences cannot bypass redaction

title: Squad Remote Control
heading: 6. **Secret Redaction (27 Patterns + ANSI Bypass Prevention)**
text:
Secrets matching patterns replaced with 
ANSI codes cannot hide redaction logic
Example:  →

title: Squad Remote Control
heading: 7. **Connection Limits**
text:
Global: Max 5 concurrent phone connections per session
Per IP: Max 2 concurrent connections per IP address
Excess connections rejected with 429 (Too Many Requests)

title: Squad Remote Control
heading: Mobile Keyboard
text:
When your phone connects, a key bar appears below the terminal:
  Key   Action  
 ----- -------- 
  ↑ / ↓   Scroll history / scroll terminal output  
  ← / →   Move cursor left / right  
  Tab   Insert tab character (or autocomplete if supported)  
  Enter   Send command / newline  
  Esc   Send Escape key (menu mode, cancel)  
  Ctrl+C   Send interrupt signal (SIGINT) — kills running command  
  Space   Insert space  
  ⌫   Backspace / delete

title: Squad Remote Control
heading: Replay Buffer
text:
When a new phone joins the session:
Terminal history is replayed — joins don't see a blank screen
Replay window — last 1000 lines of terminal output
Scrollback included — can scroll to see previous commands
This means late-joiners see context, not blank canvas.

title: Squad Remote Control
heading: Session Dashboard
text:
List and manage active devtunnel sessions:
Kill a session:

title: Squad Remote Control
heading: PTY-Only Mode
text:
Remote Control runs in PTY-only mode — no Copilot ACP (Agent Control Protocol) messages flow through the WebSocket. The terminal is a mirror, not a command channel:
Terminal I/O (text, control codes) ↔ WebSocket
ACP protocol stays local to the Copilot process
No agent instructions flow through the tunnel
This design keeps the tunnel stateless and reduces surface area.

title: Squad Remote Control
heading: Audit Logging
text:
All connections, authentication, and security events are logged:
Each line is a JSON object:
Events logged:
 — Phone connected
 — Phone disconnected
 — Token/ticket validation failed
 — Rate limit exceeded
 — Secret pattern matched and redacted
 — Command executed (summary, no args)
Rotate daily, keep 30 days by default.
