title: Response Modes
heading: Response Modes
text:
# Response Modes
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to get concise answers:
Try this to speed up simple tasks:
Squad automatically picks the right response mode based on complexity — from instant direct answers (2s) to full multi-agent parallel work (60s). You can override anytime.

title: Response Modes
heading: The Four Modes
text:
Not every request needs the full agent machinery. Squad automatically selects a response mode based on the complexity of your message.
  Mode   Time   What Happens   When Used  
 ------ ------ ------------- ----------- 
  Direct   2–3s   Coordinator answers without spawning an agent   Status checks, factual questions  
  Lightweight   8–12s   One agent, minimal prompt — skips charter, history, and decisions   Small fixes, quick follow-ups  
  Standard   25–35s   Full agent spawn with charter, history, and decisions   Normal work requests  
  Full   40–60s   Multiple agents spawn in parallel, each with full context   Complex multi-domain tasks

title: Response Modes
heading: Direct
text:
The coordinator handles it alone — no sub-agent is spawned. This isn't a response mode in the SDK sense; the coordinator answers the question itself using context it already has (team roster, decisions, history).
Fast answers from context the coordinator already has. If the coordinator responds with , no agent session is created.

title: Response Modes
heading: Lightweight
text:
One agent is spawned with a reduced prompt — skips loading charter, history, and decisions to save time.
Good for small, well-defined tasks where full context isn't needed.

title: Response Modes
heading: Standard
text:
Full agent spawn. The agent reads its charter, history, and team decisions before working.
This is the default mode for most work.

title: Response Modes
heading: Full
text:
Multiple agents spawn in parallel, each with full context. A design review ceremony may trigger first.
Used for complex tasks that span multiple domains (frontend, backend, testing).

title: Response Modes
heading: How Modes Are Selected
text:
The coordinator picks the mode automatically based on:
Complexity of the request
Number of domains involved
Whether context is needed (history, decisions, skills)
You don't need to specify a mode. When uncertain, the coordinator biases toward upgrading — it's better to spend a few extra seconds loading context than to miss something.

title: Response Modes
heading: Tips
text:
If a response feels slow for a simple question, it's likely using Standard when Direct would suffice. This is rare — the coordinator is good at picking the right mode.
"Team, ..." prompts typically trigger Full mode.
Direct-named agent prompts ("Kane, ...") typically trigger Standard mode.
Response times depend on the Copilot platform. The numbers above are approximate.

title: Response Modes
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Explicitly requests a reduced-context spawn for a simple task.
Quick factual question that triggers Direct mode with no agent spawn.
Requests Standard mode with full context load for complex work.
Checks which mode the coordinator selected for the previous request.
Multi-domain prompt that triggers Full mode with parallel agent spawns.

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
text:
# Reviewer Rejection Protocol
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to request a code review:
Try this to trigger peer review:
When a reviewer (Lead, Tester) rejects work, the original agent is locked out from self-revision. This prevents endless fix-retry loops and forces human oversight or reassignment.

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: How It Works
text:
Agent submits work — Creates draft PR, requests review from Lead or Tester.
Reviewer evaluates — Checks code quality, test coverage, adherence to directives.
Reviewer decision:
   - Approve → PR merges, issue closes, agent unlocked.
   - Request changes → Agent is locked out, work routes to another agent or escalates.

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: Strict Lockout
text:
Once a reviewer rejects work, the original agent cannot revise their own submission. This is a hard constraint:
Agent A writes code → Lead rejects
Agent A cannot fix and resubmit
Coordinator must reassign to Agent B or escalate to user

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: Why Lockout?
text:
Without lockout:
Agent A writes buggy code
Lead rejects: "This has race conditions"
Agent A fixes, resubmits
Lead rejects again: "Still broken"
Agent A fixes, resubmits
Infinite loop, no progress
With lockout:
Agent A writes buggy code
Lead rejects: "This has race conditions"
Agent A locked out
Coordinator assigns Agent B (fresh perspective) or escalates to user
Work gets done or human intervenes

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: Reassign vs. Escalate
text:
When rejection happens, coordinator has two options:
  Option   When to Use   How It Works  
 -------- ------------- -------------- 
  Reassign   Another agent has the skill   Route work to different squad member with relevant expertise  
  Escalate   No other agent fits, or multiple rejections   Notify user, ask for manual intervention or guidance

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: Reassign Example
text:
Fenster (Frontend) writes a React component → Lead rejects: "Accessibility issues"
Fenster locked out
Coordinator checks skills: Hockney (Frontend) has accessibility expertise
Work reassigned to Hockney
Hockney fixes and resubmits

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: Escalate Example
text:
Backend writes API logic → Tester rejects: "Integration tests fail"
Backend locked out
Coordinator reassigns to Core Dev → Core Dev also fails review
Core Dev locked out
All agents exhausted → Coordinator escalates to user: "Issue #42 rejected twice. Need guidance or manual fix."

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: Lockout Scope and Duration
text:
Scope   Duration  
 ------- ---------- 
  Task-specific   Lockout applies to the specific PR/issue, not all work  
  Session-persistent   Lockout survives session restarts (stored in )  
  Clearable   User can manually unlock: "Unlock Fenster for issue #42"  
An agent locked out of issue #42 can still work on issue #43, #44, etc. Lockout is not a global ban.

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: Deadlock Handling
text:
If all capable agents are locked out:
Coordinator detects deadlock: no available agents for work.
Coordinator escalates to user: "All agents locked out for issue #42. Options: 1) Manual fix, 2) Unlock an agent and provide guidance, 3) Close as won't-fix."
User chooses resolution.
This prevents the team from getting stuck in a state where no one can proceed.

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: Reviewer Authority
text:
Only designated reviewers can lock out agents:
  Reviewer   Authority   Scope  
 ---------- ----------- ------- 
  Lead   Code quality, architecture, security   All code submissions  
  Tester   Test coverage, correctness   Test-related changes  
  User (you)   Final arbiter   Can override any decision  
Other agents (Frontend, Backend, DevRel) cannot lock out peers.

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: Unlocking an Agent
text:
"Unlock Fenster for issue #42"
Coordinator clears the lockout. Fenster can now revise the PR. Use this when:
Reviewer feedback was unclear, you've provided better guidance
Agent legitimately misunderstood requirements
External factors (API change, dependency update) invalidated the original rejection

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: Lockout Logs
text: Lockouts are recorded in :

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: Trust Levels for PR Management
text: This section covers the spectrum of human oversight for Squad-created PRs:

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: 1. Full Review (Default)
text:
Every PR requires human approval before merge. This is the default and recommended for team repos, shared codebases, and anything with external collaborators.
When to use: Team repositories, public packages, shared codebases where multiple people depend on stability.
Risk: Low — human gate on every change.

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: 2. Selective Review
text:
Squad creates and reviews PRs, but the human only reviews PRs that touch specific paths or domains they care about. Everything else merges after agent review.
When to use: Personal projects with established patterns where you trust Squad's judgment on routine changes (dependency updates, test fixes, doc improvements).
Risk: Medium — some changes skip human eyes.

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: 3. Self-Managing (Personal Repos Only)
text:
Squad creates, reviews, approves, and merges its own PRs. The human only jumps in when an issue is explicitly flagged for review.
When to use: Solo personal projects where you're the sole maintainer and experimentation speed matters more than pre-merge safety.
Risk: Higher — but fast; review PRs retroactively.

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: Decision Matrix
text:
Trust Level   When   Risk  
 ------------- ------ ------ 
  Full review   Team repos, shared codebases, public packages   Low — human gate on every change  
  Selective review   Personal projects with established patterns   Medium — some changes skip human eyes  
  Self-managing   Solo personal projects, experimentation   Higher — but fast; review PRs retroactively  
Important: Self-managing mode doesn't mean unmonitored. Use Ralph's work monitoring, Teams notifications, and periodic code review to stay informed. The difference is that you review after merge rather than before.

title: Reviewer Rejection Protocol
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Triggers review. Lead evaluates code and either approves (merge + unlock) or rejects (lockout original author).
Coordinator explains: "Fenster was locked out for issue #42 after Lead rejected PR #15 due to security concerns."
Clears lockout. Fenster can now revise the PR with your additional context.
Manual reassignment. Fenster remains locked out, Hockney takes over the work.
Coordinator notifies you of deadlock or repeated rejections. You provide manual intervention or guidance.

src/api/auth.ts`, it routes to Backend automatically.

When multiple patterns match:
Most specific wins —  matches both  and , but  is more specific.

GitHub issues with  labels route directly:

title: Skills System
heading: Skills System
text:
# Skills System
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to see what your team learned:
Try this to list all accumulated knowledge:
Try this to document a reusable pattern:
Agents learn from real work and write skill files — reusable patterns, conventions, and techniques. Skills compound over time, making your team smarter with each project.

title: Skills System
heading: Where Skills Live
text:
Each skill is a directory containing a  file. Skills are team-wide knowledge — not tied to individual agents. All agents can read and use any skill.
Portable across projects: Skills export and import with your team. When you move a trained team to a new repo, all their earned knowledge comes with them.

title: Skills System
heading: Starter skills
text: Bundled when you initialize Squad. Prefixed with  (e.g., ). These encode baseline patterns for working with Squad.

title: Skills System
heading: Session Recovery
text: The  skill teaches agents to find and resume interrupted Copilot CLI sessions. When a session is interrupted (terminal crash, network drop, machine restart), in-progress work may be left incomplete. This skill uses  SQL queries to detect abandoned sessions, inspect checkpoint progress, and resume work. See  for query patterns and examples.

title: Skills System
heading: Earned skills
text: Written by agents from real work on your project. When an agent discovers a reusable pattern — a deployment strategy, a testing technique, an API integration approach — it writes a skill file.

title: Skills System
heading: Confidence Lifecycle
text:
Earned skills have a confidence level that reflects how battle-tested they are:
  Level   Meaning  
 ------- --------- 
  Low   First written — based on a single experience  
  Medium   Applied successfully in multiple contexts  
  High   Well-established, consistently reliable  
Confidence only goes up, never down. A skill that reaches  stays there.

title: Skills System
heading: How Skills Are Used
text:
Before working, agents read skill files relevant to the task at hand
Skill-aware routing — the coordinator checks available skills when deciding which agent to spawn. An agent with a relevant earned skill may be preferred over one without.
After working, agents may write new skills or update existing ones based on what they learned

title: Skills System
heading: Example
text:
After successfully setting up a CI pipeline, an agent might create:
`markdown
# CI with GitHub Actions
Confidence: medium

title: Skills System
heading: Pattern
text:
Use  for repo access
Cache nodemodules with  using hash of package-lock.json
Run lint, test, and build as separate jobs for parallel execution
Use  groups to cancel superseded runs

title: Skills System
heading: Learned from
text:
Initial CI setup (session 3)
Pipeline optimization after slow builds (session 7)
`

title: Skills System
heading: Tips
text:
Skills compound over time. A mature project has skills covering testing patterns, deployment procedures, API conventions, and more.
Starter skills () are overwritten on upgrade. Earned skills are never touched.
Skills are shared across the whole team — any agent can read any skill. They're stored in a flat  directory, not per-agent files.
You can manually edit skill files if you want to seed knowledge (e.g., paste your team's existing conventions into a ).
Skills survive export/import — your team's accumulated knowledge is fully portable across projects.

title: Skills System
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Shows all skill files in  with confidence levels for earned skills.
Checks how battle-tested a specific earned skill is.
Manually creates a new skill file and guides you through documenting the pattern.
Finds recently-created skills that haven't been validated across multiple contexts yet.
Manually increases the confidence level after successful repeated use.

title: squad rc
heading: squad rc
text:
# squad rc
Full remote control of GitHub Copilot from any device. ACP passthrough mode for complete Copilot CLI access via secure tunnel.

title: squad rc
heading: What It Does
text: (remote control) exposes GitHub Copilot CLI over a secure WebSocket tunnel, letting you chat with Copilot from your phone, tablet, or any browser. Unlike  (which mirrors terminal output),  uses ACP passthrough — raw JSON-RPC communication directly with Copilot's Agent Communication Protocol. You get full Copilot capabilities, not just terminal visibility.

title: squad rc
heading: How It Differs from `squad start`
text:
Feature                                                                     
 -------------------------- ------------------------------------- ---------------------------------- 
  Mode                   ACP passthrough (JSON-RPC)            PTY mirror (terminal streaming)   
  Protocol               Agent Communication Protocol (ACP)    xterm.js terminal emulation       
  Access                 Full Copilot capabilities             Terminal output only              
  Use Case               Remote Copilot control                Demo/pair on terminal sessions    
  Input                  Chat messages → Copilot stdin         Keyboard input → PTY              
  Output                 Copilot responses → WebSocket         Terminal ANSI → WebSocket         
  Mobile Optimized       Yes (PWA, QR code, chat UI)           Yes (xterm.js, keyboard overlay)  
  Startup Time           15-20s (MCP server loading)          Immediate                         
  Team Roster            Loaded from           Not applicable                    
When to use : You want to control Copilot remotely (ask questions, run commands, access full agent capabilities).
When to use : You want to mirror a terminal session to your phone (demos, pairing, watching long-running processes).
💡 Looking for terminal mirroring? See squad start.

devtunnel CLI (for  mode)

Outbound (Remote → Copilot):
User types message in browser

Inbound (Copilot → Remote):

title: Squad SubSquads
heading: Squad SubSquads
text:
# Squad SubSquads
Scale Squad across multiple Codespaces by partitioning work into labeled SubSquads.

title: Squad SubSquads
heading: What Are SubSquads?
text: A SubSquad is a named partition of work within a Squad project. Each SubSquad targets a specific GitHub label (e.g., , ) and optionally restricts agents to certain directories. Multiple Squad instances — each running in its own Codespace — can each activate a different SubSquad, enabling parallel work across teams.

title: Squad SubSquads
heading: Why SubSquads?
text:
Squad was originally designed for a single team per repository. As projects grow, a single Codespace becomes a bottleneck:
Model rate limits — One Codespace hitting API limits slows the whole team
Context overload — Ralph picks up all issues, not just the relevant ones
Folder conflicts — Multiple agents editing the same files causes merge pain
SubSquads solve this by giving each Codespace a scoped view of the project.

title: Squad SubSquads
heading: 2. Activate a SubSquad
text:
There are three ways to tell Squad which SubSquad to use:
#### Environment Variable (recommended for Codespaces)
Set this in your Codespace's environment or devcontainer.json:
#### .squad-workstream File (local activation)
This writes a  file (gitignored) so the setting is local to your machine.
#### Auto-select (single SubSquad)
If  contains only one SubSquad, it's automatically selected.

title: Squad SubSquads
heading: 3. Resolution Priority
text:
env var (highest)
 file
Single-SubSquad auto-select
No SubSquad (classic single-squad mode)

title: Squad SubSquads
heading: SubSquad Definition Fields
text:
Field   Required   Description  
 ------- ---------- ------------- 
     Yes   Unique SubSquad identifier (kebab-case)  
     Yes   GitHub label to filter issues  
     No   Directories this SubSquad may modify  
     No    (default) or   
     No   Human-readable purpose

title: Squad SubSquads
heading: CLI Reference
text: Note:  and  are deprecated aliases for .

title: Squad SubSquads
heading: Triage (Ralph)
text: When a SubSquad is active, Ralph's triage only picks up issues labeled with the SubSquad's . Unmatched issues are left for other SubSquads or the main squad.

title: Squad SubSquads
heading: Workflow Enforcement
text:
branch-per-issue (default): Every issue gets its own branch and PR. Agents never commit directly to main.
direct: Agents may commit directly (useful for infra/ops SubSquads).

title: Squad SubSquads
heading: Folder Scope
text:
When  is set, agents should primarily modify files within those directories. However,  is advisory, not a hard lock — agents may still touch shared files (types, configs, package exports) when their issue requires it. The real protection comes from  workflow: each issue gets its own branch, so two SubSquads editing the same file won't conflict until merge time.
Tip: If two SubSquads' PRs touch the same file, Git resolves non-overlapping changes automatically. For semantic conflicts (incompatible API changes), use PR review to catch them.

title: Squad SubSquads
heading: Cost Optimization: Single-Machine Multi-SubSquad
text:
You don't need a separate Codespace per SubSquad. One machine can serve multiple SubSquads:
This gives you 1× Codespace cost instead of N×, at the expense of serial (not parallel) execution. Each issue still gets its own branch — no conflicts.

title: Squad SubSquads
heading: Example: Multi-Codespace Setup
text: See Multi-Codespace Scenario for a complete walkthrough.

title: Team Setup & Init Mode
heading: Team Setup & Init Mode
text:
# Team Setup & Init Mode
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to initialize for a specific stack:
Try this to expand capabilities:
Try this to view the roster:
Squad analyzes your project and proposes a team roster with 3-7 members tailored to your stack. You can accept as-is, customize during setup, or modify the team anytime after.

title: Team Setup & Init Mode
heading: How Init Works
text: When you first run Squad in a repository, it doesn't impose a team — it proposes one. The init flow analyzes your project, suggests roles and members, waits for your confirmation, then creates the  directory structure and installs the crew.

title: Team Setup & Init Mode
heading: Character Casting
text: By default, Squad uses the CastingEngine to assign agent names from fictional universes (The Usual Suspects, Ocean's Eleven, etc.). The LLM proposes roles and team composition; the engine allocates curated character names, personalities, and backstories from the selected universe. Use  to opt into the base role catalog (Lead, Backend, Frontend, Tester) instead of universe casting.

title: Team Setup & Init Mode
heading: How Init Works
text:
Discovery — Squad scans your repository: language distribution, file structure, test frameworks, dependencies, existing workflows.
Proposal — Based on what it finds, Squad proposes a team roster with 3-7 members and their roles.
Confirmation — You review the proposal and can accept as-is, add members, remove members, or change roles.
Creation — Squad writes , creates agent directories under , and sets up the coordinator.

title: Team Setup & Init Mode
heading: Customizing During Init
text:
What you say   What happens  
 -------------- -------------- 
  "Accept" / "Looks good"   Creates team as proposed  
  "Add a designer"   Adds a designer role to the roster  
  "Remove the tester"   Drops tester from the team  
  "Change backend to Rust specialist"   Adjusts role focus for that member  
  "Make Fenster the frontend lead"   Assigns specific name to role

title: Team Setup & Init Mode
heading: Customizing After Init
text:
You can modify  directly or ask the coordinator:
"Add a security specialist to the team"
The coordinator will:
Cast a new member from the universe
Create their agent directory and charter
Update  and 
"Remove McManus from the team"
The coordinator will:
Remove the member from 
Archive their agent directory (moves to )
Update routing rules

title: Team Setup & Init Mode
heading: Default Team Composition
text:
For most projects, Squad proposes:
  Role   When Included  
 ------ -------------- 
  Lead   Always — triages, reviews, unblocks  
  Core Dev   Always — main implementation  
  Tester   If tests exist or / has test deps  
  DevRel   If README exists or docs/ present  
  Frontend   If React/Vue/Svelte/Angular detected  
  Backend   If API routes, database code, or server framework detected  
  Scribe   Always — decision logger

title: Upstream Inheritance
heading: Upstream Inheritance
text:
# Upstream Inheritance
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Upstream inheritance lets you declare external Squad sources (from repositories, local directories, or exports) and automatically inherit their context at session start. Share practices across teams, organizations, and projects without duplicating configuration.

title: Upstream Inheritance
heading: How it works
text:
At session start, the coordinator reads all declared upstreams from  and makes their context available to every agent:
Skills — 
Decisions — 
Wisdom — 
Casting policy — 
Routing — 
Resolution order: Later entries override earlier ones. Layer upstreams from org → team → repo, with each level adding or overriding as needed.
Source types:
  Type   Example   Use case  
 ------ --------- ---------- 
  local      Sibling repo, shared drive, monorepo package  
  git      Public/private team repo (with credentials)  
  export      Snapshot for offline use or version pinning

title: Upstream Inheritance
heading: Shared practices across teams
text:
Problem: Multiple teams need consistent agent definitions, decisions, and casting policy without duplicating configuration.
Solution: Create a central Squad repo (platform-squad) with shared context. Product teams add it as an upstream.
Outcome: Platform team updates practices once. All product teams inherit changes at next . Product teams can layer their own skills or override decisions as needed.
Also works for:
Open-source frameworks with community plugins
Consultancy methodology across client projects

title: Upstream Inheritance
heading: Domain consistency across services
text:
Problem: Multiple microservices share a domain model (user, order, payment). You need a single source of truth for how agents work with that model.
Solution: Create a shared-domain repo with domain-specific skills and decisions. Each service adds it as an upstream.
Outcome: All agents across services understand the domain model. Domain conventions change once; each service pulls independently. Services stay decoupled with consistency.

title: Upstream Inheritance
heading: Multi-team scaling patterns
text:
Problem: Post-acquisition, migration, or enterprise modernization requires coordinating practices across teams with different histories.
Solution: Create a unified practices or playbook repo. All teams add it as an upstream.
Outcome: Teams work independently while culturally aligned. Agents understand both traditions. Gradual convergence without painful rewrites.
Also works for:
Monolith-to-microservices modernization (playbook defines architecture patterns)
Multi-geo teams converging on shared standards

text:

Iterate: Update your upstream and run  to pull changes across all consuming projects

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: Squad in VS Code
text:
# Squad in VS Code
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Squad is fully supported in VS Code (v0.4.0+). Your team runs identically to the CLI, with the same  state, same agents, same decisions — but with VS Code-specific tooling and constraints.
This guide covers what's different, what's the same, and when to use CLI vs VS Code.

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: Prerequisites
text:
VS Code — Latest version
GitHub Copilot extension —  (installed, authenticated)
Workspace trust — Your workspace must be trusted (VS Code security)
Node.js 20+ (LTS) — If running CLI to initialize Squad
Squad installed — Either in the repo already (from CLI), or initialized fresh via agent selection

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: Initial Setup
text:
Option A: Initialize with CLI (recommended)
Creates  and . Then open VS Code and select Squad from the agent picker.
Option B: Fresh in VS Code
Open Copilot in VS Code, select Squad from . Squad detects it's running in VS Code and bootstraps normally. The  directory is created on first run.

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: How It Works
text:
Squad detects VS Code automatically and adapts its spawning mechanism:
In CLI: Uses  tool with full control (model selection, agent type, background mode)
In VS Code: Uses  for parallel synchronous execution
When you assign work to an agent, the coordinator spawns that agent as a sub-agent in VS Code. Multiple sub-agents spawn in the same turn run in parallel. Each completes, then you get all results at once — no intermediate "launch table" feedback like CLI shows.

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: No Per-Spawn Model Selection
text: VS Code accepts the session model (your Copilot model picker). No per-spawn dynamic selection. Cost optimization deferred — use Haiku via model picker for cheaper runs.

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: Sub-Agents Run Sync (But Parallel)
text: Agents launch in the same turn and run in parallel, but block as a group. Results arrive all at once — no launch table or  polling.

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: SQL Tool Not Available
text: SQL unavailable in VS Code agents. Workflows needing SQL should live in CLI, or use file-based state (JSON in ).

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: File Writes May Prompt for Approval
text: VS Code security feature: approve file modifications once with "Always allow in this workspace".

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: Same `.squad/` State
text: Initialize in CLI, use in VS Code, or vice versa. Team roster, decisions, histories are identical across both.

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: Same Team, Same Skills
text: Charters, histories, agent roles persist. Decisions made in CLI are visible in VS Code.

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: Parallel Execution Works
text: Multiple agents in one turn → all run in parallel. Equivalent throughput to CLI background mode.

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: Full File Access (Workspace-Scoped)
text: Read/write your entire workspace and  directory. Cannot reach outside workspace.

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: MCP Tools Inherited
text: If workspace has MCP servers configured, sub-agents inherit them (GitHub MCP, semantic search, terminal).

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: Tips
text:
Use single-root workspaces (multi-root has path resolution bugs).
Accept file modification approval once — subsequent writes are automatic.
For initial setup, heavy parallel work (5+ agents), SQL workflows, or cost optimization (per-spawn model selection) → use CLI.
Check the model picker at top of chat if agents seem slow or expensive — switch to Haiku for cost savings.

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: Known Limitations
text:
JetBrains IDEs — Untested. Agent spawning mechanism undocumented.
GitHub.com (web) — Untested. Copilot Chat on GitHub.com doesn't support Squad.
Custom agent model selection — Phase 2 future feature.
See Getting Started for your first VS Code session.

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: Extension Developer Guide
text: If you're building a VS Code extension that integrates with Squad, follow these patterns.

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: Import SDK Safely
text:
DO: Import specific types and functions
DON'T: Import the CLI entry point — this will call  and crash your extension.

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: Handle Errors Gracefully
text: Never call  in an extension — it crashes VS Code.

title: Squad in VS Code
heading: See Also
text:
Getting Started — Installation and setup guide
Parallel Execution — How Squadron fan-outs agents
Model Selection — Cost-first routing strategy
Interactive Shell — Shell commands and features
SDK API Reference — Full SDK type and function reference

title: Git Worktree Awareness
heading: Git Worktree Awareness
text:
# Git Worktree Awareness
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this to enable branch-specific state:
Try this to share state across branches:
Squad supports git worktrees with two strategies: worktree-local (each worktree has its own  state) and main-checkout (shared state across all worktrees).

title: Git Worktree Awareness
heading: What Are Worktrees?
text:
Git worktrees let you check out multiple branches simultaneously:
Now you have:
 (main branch)
 (feature-a branch)
 (feature-b branch)
All share the same  database but have separate working directories.

title: Git Worktree Awareness
heading: 1. Worktree-Local (Independent State)
text:
Each worktree has its own  directory. Agents in one worktree don't see state from another.
When to use:
Multiple features in parallel with different teams
Experimental branches where you want isolated Squad config
Different team compositions per worktree (e.g., frontend-only team in one, backend-only in another)
Structure:
Setup:

title: Git Worktree Awareness
heading: 2. Main-Checkout (Shared State)
text:
All worktrees share the  directory from the main checkout. Agents across worktrees see the same team, decisions, and routing rules.
When to use:
Same team working on multiple branches
Coordinated work where agents need shared context
Parallel feature development by the same squad
Structure:
Setup:
Or tell Squad:  — Squad creates the symlink automatically.

title: Git Worktree Awareness
heading: Coordinator Team Root Resolution
text:
When Squad starts in a worktree, the coordinator resolves team root:
Check for  in current directory — If exists and is not a symlink, use worktree-local strategy.
Check if  is a symlink — If yes, follow symlink to main checkout, use main-checkout strategy.
Scan parent worktrees — If no  found, search  for main worktree with .
Prompt for strategy — If ambiguous, ask: "Use worktree-local or main-checkout?"

title: Git Worktree Awareness
heading: Merge Driver for Append-Only Files
text:
Squad uses  for append-only log files to avoid conflicts across worktrees:
.gitattributes:
This ensures log entries from different worktrees don't conflict when merged back to main.

title: Git Worktree Awareness
heading: Worktree-Aware Commands
text:
When using main-checkout strategy:
  Command   Behavior  
 --------- ---------- 
     Reads shared  from main worktree  
     Writes to shared  in main worktree  
     Checks orchestration log in main worktree (sees all agents across worktrees)  
     Prompts: "Use main worktree's team or create new?"

title: Git Worktree Awareness
heading: When to Use Which Strategy
text:
Scenario   Strategy   Reason  
 ---------- ---------- -------- 
  Parallel features, same team   Main-checkout   Shared context, coordinated work  
  Experimental branch, isolated team   Worktree-local   No cross-contamination  
  Hotfix branch + feature branch   Main-checkout   Same squad, need shared decisions  
  Multiple teams in same repo   Worktree-local   Different roles, different directives  
  Solo dev, multiple branches   Main-checkout   No need for duplicate state

title: Git Worktree Awareness
heading: Switching Strategies
text: You can convert between strategies:

title: Git Worktree Awareness
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Creates worktree-local  directory. Team is independent from main worktree.
Creates symlink to main worktree's . All state is shared.
Scans all worktrees linked to this repository, reports which have  directories.
Resolves main worktree path, reads  from there (useful when in a feature worktree).
Removes worktree-local  and creates symlink to main worktree's .

title: Choose your interface
heading: Choose your interface
text:
# Choose your interface

⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.


Squad works across multiple interfaces. Pick the one that fits your workflow.

title: Choose your interface
heading: What are the ways to use Squad?
text: Squad runs in multiple modes and across multiple platforms:

title: Choose your interface
heading: GitHub Copilot CLI (`copilot` command)
text:
The conversational terminal interface. Powered by the GitHub Copilot CLI, this is the recommended way to work with Squad day-to-day.



Reads  and uses  to coordinate your team. Full feature set — sub-agent spawning, per-spawn model selection, background execution, SQL tools, parallel fan-out.

title: Choose your interface
heading: VS Code (GitHub Copilot in the editor)
text: Squad works identically in VS Code through GitHub Copilot. Same  directory, same agents, same decisions. Full file access, parallel execution, MCP tool inheritance. See Squad in VS Code for details.

title: Choose your interface
heading: Squad CLI (`squad` command)
text:
The Squad CLI provides setup, diagnostics, and automation commands. Not conversational — use this for installation, validation, and operational tasks.



See CLI Reference for all commands.

title: Choose your interface
heading: Interactive shell (`squad start` / `squad shell`)
text:
REPL mode for conversational interaction directly via the Squad CLI. Enter  with no arguments to start a persistent shell session. See Interactive Shell Guide.

This works, but GitHub Copilot CLI is recommended — richer agent experience, better tools, full MCP integration.

title: Choose your interface
heading: SDK (`@bradygaster/squad-sdk`)
text:
Programmatic access for building tools on top of Squad. Typed APIs, routing config, agent lifecycle hooks.





See SDK Reference for the complete API.

title: Choose your interface
heading: Copilot Coding Agent (`@copilot`)
text:
Autonomous GitHub bot that picks up labeled issues and opens draft PRs. Works across your entire organization without human intervention. Issue gets labeled → agent picks it up → PR gets opened → human reviews.

See Copilot Coding Agent for setup.

title: Choose your interface
heading: Which should I use?
text:
You want to...   Use   Why  
 ---------------- ----- ----- 
  Work with your squad day-to-day   GitHub Copilot CLI or VS Code   Conversational interface, full agent spawning, parallel execution. Most natural way to collaborate with your team.  
  Set up Squad in a new repo   Squad CLI ()   One command initializes  directory and all configuration.  
  Check if Squad is working   Squad CLI ()   Validates directory structure, agents, configuration integrity.  
  Monitor work 24/7   Squad CLI ()   Persistent polling for new issues, auto-triage, agent assignment.  
  View OpenTelemetry traces   Squad CLI ()   Launches Aspire dashboard for observability.  
  Process issues autonomously   Copilot Coding Agent   GitHub Actions workflow watches for labeled issues and dispatches .  
  Build tools on top of Squad   SDK   Typed APIs, configuration loading, agent lifecycle hooks.

title: Choose your interface
heading: Feature availability matrix
text:
Not every feature works everywhere. Here's what's available where:

  Feature   GitHub Copilot CLI   VS Code   Squad CLI   SDK  
 --------- :------------------: :-------: :---------: :---: 
  Agent spawning   ✅   ✅   ✅ (via shell)   ✅  
  Ralph / work monitoring   ✅   ✅   ✅ ()   ✅  
  Per-spawn model selection   ✅   ⚠️ (session model only)   ✅   ✅  
  Background execution   ✅   ⚠️ (parallel sync)   ✅   ✅  
  SQL tool   ✅   ❌   ✅   ✅  
  Aspire dashboard   ❌   ❌   ✅   ❌  
   diagnostics   ❌   ❌   ✅   ✅  
  Issue assignment to    ❌   ❌   ✅ (setup)   ❌  

Legend:
✅ Fully supported
⚠️ Limited or constrained
❌ Not available

For a detailed breakdown of VS Code constraints and CLI parity, see Client Compatibility Matrix.

title: Choose your interface
heading: "I use GitHub Copilot CLI for everything"
text: This is the recommended workflow. The CLI automatically invokes Squad CLI commands when needed.

title: Choose your interface
heading: "I run squad watch in one terminal and use GitHub Copilot CLI in another"
text: Keep Ralph monitoring issues in the background while you work conversationally.

title: Choose your interface
heading: "I use VS Code with Copilot for coding and Squad CLI for setup"
text: Initialize with CLI, work in VS Code.

title: Choose your interface
heading: See also
text:
Installation — Install Squad CLI, SDK, or use in VS Code
First Session — Get started with your first Squad conversation
Client Compatibility Matrix — Full feature comparison across platforms
CLI Reference — All Squad CLI commands
Squad in VS Code — VS Code-specific guidance
SDK Reference — Programmatic API

title: Choose your path
heading: Choose your path
text:
# Choose your path

⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.

CLI, Copilot agent, or SDK? Pick the right mode for your workflow.

title: Choose your path
heading: CLI mode
text:
Install Squad globally or per-project, then use terminal commands to initialize, route work, and manage your team.



Use for: Terminal workflows, automation scripts, CI/CD integration.

title: Choose your path
heading: Copilot agent mode
text:
Talk to Squad in GitHub Copilot CLI or VS Code. Squad is built-in as an agent. Your  directory works identically to CLI mode.



Use for: Conversational workflows, exploratory work, VS Code users.

title: Choose your path
heading: SDK mode
text:
Write TypeScript code that spawns agents, routes work, and coordinates teams programmatically. Full access to Squad's internals.





Use for: Building tools on Squad, custom integrations, advanced automation.

title: Choose your path
heading: Decision table
text:
Your goal   Use  
 --------------- --------- 
  Try Squad quickly   Copilot agent — no install  
  Work in the terminal   CLI  
  Work in VS Code   Copilot agent  
  Automate repetitive tasks   CLI or SDK  
  Build custom tooling   SDK  
  CI/CD integration   CLI or SDK

title: Choose your path
heading: Can I use multiple modes?
text:
Yes. Your  directory is the source of truth. CLI, Copilot agent, and SDK all read and write the same files. You can switch between modes anytime.

Example workflow:
Use Copilot agent to form your team and do exploratory work
Use CLI () to monitor issues in the background
Use SDK to build a custom deployment script that spawns agents

All three modes share the same memory and decisions.

title: Your First Session
heading: Your First Session
text:
# Your First Session
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
A step-by-step walkthrough from install to parallel fan-out. Follow along in your terminal.

heading: 1. Install Squad

Start with a git repo — new or existing:

text:
Start the Copilot CLI:
Select Squad from the  list (CLI) or  (VS Code). Squad greets you by name:

title: Your First Session

text:
Say "yes" or skip straight to a task (which is implicit confirmation):
Squad creates the  directory structure — team roster, routing rules, casting state, ceremony config, agent charters, and histories — all seeded with your project context. Each agent spawns to do their work.

title: Your First Session
heading: What's inside .squad/?
text:
File/Directory   Purpose  
 --- --- 
     Team roster, roles, and member info  
     Work routing rules (which agent handles what)

     Each agent's charter and history (their memory)  
     Ceremony schedule (retrospectives, reviews, etc.)  
     Team formation history and casting state  
     Reusable capabilities agents can learn  
     Format reference files for docs, PRDs, charters  
You own these files. Edit them anytime — change roles, add routing rules, fix decisions. Squad reads them before every spawn.
Commit  to version control. It's your team's brain. Anyone who clones the repo gets the team with all their knowledge.
Then it spawns Dallas:

heading: 4. Parallel Fan-Out — The Launch Table
text:
Now give a bigger task to see parallel work:
Squad decomposes the work and launches agents simultaneously:
All five agents are working at the same time in separate context windows. The tester writes test cases from the requirements while the implementation is being built — no waiting.

text:
When agents finish, you see results tagged with each agent's identity:
Behind the scenes, the Scribe merged decisions from each agent into  and logged the session.

At any point, check status:

After completion, check decisions:

title: Your First Session
heading: 2025-07-15: Cursor-based pagination for recipe lists
text:
By: Hicks
What: All list endpoints use cursor-based pagination
Why: Scales better than offset pagination for growing datasets

title: Your First Session

By: Ripley

Why: Native MongoDB capability, no external search service needed
`
Every agent reads these decisions before working. As the list grows, the team self-aligns — agents respect each other's architectural choices without you repeating them.

title: Your First Session
heading: 8. Set a Directive
text:
You notice something you want to standardize:
Squad captures it — no agent spawn needed:
This gets written to . Every agent will see it in their next spawn.

title: Your First Session
heading: 9. Export Your Squad
text:
After a productive session, export your team for portability:
This snapshot contains charters, histories, casting state, skills, and decisions. Import into another repo anytime:

title: Your First Session
heading: Tips
text:
First session is slowest. Agents have no history yet. After 2–3 sessions, they know your conventions.
Commit  — your team's brain. Anyone who clones the repo gets the full team.
Say "team" for big tasks. The word "team" triggers parallel fan-out across multiple agents.
Name an agent for focused work.  sends work to one specific agent.
Directives are sticky. Once captured, they persist across all future sessions.

title: Installation

SDK API: The public API changed significantly if you were using Squad programmatically.
Distribution: npm-only. The  install path is gone.

Key improvements since the migration from v0.5.4 beta:
Remote Squad Mode: , , and dual-root path resolution for team identity directories.

npm-only distribution: Simpler install, semantic versioning, stable and insider channels.
TypeScript strict mode: Full type safety across the SDK and CLI.
Semver fix: Version format now follows the semver spec ().
Node 22+ compatibility: ESM import fixes for vscode-jsonrpc (v0.8.23+).
Casting system: Universe-based agent naming with persistent registries (v0.8.25+).
For the full list of changes, see the CHANGELOG.
Questions or issues? Open an issue at github.com/bradygaster/squad.

title: Build an autonomous agent
heading: Build an autonomous agent
text:
# Build an autonomous agent
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Build a CLI-wrapped autonomous agent pipeline that picks up tasks, coordinates work across teammates, and runs unattended.
Try this: Clone the autonomous-pipeline sample and run  to see the pattern in action.
This guide walks you through the pattern used by production autonomous agents — like a docs agent that monitors a repo for changes and generates documentation without human intervention.

title: Build an autonomous agent
heading: What an autonomous agent is
text:
An autonomous agent is a program that:
Receives a task (from a queue, a CLI argument, or a cron job)
Routes the task to the right agent based on role and skill match
Executes the work without waiting for human input
Records decisions and learnings for future runs
Reports results (cost, tokens, timeline)
In Squad, you build this by composing SDK primitives — , , , and  — into a loop that assigns work, collects results, and decides what to do next.

title: Build an autonomous agent
heading: Set up the project
text:
Create a new directory and initialize it with the Squad SDK dependency:
Create a :
Set  in your  and add scripts:

title: Build an autonomous agent
heading: Define your agents
text:
Use  to declare each agent's name, role, and capabilities:
Each  call validates the config at runtime and returns a typed  object.

title: Build an autonomous agent
heading: Build the squad
text:
Compose your agents into a squad with :
 validates every nested section through its respective builder — , , , . If any field is invalid, you get a  at startup, not at runtime.

title: Build an autonomous agent
heading: Create a CLI wrapper
text: Wrap your pipeline in a CLI entry point so you can invoke it from a terminal, cron job, or CI workflow:

title: Build an autonomous agent
heading: The autonomous pipeline pattern
text:
The core of an autonomous agent is a loop with four phases:
Assign — find the next queued task and match it to an available agent by role
Execute — the agent processes the task, streaming results and tracking cost
Coordinate — the agent may route follow-up tasks, record decisions, or save learnings
Repeat — continue until no tasks remain

title: Build an autonomous agent
heading: Coordination tools
text:
The autonomous-pipeline sample demonstrates three coordination patterns that agents use during the loop:
  Tool   What it does   Example  
 ------ ------------- --------- 
     Routes a follow-up task to a teammate   Developer finishes auth → routes test-writing to Tester  
     Records an architectural decision   "Use JWT with RS256 signing for auth"  
     Saves a learning for future sessions   "Connection pool sweet spot: 20 connections"  
These patterns let agents coordinate without a central orchestrator. Each agent makes local decisions that accumulate into a shared knowledge base.

text:
Track cost, token usage, and agent activity with the built-in  and OpenTelemetry integration:
To view traces and metrics in the .NET Aspire dashboard, see the Aspire dashboard scenario.

text: An extension is a reusable collection of skills, ceremonies, and directives that any team can install. It lives outside Squad core, packaged as a GitHub repository or marketplace plugin. Extensions let you codify workflows (the client-delivery pattern), domain expertise (Azure deployment strategies), or testing ceremonies that other teams benefit from.

title: Untitled
heading: Untitled
text:
Long-running agents face inevitable transient failures: rate limits, network timeouts, upstream service degradation. The circuit breaker pattern protects your agents from cascading failures by stopping requests before they fail, and automatically recovering when the system stabilizes.
The circuit breaker works out of the box. Squad includes sensible defaults for your agents. You only need to configure it if you want to customize thresholds or backoff timing. See Configuration for details.

title: Untitled
heading: The circuit breaker state machine
text:
The circuit breaker operates in three states:
  State   Behavior   Transition  
 ------- ---------- ------------ 
  CLOSED   Requests pass through normally   Open on threshold exceeded  
  OPEN   Requests fail fast without attempting   Half-open after backoff timeout  
  HALF-OPEN   Allow a single probe request to test recovery   Closed if probe succeeds; open if fails  
When failures exceed the configured threshold within a time window, the breaker opens to prevent further failing requests. After the backoff timeout, it enters half-open to test if the system has recovered. A successful probe closes the circuit; a failure re-opens it.

title: Untitled
heading: Exponential backoff strategy
text:
Instead of hammering a recovering service, the circuit breaker uses exponential backoff:
Initial: 2 minutes
Second attempt: 4 minutes
Third attempt: 8 minutes
Cap: 30 minutes
Each failed probe attempt resets the backoff. This gives flaky services time to recover without overwhelming them.

title: Untitled
heading: Configuration
text:
Squad includes sensible defaults — most agents won't need to change these. Configure the circuit breaker in your agent's  or initialization code only if you want to customize:
  Parameter   Default   Meaning  
 ----------- --------- --------- 
     5   Open circuit after this many failures  
     2   Close circuit after this many successes in half-open  
     60s   Count failures within this window  
     2m   Start backoff at this duration  
     30m   Cap backoff at this duration

title: Untitled
heading: Persistent state across restarts
text: The circuit breaker persists its state to disk. If an agent restarts while the circuit is open, it resumes from the same state — it won't immediately resume hammering a still-recovering service. This ensures resilience survives process restarts.

title: Untitled
heading: How to apply to custom agents
text:
When building a custom agent, wrap your external calls with circuit breaker protection:
When the circuit opens,  throws a  error. Your agent can catch this and backoff gracefully, or fail-fast to upstream callers.

title: Untitled
heading: Monitoring and observability
text:
Emit metrics on circuit breaker state changes:
Circuit opened: Alert on repeated failures
Circuit half-open: Monitor probe requests
Circuit closed: No action needed
Log state transitions and include the circuit state in agent status or dashboards. This helps you correlate agent degradation with downstream service issues.

title: Untitled
heading: See also
text:
PR #552 — Circuit breaker implementation
Failure handling — Error boundaries and fallbacks

title: Contributing to Squad
heading: Contributing to Squad
text:
# Contributing to Squad
Squad is an open-source project built by contributors who believe in democratizing multi-agent development. Whether you're fixing a bug, adding a feature, or improving documentation, your work helps the entire community.
This guide covers everything you need to know: from setting up your local environment to opening a pull request.

title: Contributing to Squad
heading: Prerequisites
text:
Before contributing, ensure you have:
Node.js ≥20.0.0
npm ≥10.0.0 (for workspace support)
Git with SSH agent (for package resolution)
gh CLI (for GitHub integration testing)

title: Contributing to Squad
heading: Monorepo Structure
text: Squad is an npm workspace monorepo with two packages:

title: Contributing to Squad
heading: Package Independence
text:
squad-sdk: Core runtime, agent orchestration, tool registry. No CLI dependencies.
squad-cli: Command-line interface. Depends on squad-sdk.
Each package has independent versioning via changesets. A change to squad-sdk may bump only squad-sdk; a change to CLI bumps only squad-cli.

Squad follows strict TypeScript conventions:
Type Safety: ,

title: Extensibility guide
heading: Extensibility guide
text:
# Extensibility guide

⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.

Where does your change idea belong? Squad core, marketplace plugin, or team config?

Key principle: Squad core stays small. Most ideas are skills, ceremonies, or directives.

title: Extensibility guide
heading: The three layers
text:
Layer   What lives here   Who changes it   Distribution  
 ------- ---------------- ---------------- -------------- 
  Squad Core   Coordinator behavior, routing logic, reviewer protocol   Squad maintainers only   npm releases  
  Squad Extension   Reusable patterns (skills, ceremonies, workflows)   Plugin authors   Marketplace plugins  
  Team Configuration   Decisions unique to THIS team   The team itself    files

title: Extensibility guide
heading: Decision tree
text: Heuristic: "Squad should..." → check if it's really "My team should..." or "Teams using X should...".

title: Extensibility guide
heading: Worked example: Client-delivery RFC
text:
RFC #328 proposed a sophisticated client-delivery workflow: discovery interviews, research sprints, multi-round review with // verdicts, evidence bundles.

The realization: It maps entirely to existing Squad primitives. No core changes needed.

Where it belongs: Layer 2 (Squad Extension)

This workflow is a reusable pattern any team could adopt — perfect as a marketplace plugin.

Plugin structure:


Usage:


Lesson: Most sophisticated workflows are compositions of primitives, not core features.

title: Extensibility guide
heading: When to escalate to core
text:
You likely need a core change if:

New coordinator mode — Example:  mode that runs checks before 
Routing logic change — Example: Route based on agent workload, not labels
Reviewer protocol change — Example: Conditional approvals ("approved if tests pass")
Global enforcement rule — Example: Block merges if evidence missing
Skill needs coordinator data — Example: Access to agent spawn history

You DON'T need core if:

Workflow pattern → Build a plugin (skills + ceremonies)
Domain expertise → Write a skill
Team process → Add a ceremony to 
Reusable templates → Build a plugin
Configuring existing behavior → Update

title: Extensibility guide
heading: Build an extension
text: Ready to build? See Building extensions for a five-minute walkthrough.

title: Extensibility guide
heading: Summary
text:
Start with the decision tree — Most ideas are Layer 2 or 3
Default to team config — Unique to your team? → 
Build a plugin if reusable — Other teams benefit? → Package and share
Escalate to core rarely — Need coordinator/routing changes? → Open an RFC

When in doubt: Start with team config. Copy-pasting to other teams? Promote to plugin. Plugins repeatedly hitting limits? Signal for core change.

title: Extensibility guide
heading: Related documentation
text:
Plugin Marketplace — How to browse, install, and share plugins
Skills — How to write skills for your team or plugins
Ceremonies — How to define team meetings and gates
Routing — How to configure work assignment rules
Building extensions — Step-by-step guide to building and sharing extensions
Contributing — How to propose changes to Squad core



Questions? Open an issue or join the discussion in the Squad community.

title: Frequently asked questions
heading: Frequently asked questions
text:
# Frequently asked questions
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Common questions, troubleshooting tips, and clarifications based on community feedback. Can't find your answer? Open an issue.

title: Frequently asked questions
heading: Which CLI should I use?
text:
Short answer: Use GitHub Copilot CLI for day-to-day work. Use Squad CLI for setup, diagnostics, and specific features.
Why GitHub Copilot CLI?
Full agent spawning capabilities
Access to all Squad features through natural conversation
Model selection and background execution
No manual commands — just describe what you need
When to use Squad CLI:
Initial setup: 
Diagnostics: 
Continuous triage: 
Aspire dashboard: 
Export/import:  and 
Remote phone access: 
Common workflow pattern:
For a detailed feature comparison, see Client Compatibility Matrix.

title: Frequently asked questions
heading: Why doesn't `gh issue edit --add-assignee "@copilot"` work?
text:
Problem: Running  (or variants like ) fails locally, even with a Personal Access Token.
Why this happens: The GitHub Copilot coding agent is a bot account. Bot accounts cannot be assigned to issues via the GitHub CLI in the same way as human users — the GitHub API restricts direct assignment of bot accounts through standard endpoints.
Recommended workaround: Use label-based assignment through the GitHub Actions workflow:
Add the  label to the issue:
   
The auto-assign workflow () detects the label and assigns @copilot automatically.
Prerequisites for auto-assign:
You must create a GitHub Classic Personal Access Token with  scope
Add it as a repository secret: 
The workflow uses this token to perform the assignment on your behalf
See Copilot Coding Agent for full setup instructions.

title: Frequently asked questions
heading: I don't see anything on the Aspire dashboard
text:
Problem: You ran  and opened the dashboard, but no telemetry is showing up.
Why this happens: The Aspire dashboard integration requires the Squad CLI. It is not available when using GitHub Copilot CLI directly.
How to fix:
Ensure you started Aspire with the Squad CLI:
   
Confirm the container is running:
   
Look for the dashboard URL in the output (usually )
Run a Squad CLI command that generates telemetry:
   
Refresh the Aspire dashboard — you should see traces, metrics, and logs appear
Note: GitHub Copilot CLI sessions do not send telemetry to Aspire. Only Squad CLI commands emit OpenTelemetry data to the dashboard.
See Using Squad with the Aspire Dashboard for details.

title: Frequently asked questions
heading: `squad doctor` complains about absolute path for teamRoot
text:
Problem: Running  shows a warning like:
Why this matters: Absolute paths (e.g.,  or ) break portability. If you share the squad with a teammate or clone it to a new machine, the absolute path won't resolve correctly.
How to fix: Make the  path relative to the project root.
Example — Before (absolute):
Example — After (relative):
For linked teams (dual-root mode):
If your project links to a remote team repository:
The path should be relative to your project root (where  or  lives), not to the  directory itself.
Verify the fix:
You should see  or no warning.

title: Frequently asked questions
heading: Can I use Squad CLI and GitHub Copilot CLI at the same time?
text:
Yes! They complement each other:
Squad CLI provides infrastructure: triage, Aspire observability, export/import, diagnostics
GitHub Copilot CLI provides conversational interface to your team
Recommended setup:
Run  in a dedicated terminal (or as a cron job / GitHub Action)
Use  (or  in VS Code) for all team interactions
Use  or  for diagnostics when needed
Both CLIs read and write the same  directory, so state stays synchronized.

title: Frequently asked questions
heading: What's the difference between Ralph and triage?
text:
Ralph and triage are different names for the same functionality:
 is the legacy command name
 is the new primary command name (as of v0.8.26)
Both commands do the same thing: monitor GitHub issues, apply routing rules, and assign work to team members
Migration path:
Existing scripts using  will continue to work (it's an alias)
New projects should use  in documentation and automation
The  directory in  remains unchanged for backward compatibility

title: Frequently asked questions
heading: How do I add a new agent to my squad?
text:
In conversation (recommended):
Squad will create the charter, update the team roster, and add routing rules.
Manual creation:
Create a charter file in 
Update  to include the new agent in the roster
Add routing rules in  (if applicable)
Optionally add a history file in 
See Team Setup for details.

title: Frequently asked questions
heading: What happens if I run `squad init` twice?
text:
Nothing breaks!  is idempotent — it's safe to run multiple times.
What it does:
Checks if  exists; if yes, does nothing
Copies missing templates to 
Updates  with Squad Actions (skips existing files)
Adds  if missing
Use cases:
Recover from partial initialization
Update workflows after a Squad upgrade
Add missing templates without overwriting custom changes

title: Frequently asked questions
heading: Can I use Squad without GitHub Issues?
text:
Yes, but with limitations.
What works without GitHub Issues:
Conversational team interaction (, )
Agent spawning and parallel execution
Memory, decisions, and knowledge sharing
Skills and ceremonies
Export/import for portability
What requires GitHub Issues:
Ralph/triage auto-assignment
Issue-driven development workflows
Project board integration
Label-based routing
Copilot coding agent auto-assignment
If you're using GitLab, see GitLab Issues for integration options.

title: Frequently asked questions
heading: How do I reset my squad without losing decisions?
text:
Option 1: Archive and start fresh
Manually copy decisions from the backup JSON or  if you archived it separately.
Option 2: Selective cleanup
See Disaster Recovery for more recovery patterns.

title: Frequently asked questions
heading: Where should I report bugs or request features?
text:
Open an issue on GitHub with:
Environment: OS, Node.js version, Squad version ()
Reproduction steps: What you ran, what happened, what you expected
Output: Copy the full terminal output, including any errors
For questions or discussions, use GitHub Discussions.

# GitHub auth setup for project boards
Try this to verify your auth setup:
Try this to switch accounts before working with project boards:
You need an authenticated GitHub CLI to manage project boards with Squad. This guide covers login, required scopes, multi-account switching, and common pitfalls.

title: GitHub auth setup for project boards
heading: Log in with `gh auth login`
text:
If you haven't authenticated yet, run:
Follow the interactive prompts. When asked about scopes, request the ones Squad needs for project board management (see the next section).

title: GitHub auth setup for project boards
heading: Required scopes
text:
Project board management requires specific token scopes. Without them,  commands fail silently or return permission errors.
  Scope   Why you need it  
 ------- ---------------- 
     Read and write access to repositories and their linked project boards  
     Read organization membership — required to access org-level project boards  
     Trigger and manage GitHub Actions workflows from Squad agents  
     Allow Squad to clean up temporary repos during testing (optional but recommended)  
     Create gists for sharing logs and snippets (optional but recommended)

title: GitHub auth setup for project boards
heading: EMU account reactivates between commands
text:
Problem: You switch to your personal account with , but subsequent commands run under your EMU account.
Why it happens: Some credential managers and enterprise policies can reactivate the EMU account. Background processes or IDE extensions may also trigger re-authentication with the work account.
Fix: Always verify with  immediately before running  or  commands. If the wrong account is active, switch again:

heading: Wrong account creates issues or PRs

Problem: Issues or PRs appear under your EMU username instead of your personal account.
Why it happens: The active  account changed between when you started work and when the PR was created.
Fix: Check  before every  or  command. Add a verification step to your workflow:

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: Your Personal Squad
text:
# Your Personal Squad
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this:
Your agents follow you everywhere now. Same team, every project, persistent memory.
This tutorial walks you through setup, explains what's happening behind the scenes, and shows a few ways personal squads make your work better.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: 1. What Is a Personal Squad?
text:
Normally, Squad lives inside a single project —  in your repo root. Your agents know that project. They don't know your other ones.
A personal squad flips that. Your team identity — agents, charters, skills, casting history — moves to your personal squad directory. Every project you work in can point to it.
Personal squad location by platform:
  Platform   Path  
 ---------- ------ 
  Linux     
  macOS     
  Windows     
What that means in practice:
Your agents remember your conventions across all your repos
Skills learned in one project carry over to every other one
Your Lead reviews code the same way everywhere
You don't repeat yourself when starting a new project
You're still one person. But your team travels with you.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: Install the CLI globally
text: Now  works from any directory. Not tied to a specific project.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: Initialize your personal squad
text:
You'll see:
That's it. You have a personal team root.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: Verify it's working
text: No agents yet. They form when you start your first session. The global directory is just the container — your team comes to life when you give them work.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: Connect a project
text:
Navigate to any project and run:
Squad detects your global team root and writes a pointer:
Your project now has its own  directory for local state, but your agents, skills, and casting come from the global root.
Repeat for any project you want connected.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: 3. What Just Happened? (Behind the Scenes)
text: Two things were created. Understanding the split is the key to personal squads.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: The global directory: your personal squad
text:
This is your team identity. It contains:
This is the stuff that makes your agents yours. It persists across sessions. It grows as you work. It follows you from project to project.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: The project pointer: `.squad/config.json`
text:
Inside each connected project,  looks like this:
That  field is the magic. When Squad's resolution system sees it, the project enters remote mode. Here's how the two modes compare:
    Local mode (default)   Remote mode (personal squad)  
 --- --- --- 
  Team identity    in project   Personal squad directory (global)  
  Decisions & logs    in project    in project  
  Agents shared?   No — project only   Yes — across all connected projects  
  Skills shared?   No   Yes  
In remote mode:
Team identity (agents, charters, skills, casting) → loaded from your personal squad directory
Project-local state (decisions, logs, orchestration-log) → stays in this project's 
The resolution system walks up directories looking for . When it finds one with a  in , it switches to remote mode — pulling team identity from the external path while keeping project state local.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: Why the split matters
text:
Your agents know you across projects. They remember your conventions. But each project has its own decisions and logs.
The API project doesn't get cluttered with the frontend project's session history. The Discord bot's architectural decisions stay in the Discord bot. Clean separation — shared brain, local memory.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: 4. Use Case: Your Side Projects Get a Team
text:
You have three side projects. A CLI tool, a web app, a Discord bot. All different stacks. All just you.
Now when you open any of them:
Fredo already knows you prefer PostgreSQL. He knows you use Zod for validation. He picked that up from working on your CLI tool last week. You didn't tell him again — he remembered.
Your Lead reviews code the same way in every repo. Your Tester follows the same patterns. Three projects, one consistent team.
Without a personal squad, you'd re-explain your conventions every time you start a new project. With one, the onboarding is already done.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: 5. Use Case: Code Review Across Repos
text:
Your Lead agent reviews every pull request in every project. But more than that — they remember architectural decisions from other repos.
Michael isn't just reviewing code in isolation. He's cross-referencing decisions and skills from your other projects. That's the personal squad at work — knowledge that spans repos.
The more projects you connect, the richer these reviews become. Your Lead builds a mental model of how you build software, not just how one project works.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: 6. Use Case: Learning a New Codebase
text:
You join an open-source project. New repo, unfamiliar code. But your agents already know you.
For projects you don't own — OSS contributions, client work, temporary collaborations — use consult mode. Your team consults invisibly, and the project never knows Squad was there:
Your personal squad is copied into the project's  directory, hidden via . The agents don't know the codebase yet — they'll learn it. But they already know your preferences:
How you like code structured
What testing patterns you follow
Your communication style (direct? exploratory? detail-oriented?)
Your agents explore the repo with your familiar voice. They ask the questions you'd ask. The codebase is new — but your relationship with your team isn't.
When you're done, extract the generic learnings back to your personal squad and clean up:
It's not a cold start. It's your team meeting a new project.
📖 Full guide: Consult Mode — invisible consulting, learning extraction, license handling.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: 7. Use Case: Automating Your Personal Workflow
text:
Your personal squad isn't just for writing code. It's a workflow tool.
Set a directive once:
That directive is now in your personal squad directory — every project, every session. Your agents enforce it everywhere. You set the standard once and it sticks.
Over time, your personal squad becomes an opinionated workflow engine. Not because you configured it that way — because you worked with it and it learned.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: 8. Use Case: Skills That Grow Everywhere
text:
Skills accumulate in your personal squad directory under . Every project contributes.
After a few weeks:
These came from different projects. The Zod skill was learned in your API. The Tailwind preference was captured in your web app. The rate-limiting skill came from a code review rejection.
Now every new project starts with all of that knowledge baked in. Your agents don't make the same mistakes twice — in any repo.
This is the long game. Early sessions feel similar to project-local squads. But after a few weeks of cross-project work, the difference is real. Your skills directory becomes a personal engineering handbook that your agents actually read.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: 9. Where It's Headed
text:
Honest moment: this is new. Personal squads work, but they're early.
What works well today:
Shared team identity across projects
Skills that accumulate and carry over
Consistent agent behavior everywhere you work
Consult mode — bring your team to projects you don't own, invisibly (docs)
What's still rough:
No sync mechanism between machines yet — your personal squad directory is local to your machine
Project keys aren't used for anything yet (that  in config.json)
No UI for browsing your global skills or agent histories (it's files for now)
We're building in the open. If something feels off, open an issue. If something feels right, we want to hear about that too.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: Tips
text:
Start with one project. Get comfortable with the personal squad on one repo before connecting others. The value compounds, but so does confusion if something's misconfigured.
Commit project  but not personal squad directory. The project-local state (decisions, logs) belongs in version control. Your global identity is personal — keep it out of repos.
Check status anytime.  shows your global squad directory and which projects are connected.
Skills are the payoff. The more projects you work across, the more skills accumulate. After a month, your agents have a real knowledge base tailored to how you build software.
It's just files. Your personal squad directory is a folder on your machine. You can browse it, edit it, back it up, copy it to another machine manually. No magic, no cloud, no lock-in.
Global install matters.  gives you the  command everywhere. Without it, you'd need  in each project. Global CLI + global squad = full portability.

title: Your Personal Squad
heading: What to Try Next
text:
Your First Session — The full walkthrough from zero to fan-out
Solo Dev Scenario — Squad for one-person teams
Tips & Tricks — Patterns that work

title: Interactive Shell Guide
heading: Interactive Shell Guide
text:
# Interactive Shell Guide
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
💡 Recommended: Use GitHub Copilot CLI — The best way to interact with Squad today is through the GitHub Copilot CLI. Run  to start a session with your team. The Copilot CLI provides the richest agent experience, including tool use, parallel execution, and full MCP integration.
The Squad interactive shell gives you a persistent connection to your team.Instead of spawning short-lived CLI invocations, the shell maintains a real-time session where you can talk to agents, issue commands, and watch work happen.

title: Interactive Shell Guide

text:
You type a message → Shell receives it

Agents launch in parallel → All applicable agents work simultaneously

title: Interactive Shell Guide
heading: Parallel Execution
text:
When you give a task that multiple agents can handle:
The coordinator might spawn:
McManus (frontend) → building the UI
Verbal (backend) → setting up auth endpoints
Fenster (tester) → writing test cases
Kobayashi (scribe) → logging everything
All at once. All in parallel.

title: Interactive Shell Guide
heading: What Is a Session?
text:
Each agent gets a persistent session — a long-lived context where it remembers:
The task you gave it
What it's already written to disk
Previous decisions and learnings
Its own knowledge base (charter, history)
Sessions survive crashes. If an agent dies mid-work, it resumes from the exact checkpoint.
