title: Tips & Tricks
heading: Tips & Tricks
text:
# Tips & Tricks
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this:
Patterns that make Squad click. Skim the headers, steal what's useful.

title: Tips & Tricks
heading: The Big Three
text:
Say "team" for parallel work. The word "team" triggers fan-out — frontend, backend, testing, all at once.
Name an agent for focused work.  sends work to one specific agent. No team overhead.
Set rules early. First session, drop your conventions into a directive. Agents read  before every task — you only say things once.

title: Tips & Tricks
heading: Write Better Prompts
text: Be specific about scope. Tell the team what's in, what's out, what's next. Use bullet points for multi-part tasks — agents process lists better than paragraphs.

title: Tips & Tricks
heading: Direct vs Team vs General
text:
When   Do this   Example  
 ------ --------- --------- 
  Parallel/cross-functional   Say "Team"     
  Sequential/specialized   Name the agent     
  Don't care who   Just describe it

title: Tips & Tricks
heading: Parallel Work — Let It Cook
text:
Don't interrupt parallel work. Squad agents chain automatically — the tester catches failures, the backend fixes them, the tester re-runs. If you jump in after 2 minutes, you break the chain.
When they're done, ask Scribe:

title: Tips & Tricks
heading: Ralph — Your Work Monitor
text:
Got a backlog? Let Ralph handle it while you focus on the critical path.
Ralph triages issues, assigns them, spawns agents, and reports every 3-5 rounds. Say  to stop.
The  workflow runs Ralph on event-based triggers (issue close, PR merge, manual dispatch).

title: Tips & Tricks
heading: Decisions & Memory
text:
Set permanent rules:  → goes to 
Capture lessons:  → agents remember forever
Check alignment: When agents disagree, the decision is probably missing. Add it.
Commit  — it's your team's brain. Anyone who clones gets the full team.

title: Tips & Tricks
heading: Common Pitfalls
text:
Pitfall   Fix  
 --------- ----- 
  Vague prompt → agents ask questions   Be specific about scope upfront  
  Interrupting parallel work   Let it finish, then review  
  Contradicting old decisions   Ask Scribe to remind you of rules  
  Not using Ralph on a full backlog    — let the bot grind  
  Too many agents   Start with 4-5, add specialists later  
  Lost team knowledge   Commit  to git

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Squad — Product Guide
text: # Squad — Product Guide

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: What is Squad?
text:
Squad gives you an AI development team through GitHub Copilot. You describe what you're building. Squad proposes a team of specialists — lead, frontend, backend, tester — that live in your repo as files. Each agent runs in its own context window, reads its own knowledge, and writes back what it learned. They persist across sessions, share decisions, and get better the more you use them.
It is not a chatbot wearing hats. Each team member is spawned as a real sub-agent with its own tools, its own memory, and its own area of expertise.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Which CLI should I use?
text:
Use GitHub Copilot CLI for day-to-day work. It's the recommended interface for interacting with your Squad — full agent spawning, model selection, and conversational access to all features.
Use Squad CLI for setup and operations:
Initial setup: 
Build from config: 
Diagnostics: 
Interactive shell: 
Continuous triage: 
Watch mode: 
Aspire dashboard: 
Export/import:  and 
Plugin management: 
Common workflow:
Both CLIs read and write the same  directory, so state stays synchronized. For more details, see FAQ: Which CLI should I use? and Client Compatibility Matrix.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Supported platforms
text:
Squad works across multiple interfaces — GitHub Copilot CLI, VS Code, Squad CLI, SDK, and the Copilot Coding Agent. Pick the one that fits your workflow:
GitHub Copilot CLI — Day-to-day conversational work with your squad (recommended)
VS Code — Same experience, editor-integrated
Squad CLI — Setup, diagnostics, monitoring (, , )
SDK — Build tools on top of Squad with 
Copilot Coding Agent — Autonomous issue processing via 
Multi-platform support: Squad also works with Azure DevOps (work items, PRs via /), GitLab Issues, and Microsoft Planner through pluggable platform adapters. See Enterprise Platforms for details.
Not sure which to use? See Choose your interface for a complete comparison and decision tree.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Installation
text:
Requirements:
Node.js 20+ (LTS)
GitHub Copilot (CLI, VS Code, Visual Studio, or Coding Agent)
A git repository (Squad stores team state in )
 CLI — required for GitHub Issues, PRs, Ralph, and Project Boards (install)
Running  creates the  directory structure, copies  into , and installs GitHub Actions workflows into . Your team is created at runtime when you first talk to Squad.
Note: When you select Squad from the agent picker, you'll see the version number in the name (e.g., "Squad (v0.8.25)"). This helps you confirm which version is installed.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: GitHub CLI authentication
text:
Squad uses the  CLI for all GitHub API operations — issues, PRs, labels, project boards, and Ralph's work monitoring. You must authenticate before using any of these features.
Quick start:
Choose GitHub.com, HTTPS, and authenticate with your browser or a Personal Access Token (PAT Classic).
Verify it worked:
Additional scopes — some features require scopes beyond the default:
  Feature   Required scope   Command  
 --------- --------------- --------- 
  Issues, PRs, Ralph    (included by default)   —  
  Project Boards        
The  command adds scopes to your existing token — it takes about 10 seconds and you only need to do it once.
Troubleshooting:
"gh: command not found" — Install the GitHub CLI from https://cli.github.com/
"HTTP 401" or "authentication required" — Run  to re-authenticate
Project board commands fail — Run  to add the  scope
"Resource not accessible by integration" — Your token may lack the  scope. Re-authenticate with a PAT Classic that has  and  scopes

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: How teams form (init mode)
text:
When you open Copilot and select Squad for the first time in a repo, there's no team yet. Squad enters Init Mode:
Squad identifies you via  and uses your name in conversation.
You describe your project — language, stack, what it does.
Squad casts a team — agents get names from a single fictional universe (e.g., Apollo 13 / NASA Mission Control, The Usual Suspects, Ocean's Eleven). The universe is selected deterministically based on team size, project shape, and what's been used before. Names are persistent identifiers — they don't change the agent's behavior or voice.
Squad proposes the team:
You confirm — say "yes", adjust roles, add someone, or just give a task (which counts as implicit yes).
Squad then creates the  directory structure with charters, histories, routing rules, casting state, and ceremony config. Each agent's  is seeded with your project description and tech stack so they have day-1 context.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: What gets created
text: Commit this folder. Anyone who clones your repo gets the team — with all their accumulated knowledge.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Talking to your team (routing)
text: How you phrase your message determines who works on it.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Name an agent directly
text: Squad spawns FLIGHT specifically.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Say "team" for parallel fan-out
text: Squad spawns multiple agents simultaneously — frontend builds the UI, backend sets up endpoints, tester writes test cases from the spec, all at once.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: General requests
text: Squad checks , picks the best match, and may launch anticipatory agents (e.g., tester writes validation test cases while the implementer builds).

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Quick questions — no spawn
text: Squad answers directly without spawning an agent.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Example prompts to try
text:
You say   What happens  
 --------- ------------- 
     RETRO (Frontend) scaffolds the project  
     Multiple agents launch in parallel  
     Squad gives a quick status from recent logs  
     Lead facilitates a retrospective ceremony  
     A new agent joins, named from the same universe  
     Captured as a directive to

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Response modes
text:
Squad automatically picks the right response speed based on your request complexity. Direct answers take seconds, full agent spawns take longer but deliver deeper reasoning and parallel work. You don't control the mode — Squad routes based on what the task needs.
→ Full guide: Response Modes

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: SDK-first mode
text:
Define your team in TypeScript instead of maintaining markdown files manually. Write a  with type-safe builder functions, and  generates the  governance markdown.
Get started:
Builder functions: , , , , , , , , .
→ Full guide: SDK-First Mode

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Casting system
text:
Squad names agents from fictional universes — Apollo 13 / NASA Mission Control (the default), The Usual Suspects, Breaking Bad, Star Trek, and others. The universe is selected deterministically based on team size and project shape.
Casting is persistent — once an agent receives a name, it keeps that name across sessions. The casting registry lives in . You control which universes are available through a policy allowlist and can set per-universe capacity limits.
In SDK-first mode, configure casting with :
When a universe runs out of names, the overflow strategy determines what happens:  (error),  (use a functional name), or  (move to the next universe).

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Skills system
text:
Skills are reusable knowledge patterns that agents load on demand. They live in  and teach agents how to handle specific tasks — branching workflows, deployment strategies, testing patterns, or domain expertise.
Skills have a confidence lifecycle:  →  → , and track their source:  (you wrote it),  (agent saw a pattern),  (validated through use), or  (imported from another project).
In SDK-first mode, define skills with :
Skills accumulate as you work. After a few sessions, your team has a knowledge base tailored to your codebase.
→ Full guide: Skills

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Ceremonies
text:
Ceremonies are structured team meetings. Squad ships with two default ceremonies — Design Review (triggers before multi-agent work) and Retrospective (triggers after failures). You can trigger ceremonies manually, create custom ones, or disable them. Configuration lives in .
In SDK-first mode, define ceremonies with :
→ Full guide: Ceremonies

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Ralph — work monitor
text:
Ralph triages your issue backlog, assigns work to agents, and keeps the board moving. Activate Ralph when you have open issues, and he reports every 3–5 rounds.
CLI commands:
 — run a single triage pass
 — continuous triage every 10 minutes
 — Ralph watchdog mode (monitors and auto-restarts)
The  workflow runs Ralph on a schedule — your squad triages issues between sessions.
Note:  is a legacy alias. New projects should use .
→ Full guide: Ralph — Work Monitor

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Memory system
text:
Squad's memory is layered — personal agent histories, shared team decisions, and reusable skills. Knowledge compounds over sessions. After a few sessions, agents stop asking questions they've already answered. Mature projects carry full architecture knowledge and decision history.
→ Full guide: Memory System

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Plugin marketplace
text:
Extend your squad with community plugins — reusable collections of skills, ceremonies, and directives.
Plugins let you add domain expertise (Azure infrastructure patterns), workflow templates (client-delivery processes), or testing ceremonies without modifying Squad core. Build your own and share them.
→ Full guide: Plugins   Marketplace

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: SubSquads (streams)
text:
Break large teams into focused SubSquads — smaller groups that work independently on different features or domains. SubSquads maintain their own routing and task queues while sharing the parent squad's decisions and memory.
→ Full guide: Streams

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Export and import
text:
Export creates a portable snapshot of your entire team — agents, knowledge, skills. Import brings that snapshot into another repo. Squad handles collision detection and splits imported knowledge into portable learnings and project-specific context automatically.
→ Full guide: Export and Import

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: GitHub Issues mode
text:
Squad integrates with GitHub Issues for issue-driven development. Connect to a repo, view the backlog, assign issues to agents, and Squad handles branch creation, implementation, PR creation, and review feedback. Agents link work to issues automatically.
→ Full guide: GitHub Issues Mode

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: PRD mode
text:
Paste your product requirements document directly into Squad. The Lead agent decomposes the spec into discrete work items, assigns them to the right agents, and the team works in parallel. Specs become trackable tasks automatically.
→ Full guide: PRD Mode

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Human team members
text:
Not every team member needs to be an AI agent. Add humans to the roster for decisions that require a real person — design sign-off, security review, product approval. Squad pauses when work is routed to a human and reminds you if they haven't responded.
→ Full guide: Human Team Members

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Notifications
text:
Your squad can notify you when they need input — send instant pings to Teams, Discord, iMessage, or any webhook. Agents trigger notifications when they're blocked, need a decision, hit an error, or complete important work.
Setup is quick: Configure an MCP notification server (takes 5 minutes), and agents automatically know when to ping you.
See Notifications Guide for platform-specific setup and examples. For MCP configuration details, see MCP Setup Guide.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Multi-platform support
text:
Squad works with more than GitHub. Pluggable platform adapters let you use:
GitHub — Issues, PRs, Project Boards (via  CLI)
Azure DevOps — Work items, repos, PRs (via / CLI)
GitLab — Issues and merge requests
Microsoft Planner — Hybrid work-item tracking (via Microsoft Graph API)
Configure cross-project ADO support in  — work items can live in a different org/project than the repo.
→ Full guide: Enterprise Platforms   GitLab Issues

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Upgrading
text:
Already have Squad installed? Update to the latest version:
Run  to validate your setup after upgrading:
Doctor runs 9 checks — Node.js version,  CLI auth,  directory structure, team state, and more. It reports issues with clear fix instructions.
Migrating from  to :
This renames  to  and updates all internal references.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Context budget
text:
Each agent runs in its own context window. Real numbers:
  What   Tokens   % of 200K window  
 ------ -------- ------------------- 
  Coordinator (squad.agent.md)   13,200   6.6%  
  Agent at Week 1 (charter + seed history + decisions)   1,250   0.6%  
  Agent at Week 4 (+ 15 learnings, 8 decisions)   3,300   1.7%  
  Agent at Week 12 (+ 50 learnings, 47 decisions)   9,000   4.5%  
  Remaining for actual work   187,000   93%+  
The coordinator uses 6.6% of its window. A 12-week veteran agent uses 4.5% — but in its own window, not yours. Fan out to 5 agents and you get 1M tokens of total reasoning capacity across all windows.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Known limitations
text:
Experimental — file formats and APIs may change between versions.
Silent success bug — approximately 7–10% of background agent spawns complete all their file writes but return no text response. This is a platform-level issue. Squad detects it by checking the filesystem for work product and reports what it finds. Work is not lost.
Platform latency — response times depend on the Copilot platform. Complex multi-agent tasks take 40–60 seconds. Simple questions are answered in 2–3 seconds.
Node 20+ — requires a Node.js LTS release (v20.0.0 or later).
GitHub Copilot required — Squad works across Copilot hosts (CLI, VS Code, Visual Studio, Coding Agent).
First session is the least capable — agents improve as they accumulate history. Give it a few sessions before judging.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Adding
text: Squad allocates a name from the current universe, generates a charter and history seeded with project context, and adds them to the roster. Immediately productive.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Removing
text: Agents are never deleted. Their charter and history move to . Knowledge is preserved. If you need them back later, they remember everything.

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: Reviewer protocol
text:
Agents with review authority can reject work. On rejection, the original author is locked out and a different agent must handle the revision. This prevents the common failure mode where an agent keeps fixing its own work in circles.
→ Full guide: Reviewer Protocol

title: Squad — Product Guide
heading: File ownership
text:
Squad maintains a clear ownership model:
  What   Owner   Safe to edit?  
 ------ ------- -------------- 
     Squad (overwritten on upgrade)   No — your changes will be lost  
     You and your team   Yes — this is your team's state  
     You   Yes — your SDK-first config  
  Everything else   You   Yes

title: Insider Program
heading: Insider Program
text:
# Insider Program
Get early access to Squad development builds and shape the future of the project.

title: Insider Program
heading: What Is the Insider Program?
text:
The Insider Program gives you continuous access to development builds of Squad. Insiders run code from the  branch — the bleeding edge where new features land first. It's a lightweight, honor-system program designed for developers who want to:
Try new features early — Before they ship in a release
Help catch bugs — Report issues before they reach stable versions
Shape the roadmap — Your feedback directly influences what we build next
Move fast — No waiting for monthly releases; updates flow as commits land

title: Insider Program
heading: Upgrade Existing Repo to Insider
text: This updates Squad-owned files (, workflows, templates) to the latest insider build. Your  team state (agents, decisions, casting, history) is always preserved.

title: Insider Program
heading: You'll Get
text:
Continuous updates — The insider branch is always ahead of 
New features — Preview functionality months before stable releases
Direct access — Pull latest with a single command
Community input — Your feedback shapes prioritization

title: Insider Program
heading: You Might Hit
text:
Rough edges — Features may not be fully polished
Occasional bugs — Development builds are less tested than releases
Breaking changes — API surface may shift between insider versions
Missing documentation — New features may not have guides yet
This is expected. The insider program trades stability for speed.

title: Insider Program
heading: Version Format
text:
Insider builds use this version scheme:
Where:
 — The semver for the upcoming release
 — Insider build flag
 — Commit hash (first 8 chars)
You'll see this in your  HTML version comment:
Pin a specific tagged version:

title: Insider Program
heading: Reporting Issues
text:
Found a bug? We want to hear about it.
Open a GitHub issue with:
Version — Full version from your 
What happened — Clear description of the bug
Steps to reproduce — Exact steps to trigger it
Environment — CLI or VS Code, Node version, OS
Label it with  so we can track insider-specific issues.
Example:

title: Insider Program
heading: Opting Out
text:
Want to go back to stable releases?
This installs the latest stable version. Your  state is safe — it'll work with any version.

title: Insider Program
heading: Q: Will insider builds break my project?
text: A: Unlikely, but possible. The insider branch is tested before pushing, but it's less stable than releases. Make sure you can roll back if needed.

title: Insider Program
heading: Q: Can I switch between insider and stable builds?
text: A: Yes. Insider builds are backward compatible with stable installs. Your  directory works with any version.

title: Insider Program
heading: Q: How often do insider builds update?
text: A: As often as commits land on the  branch. Could be daily, could be weekly — depends on the dev cycle. Run  again to fetch the latest.

title: Insider Program
heading: Q: Will my team state be preserved?
text: A: Yes.  is never overwritten on upgrade. All your agents, decisions, and histories are safe.

title: Insider Program
heading: Q: What if an insider build has a bad bug?
text:
A: Roll back immediately:
Then report the issue.

title: Insider Program
heading: Thank You
text:
Insiders help us ship better software. Your bug reports, feature requests, and feedback make Squad stronger. Thank you for being part of the journey.
Have questions? Start a discussion.

title: Glossary
heading: Glossary
text:
# Glossary

⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.

Key terms defined in one sentence each. Alphabetical order.



Agent — A specialist AI team member with a role, charter, and persistent memory that handles specific types of work.

Casting — The process of forming your team by proposing agents, confirming roles, and writing their charters to .

Ceremony — A scheduled team event like retrospectives, reviews, or planning sessions defined in .

Coordinator — Squad's routing engine that reads your request, checks routing rules, and spawns the right agents.

Decisions — Architectural choices, conventions, and directives captured in  that all agents read before working.

Directive — A persistent rule or convention you give the team (like "Always use Zod for validation") that gets written to .

History — Each agent's memory of past work, stored in  and read before every spawn.

Memory — All persistent team state stored in the  directory, including roster, routing rules, decisions, and agent histories.

Ralph — The silent work monitor agent that watches your GitHub or GitLab issues and tracks work in progress.

Routing — Rules in  that define which agent handles which type of work, read by the coordinator before spawning.

Scribe — The silent agent that tracks decisions and logs sessions, merging proposals from all agents into .

Skill — A reusable capability stored in  that agents can learn and execute.

Spawn — The act of starting an agent as an independent subprocess with its own context window, tools, and memory.

Squad — Your AI development team, coordinated through the Squad framework.

.squad/ directory — The root directory containing all team state: roster, routing, decisions, agent charters and histories, and ceremony config.

Team — The collection of agents working on your project, defined in .

title: VS Code Troubleshooting
heading: VS Code Troubleshooting
text:
# VS Code Troubleshooting
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
This page covers known issues and mitigations when running Squad inside VS Code's integrated terminal.

title: VS Code Troubleshooting
heading: Symptom: VS Code closes unexpectedly during Squad execution
text:
Reported in: Issue #259, Discussion #174
VS Code (especially Insiders/Nightly builds) may close without a crash dialog while Squad tasks are executing. This is most often caused by resource pressure — not a bug in Squad itself — but Squad's runtime patterns can contribute to the problem.

title: VS Code Troubleshooting
heading: Root cause analysis
text: An audit of the Squad codebase (SDK and CLI) identified the following resource-pressure vectors:

title: VS Code Troubleshooting
heading: 1. Unbounded in-memory collections (fixed)
text:
Streaming usage history () and telemetry queue () previously grew without limit during long-running sessions. In multi-agent sessions with heavy token throughput, these arrays could accumulate tens of thousands of entries over hours.
Fix applied: Both collections now enforce FIFO eviction caps — 1,000 events for usage history and 500 for telemetry. This bounds worst-case memory contribution to a predictable ceiling.

title: VS Code Troubleshooting
heading: 2. File watcher scope (fixed)
text:
The  watches the  directory with . On projects with large orchestration logs, this could generate a high volume of  events. Combined with VS Code's own file watchers on the same workspace, the total watcher count can approach OS limits.
Fix applied: The observer now filters out  and  subdirectories at the watcher callback level, reducing event volume significantly.

title: VS Code Troubleshooting
heading: 3. Stream buffer accumulation (already mitigated)
text: The CLI shell accumulates per-agent streaming content in  buffers. The existing  enforces a 1 MB per-stream cap and trims the active message list to 200 entries. No additional changes needed.

title: VS Code Troubleshooting
heading: 4. Process spawning (no issues found)
text: All child processes ( for Copilot, , Docker, .NET) are properly tracked and cleaned up via signal handlers (, ). No process leak patterns were detected.

title: VS Code Troubleshooting
heading: 5. Terminal output rate (low risk)
text: Squad uses the Ink framework for rendering, which batches React state updates. Direct  calls are rate-limited by the model's token generation speed. No fire-hose output patterns were found.

title: VS Code Troubleshooting
heading: 6. Synchronous I/O (startup only)
text: and  calls exist in config loading and shell initialization, but these run only at startup — not in hot paths or event loops.

title: VS Code Troubleshooting
heading: For users
text:
Use VS Code Stable instead of Insiders/Nightly. Nightly builds may have unresolved memory or renderer bugs that amplify resource pressure from terminal-heavy workloads.
Increase the file watcher limit (Linux/macOS):
   
Exclude  from VS Code's file watcher by adding to :
   
Monitor resource usage during Squad sessions:
   - Windows: Task Manager → Details tab, watch  memory
   - macOS/Linux:  or Activity Monitor
   - If memory climbs steadily past 1 GB, restart the Squad shell
Close unused terminals in VS Code before starting a Squad session. Each terminal consumes renderer memory.
Disable terminal GPU acceleration if you see flickering (related: #254):

title: VS Code Troubleshooting
heading: For developers
text:
When adding new in-memory collections, enforce size caps with FIFO eviction (see  pattern).
When adding file watchers, always filter high-churn directories in the callback.
Prefer  file I/O in any code path that runs during active sessions.
Test long-running sessions (2+ hours) to verify memory stays bounded.

title: VS Code Troubleshooting
heading: Related issues
text:
Issue   Description   Status  
 ------- ------------- -------- 
  #259   VS Code crash during Squad execution   This investigation  
  #254   Terminal flicker in VS Code   Open  
  Discussion #174   Original crash report by @diberry   Open

title: Using Squad with the Aspire Dashboard
heading: Using Squad with the Aspire Dashboard
text:
# Using Squad with the Aspire Dashboard
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
📌 Squad CLI only — The Aspire dashboard integration requires the Squad CLI (). It is not available when using GitHub Copilot CLI directly. Only Squad CLI commands emit OpenTelemetry data to the dashboard.
Try this:
Aspire is a free, open-source dashboard for observing any OpenTelemetry app — traces, metrics, logs, all in one place. Squad ships with an Aspire integration that streams all your telemetry (agent spawns, token usage, session metrics, errors) to the dashboard in real time.

title: Using Squad with the Aspire Dashboard
heading: 1. What Is Aspire?
text:
Aspire is not a .NET thing — it's a standalone dashboard for any app that speaks OpenTelemetry. You can run it in Docker, point any OTLP client at it, and watch telemetry flow in:
Traces — see every agent spawn, task execution, and error with timing
Metrics — counters (agents spawned, tokens consumed), histograms (latency), gauges (active sessions)
Resources — grouping by service and environment
Squad's OTel integration exports OTLP/gRPC (the only protocol Aspire understands), so you get instant visibility into what your agents are doing.

title: Using Squad with the Aspire Dashboard
heading: 2. Launch the Aspire Container
text:
The easiest way is the built-in  command:
This will:
Pull the Aspire dashboard image: 
Start the container on port 18888 (UI) and port 4317 (OTLP gRPC)
Print the dashboard URL (usually )
Behind the scenes, the container runs with:
⚠️ Both  flags are required for local dev. Without , the OTLP endpoint rejects connections with: . Without , the UI requires a login token.
If you started the container yourself (without ) and you're seeing auth errors, stop it and re-run with the flags above. Or, if you prefer to keep API key auth, set a key on both sides:
For local dev, unsecured mode is simplest. For shared environments, use an API key.

title: Using Squad with the Aspire Dashboard
heading: 3. Connect Squad to Aspire
text: When you run Squad (via the CLI or SDK), set the OTLP endpoint:

text:
That's it. Squad will automatically:
Initialize OpenTelemetry providers (tracing + metrics)
Export all agent spawns, token usage, session metrics, and errors to Aspire
Flush telemetry on shutdown

title: Using Squad with the Aspire Dashboard

You'll see a list of spans for each operation. Examples:
Each span has attributes:

Click a span to see full details (attributes, events, timing).

title: Using Squad with the Aspire Dashboard

You'll see gauges, counters, and histograms:
Counters:

Gauges:

Histograms:

Is the container running?

Is OTLP auth disabled?

Is the protocol correct?

Firewall / network:
   Port 4317 must be reachable between your app and the Docker host. On Docker Desktop (Windows/Mac),  should work.
Wait for batching:
   OTel batches span exports. Traces may take 1–2 seconds to appear. Metrics export every 30 seconds by default (set  for faster feedback).

title: Squad in CI/CD Pipelines
heading: Squad in CI/CD Pipelines
text:
# Squad in CI/CD Pipelines
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this:
Ralph runs periodically via GitHub Actions to handle housekeeping between Copilot sessions — triage new issues, apply squad labels, check stale branches, archive old decisions.

title: Squad in CI/CD Pipelines
heading: 1. The Heartbeat Workflow — Ralph Between Sessions
text:
Ralph (the manager agent) runs via GitHub Actions on event-based triggers:
Triage new issues
Apply squad labels based on routing rules
Check for stale branches
Archive old decisions
The workflow is in  and runs on issue close, PR merge, and manual dispatch (or via  for local polling).
You don't have to do anything — it's installed automatically (along with 9 other workflows) when you run .
⚡ Cron is permanently disabled. Scheduled cron jobs are no longer supported in GitHub Actions to reduce costs. The heartbeat workflow runs on event-based triggers: when issues are closed, PRs are merged, or you manually trigger via . For periodic polling without events, use  in a separate terminal (local, no GitHub Actions cost).
Ralph reads , looks at open issues, and applies labels:
Now when you open Copilot, you see issues pre-triaged.

title: Squad in CI/CD Pipelines
heading: 2. Label-Driven Automation
text:
Squad uses GitHub labels to drive workflows:
Routing labels (auto-applied by Ralph):
 — routed to Neo (Lead)
 — routed to Trinity (Frontend Dev)
 — routed to Morpheus (Backend Dev)
 — routed to Tank (Tester)
Control labels:
 — tells Copilot to auto-assign this issue to Neo
 — auto-assign to Trinity
 — auto-assign to Morpheus
 — auto-assign to Tank
Type labels (for filtering):
Priority labels:
Release labels:
 — include in the next release
 — not scheduled yet
Ralph applies  and  labels automatically. You apply  labels manually when you want autonomous processing.

title: Squad in CI/CD Pipelines
heading: 3. @copilot Auto-Assign for Autonomous Issue Processing
text:
When you add a  label to an issue, the  automation picks it up:
Ralph labels issue #42 with  (backend work)
You review the issue and add  (approval to proceed)
GitHub Actions triggers the  workflow
Copilot session spawns Morpheus to handle the issue
Morpheus reads the issue, implements the feature, opens a PR
PR is tagged for human review
This is autonomous issue processing. You don't open Copilot manually — the workflow does.
Workflow file: :
Note: This workflow requires GitHub Actions to have access to your Copilot session. See GitHub's docs for  in Actions.

title: Squad in CI/CD Pipelines
heading: 4. What You CAN'T Do: Agents Can't Run in Actions (Yet)
text:
Squad agents require a live Copilot session. They can't run in a GitHub Actions runner without Copilot CLI access.
This means:
❌ You can't run  inside a GitHub Actions workflow  
✅ You can use Ralph to triage and label issues  
✅ You can trigger Copilot sessions via Actions (if you have  access)  
❌ You can't have agents autonomously merge PRs without human approval (by design)

title: Squad in CI/CD Pipelines
heading: 5. Sample Workflow: Issue Filed → Triage → Assign → Build → Review
text:
User files issue #42: "Add Stripe payment integration"
Ralph (heartbeat) runs, reads routing rules, applies  and 
You review the issue, decide it's good, add  label
GitHub Actions triggers Copilot auto-assign workflow
Copilot spawns Morpheus to handle issue #42
Morpheus builds the Stripe integration, writes tests, opens PR #43
Neo (Lead) reviews PR #43, approves or requests changes
You merge PR #43 after human review
Steps 2, 4, 5, 6, 7 are automated. You only do steps 3 and 8.

title: Squad in CI/CD Pipelines
heading: 6. Workflow Templates Ship with Squad
text:
When you run , these workflow templates are installed:
 → Ralph runs every 6 hours
 → Triggers Copilot on  labels
 → Reminds you of open PRs needing review
To activate them:
Now they're live.

title: Squad in CI/CD Pipelines
heading: 7. Sample Prompts for CI-Adjacent Workflows
text:
Trigger Ralph manually:
Check what Ralph would do (dry run):
Have agents work on labeled issues:
Autonomous issue pickup:

title: Squad in CI/CD Pipelines
heading: Tips
text:
Ralph is your assistant between sessions. It triages issues, applies labels, and keeps things organized while you're not in Copilot.
 labels mean "approved to proceed." Don't add them to every issue — only the ones you've reviewed and want agents to handle autonomously.
Agents still need human review. PRs created by agents should be reviewed by a human before merging.
Workflows are templates. Customize  to match your CI/CD setup, then copy to .
Heartbeat frequency is configurable. Edit  to change from every 6 hours to daily, hourly, etc.

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
text:
# Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
Quick answer: Not sure which interface to use? See Choose your interface for a concise decision tree and comparison.
Squad runs on multiple Copilot surfaces — each with its own agent spawning mechanism, tool set, and constraints. This document maps Squad's core capabilities across CLI, VS Code, JetBrains, and GitHub.com to help you understand what works where.

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Quick Reference
text:
Feature   CLI   VS Code   JetBrains   GitHub.com  
 --------- ----- --------- ----------- ----------- 
  Sub-agent spawning   ✅  tool   ✅  /    ⚠️ Limited   ❌ Not available  
  Agent type selection   ✅ Full (, , , )   ✅ Custom agents   ⚠️ Limited   ❌ Not available  
  Per-spawn model selection   ✅ Dynamic (4-layer hierarchy)   ⚠️ Static (custom agent frontmatter)   ?   ?  
  Background/async execution   ✅  (fire-and-forget)   ⚠️ Sync only (parallel concurrent)   ?   ?  
  Parallel fan-out   ✅ Background tasks +    ✅ Multiple subagents in one turn   ?   ?  
  File discovery (.github/agents/)   ✅ Automatic   ✅ Automatic   ?   ?  
   file access (read)   ✅ Full   ✅ Full (workspace-scoped)   ?   ?  
   file access (write)   ✅ Full   ✅ Full (with approval prompt)   ?   ?  
  SQL tool   ✅ Available   ❌ Not available   ❌ Not available   ❌ Not available  
  MCP server access   ✅ Full   ✅ Full (inherited)   ⚠️ Limited   ⚠️ Limited  
Legend:
✅ Works — Feature is fully supported with no degradation
⚠️ Limited — Feature works with constraints, degraded experience, or special configuration
❌ Not available — Feature cannot be used on this surface
? Untested — Not yet validated in production

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: CLI (Copilot CLI)
text: Squad's primary platform. All features are fully supported.

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Agent Spawning
text:
Tool: 
Parameters: , , , , 
Agent types:
  -  — Full tool access (file ops, CLI, SQL, web, GitHub MCP)
  -  — Read-only tools (grep, glob, view) — optimized for speed and cost
  -  — CLI tools + Haiku model (rarely used by Squad)
  -  — Investigation tools (available but Squad uses its own reviewer pattern)

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Model Selection
text:
Mechanism: Per-spawn  parameter
Dynamic: Yes — each spawn can use a different model
4-layer hierarchy:
  1. User override ("use opus")
  2. Agent charter preference ( section)
  3. Task-aware auto-select (cost-first: haiku for docs, sonnet for code, opus for design)
  4. Default: 
Fallback chains: 3 retries + nuclear (omit parameter → platform default)
  - Premium: 
  - Standard: 
  - Fast:

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Background/Async Execution
text:
Mechanism: 
Behavior: Non-blocking spawns, fire-and-forget
Result collection:  with  for polling
Squad's typical flow:
  1. Spawn 3-5 agents as background tasks in one response
  2. Show launch table acknowledgment to user
  3. Poll each agent's results via  with 
  4. Assemble and present results

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: File Discovery & Access
text:
Auto-discovery:  is discovered automatically
 access: Unrestricted (full filesystem)
Parallel reads: Multiple file operations in one turn supported
Parallel writes: Multiple file creates/edits in one turn supported

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Special Tools
text:
SQL: ✅ Available — Squad uses  for tracking todos and batch processing
Web fetch: ✅ Available —  for live data
GitHub MCP: ✅ Available — Full GitHub CLI + API access
PowerShell: ✅ Available — Terminal commands for git operations, builds, tests

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: VS Code (Copilot in VS Code)
text: Squad runs on VS Code with conditional support. Key differences from CLI:

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Agent Spawning
text:
Tools:  (anonymous) or  (named custom agent)
Behavior: Sub-agents are always synchronous (blocking) individually, but multiple subagents run in parallel when spawned in the same turn
Custom agents: Auto-discovered from  (same location as CLI)
Default behavior: Subagents inherit parent model and tools

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Model Selection
text:
Mechanism A (Phase 1 — MVP): Accept session model
  - Subagents use whatever model the user selected in VS Code's model picker
  - No per-spawn control
  - Loss of cost optimization (Scribe might run on Opus instead of Haiku)
Mechanism B (Phase 2 — Future): Custom agent frontmatter
  - Define  in  files: 
  - Supports prioritized lists: 
  - Static per-agent, not per-spawn dynamic
  - Requires experimental setting: 
Recommendation for now: Use  (anonymous) for all spawns. Accept session model. Model cost optimization deferred.

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Background/Async Execution
text:
Mechanism: Parallel concurrent subagents (not fire-and-forget)
Result collection: Automatic — no  polling needed
Synchronicity: All subagents are sync individually, but multiple subagents in one turn run concurrently
Fire-and-forget (Scribe): Not possible
  - Workaround: Batch Scribe as the last subagent in parallel groups. Scribe is light work (Haiku model, file ops only), so blocking is tolerable
Launch acknowledgment: Skip launch tables — results arrive with response, not separately
Key insight: VS Code's parallelism model is functionally equivalent to CLI's background mode when multiple subagents launch in the same turn. The difference is UX: CLI shows intermediate feedback (launch table), VS Code waits and shows all results at once.

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: File Discovery & Access
text:
Auto-discovery:  auto-discovered from workspace on load (file watchers enabled — no restart needed on changes)
Scope: Workspace-scoped (cannot access outside workspace directory)
 read: ✅ Full access via  tool
 write: ✅ Full access via  /  tools
First-time approval: VS Code may prompt for file modification approval on first write (security feature)
  - User experience: "Always allow in this workspace" option available
  - Subsequent writes in same workspace are automatic
Tool inheritance: Sub-agents inherit parent's tools by default (a net positive vs CLI)
Parallel operations: Multiple  /  /  calls in one turn supported

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Special Tools
text:
SQL: ❌ Not available — avoid SQL-dependent workflows
Web fetch: ✅ Available as  tool (may require URL approval)
GitHub MCP: ✅ Available if configured in workspace
Terminal: ✅ Available as  — works for git operations
Codebase search: ✅ Available as semantic () + literal () search

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Constraints & Caveats
text:
Workspace trust: Squad requires a trusted workspace (VS Code security setting)
Single-root workspaces: Recommended; multi-root has path resolution bugs (vscode#264837, vscode#293428)
Silent success bug: VS Code may report file edits as successful when no changes occurred (vscode#253561) — same bug as CLI's P0 issue (Proposal 015)

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: JetBrains IDEs (IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm, etc.)
text: Status: Untested. JetBrains Copilot integration exists but sub-agent spawning mechanisms are not yet documented.

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Known Constraints
text:
No native Copilot CLI equivalent
Copilot plugin provides chat but sub-agent spawn capability is unclear
File discovery and workspace integration differ from VS Code

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Questions to Answer
text:
Does JetBrains Copilot support agent spawning via a tool equivalent to  or ?

What model selection mechanisms exist?
Is there a background/async mode?

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: GitHub.com (Copilot Chat in GitHub)
text: Status: Untested. GitHub's web-based Copilot has limited agent orchestration.

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Known Constraints
text:
Copilot Chat on GitHub.com focuses on issue-centric workflows (not general agent spawning)
No documented sub-agent spawning mechanism
Context is limited to conversation scope

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: Questions to Answer
text:
Can GitHub Copilot spawn agents for background work?
Can agents read  files from the repository?
Is there a GitHub-specific command protocol for delegation?

title: Copilot Client Compatibility Matrix
heading: For Developers Using Squad
text:
Use CLI if:
You need sub-agent spawning with full control (model selection, agent type, background mode)
You use SQL in your Squad workflows
You need fire-and-forget execution (Scribe)
You want cost optimization via Haiku/Sonnet/Opus tiering
Use VS Code if:
You work in VS Code and want seamless integration
You don't need per-spawn model selection (accept session model)
You're OK with Scribe running synchronously (batched with other agents)
You prefer not to see intermediate launch tables
Using Both:
CLI is recommended for initial Squad setup and learning
VS Code works for day-to-day development once Squad is established
They share the same  state — both can read/write the same team files
Team state is portable — init in CLI, use in VS Code, export/import across repos

heading: For Squad Developers
text:

title: Cross-organization authentication
heading: Cross-organization authentication
text:
# Cross-organization authentication
Try this when you have repos in multiple GitHub accounts:
Try this to set up multi-account auth:
You have repositories in both personal GitHub (github.com) and GitHub Enterprise Cloud with Enterprise Managed Users (EMU). The  CLI and git credentials are tied to one account at a time. Squad agents hit authentication errors when working across these boundaries.

title: Cross-organization authentication
heading: The problem
text:
GitHub Enterprise Managed Users (EMU) provisions user accounts managed by your enterprise. Usernames typically follow a pattern like  (e.g., ). When you work across personal GitHub and EMU organizations:
Your git credentials authenticate to one account at a time
The  CLI authenticates to one account at a time
Squad agents inherit your authentication context
When an agent tries to access a repo tied to a different account, authentication fails
Common error messages:

Both should succeed without authentication errors.

title: Cross-organization authentication
heading: Tips
text:
Switch before starting Squad — run  before launching a Squad session if you know which repos you'll work on
Error detection works both ways — if an agent hits an auth error, check  and switch manually before retrying
Use Copilot instructions for documentation — document account mappings in  so Squad agents (and human teammates) know which account to use
Test both accounts — verify both  and  operations work for each account before relying on multi-account workflows
EMU hostname varies — some EMU orgs use  with organization-scoped access; others use dedicated hostnames like . Check with your GitHub admin.
Token permissions matter — EMU accounts may have restricted permissions. Ensure your token has , , and  scopes.

title: Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
heading: Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
text:
# Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
Try this to recover from data loss:
Try this to revert bad code:
Try this to reset confused agents:
Recovery procedures for deleted , bad agent code, confused squads, and upgrade issues. Most problems are fixable with Git or re-init.

title: Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
heading: 1. "I accidentally deleted `.ai-team/`"
text:
Recovery scenarios: deleted , bad agent code, confused squad, upgrade issues.
Solution: It's in Git. Restore it.
If you haven't committed  yet, it's gone. Rebuild:
Start from scratch. If you exported your squad before, import the export:
Prevention: Commit  early. Don't let it stay uncommitted for long.

title: Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
heading: 2. "An agent wrote bad code"
text:
What happened: Morpheus implemented a feature, but it has a bug. Michael (the Lead) didn't catch it during review.
Solution: Use the reviewer rejection protocol.
Morpheus fixes it:
Alternatively, have a different agent fix it:
Sonny reads the code, fixes the issue, commits.
Prevention: Use code review. Michael should always review code before it lands.

title: Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
heading: 3. "An agent made a wrong decision"
text:
What happened: Neo decided to use REST when GraphQL was the better choice. The decision is logged in .
Solution: Add a directive to override it.
`
📋 Scribe — logged decision override

title: Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
heading: 2025-07-15: Using GraphQL instead of REST
text:
By: You (overriding Neo's recommendation)
What: API will use GraphQL, not REST
Why: Client needs flexible queries, REST would require too many endpoints
Neo's previous recommendation archived.
`
Agents now read the new decision and build accordingly.
Prevention: Review major decisions before agents implement them. Use the Lead as a sounding board, not a dictator.

title: Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
heading: 4. "My squad is confused after a bad session"
text:
What happened: Agents learned incorrect information during a session. Now they're making mistakes.
Solution: Have the Scribe archive old learnings and start fresh.
Agents forget the bad session. They still have their long-term skills and decisions.
Prevention: End sessions if agents are going in the wrong direction. Don't let them accumulate bad context.

title: Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
heading: 5. "I want to start over completely"
text:
Solution: Delete  and reinitialize.
You're back to day one. Clean slate.
Prevention: Only do this if the squad is truly beyond repair. Usually archiving histories (above) is enough.

title: Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
heading: 6. "Upgrade broke something"
text:
What happened: You upgraded Squad to a new version, and now something doesn't work.
Solution: Squad upgrades never touch . The issue is likely in:
Workflow templates — check 
Squad agent definition — check 
Model configuration — check 
Roll back the Squad agent definition:
Or reinstall a previous version:
Your team's knowledge is safe.  is untouched.
Prevention: Check the CHANGELOG before upgrading. If the upgrade is major, test in a branch first.

title: Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
heading: 7. "An agent is stuck in a loop"
text:
What happened: Tank keeps writing the same failing test over and over.
Solution: Stop the agent manually.
If the agent doesn't stop:
Or just close the Copilot session (Ctrl+C) and start a new one.
Prevention: If a test is flaky, tell agents to skip it until the environment is fixed.

title: Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
heading: 8. "Skills are outdated or wrong"
text:
What happened: A skill file in  contains outdated information. Agents are following bad advice.
Solution: Edit or delete the skill file.
Prevention: Review skills periodically. If your patterns change, update the skills.

title: Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
heading: 9. "Decisions.md is a mess"
text:
What happened:  has 200 entries and it's hard to find anything.
Solution: Archive old decisions.
Agents still have access to archived decisions if they need them, but the main file is cleaner.
Prevention: Periodically archive old decisions. Keep  focused on recent, relevant choices.

title: Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
heading: 10. "I can't tell which agent did what"
text:
What happened: Multiple agents worked on the same feature, and the commit history is tangled.
Solution: Check agent histories.
Each agent logs what they did in their .
Prevention: Agents automatically log their work. You don't have to do anything.

title: Disaster Recovery — When Things Go Wrong
heading: Tips
text:
is in Git. If you delete it, restore from Git. If it's uncommitted, it's gone.
Code review catches bad agent code. Use the Lead to review before merging.
Override bad decisions with directives. If an agent made the wrong call, tell the team the correct one.
Archive confused histories. If a session went badly, archive the learnings so agents forget.
Upgrades don't touch . Your team's knowledge is safe across upgrades.
Edit skill files directly. They're just markdown. If a skill is wrong, fix it or delete it.
Agent histories are the audit log. Check them to see what each agent did.

title: Keeping Your Squad Across Many Projects

text:
# Keeping Your Squad Across Many Projects
Try this to prevent re-casting:
Try this to save your team:
Try this to load a saved team:
Your squad remembers skills, casting, and knowledge. Export them from one project, import into another. They bring all their accumulated expertise with them.

title: Keeping Your Squad Across Many Projects
heading: 1. Your Squad Remembers
text:
Squad persistence and portability. Your squad remembers skills, casting, and knowledge. Take them everywhere.
After working on a project for a few weeks, your squad has learned:
Skills — patterns, conventions, best practices (23 skill files in )
Decisions — architectural choices, why you picked X over Y ()
Histories — project-specific context each agent accumulated ()
Casting state — the chosen agent names, roles, universe ()
All of this lives in . Commit it, and anyone who clones your repo gets the full team.

title: Keeping Your Squad Across Many Projects
heading: 2. Export Your Squad
text:
When you finish a project or want to take your squad to a new one:
Important: The export removes project-specific details from histories. What remains is portable knowledge — skills, decisions, patterns. Not "we use PostgreSQL in this repo," but "always validate input with Zod."

title: Keeping Your Squad Across Many Projects
heading: 3. Import Your Squad Into a New Project
text:
Start a new project:
When Squad asks what you're building:
Now describe the new project:
Agents already know:
TypeScript conventions (from skills)
Testing patterns (from skills)
Decision-making norms (from decisions)
Their own specialties (from histories)
But they don't know the old project's code structure — that was stripped from the export.

title: Keeping Your Squad Across Many Projects
heading: ✅ Portable knowledge (carries over):
text:
Skills — generic patterns ("always rate-limit auth endpoints")
Decisions — architectural reasoning ("why we chose WebSockets over polling")
Casting state — agent names, universe theme
Agent roles — who does what

title: Keeping Your Squad Across Many Projects
heading: ❌ Project-specific details (stripped):
text:
"The users table has 12 columns"
"The API is at /server/routes/"
"We use Prisma with PostgreSQL"
File paths, module names, specific code references
This is intentional. Skills are reusable. Project details are not.

title: Keeping Your Squad Across Many Projects

When Squad releases a new version:
Upgrades only change:
The Squad agent definition ()
Workflow templates ()
The installer itself
Your team's knowledge is safe.

text:
You commit :
A teammate clones the repo:
They select Squad from . The full team loads instantly — same agents, same skills, same knowledge.
No re-training. No setup. The squad is in the repo.

title: Keeping Your Squad Across Many Projects
heading: 7. Long-Lived Squads
text:
Some squads persist across multiple projects over months or years:
Start project A — squad learns TypeScript, React, testing conventions
Export → 
Start project B — import the squad, they already know TypeScript patterns
Squad learns Firebase — new skills accumulated
Export → 
Start project C — import v2, squad now knows TypeScript + Firebase
Squad learns GraphQL — more skills
Export → 
Your squad becomes more valuable over time. Each project adds to their knowledge.

title: Keeping Your Squad Across Many Projects

text:
Starting a new project with an experienced squad:
After a project ends:
Mid-project knowledge checkpoint:

title: Keeping Your Squad Across Many Projects
heading: Tips
text:
Export often. At the end of each project or after a major milestone, export your squad. It's your team backup.
Generic skills are gold. Skills like "always validate input" are reusable everywhere. Project-specific notes like "our API is at /api/v2" are not.
Casting state persists. If your team is Neo, Trinity, Morpheus, they stay that way across projects. Consistent names build familiarity.
Import, don't rebuild. Starting fresh every project wastes the knowledge you've accumulated. Always import your latest squad export.
Your squad gets smarter. After 5 projects, your squad has seen more patterns than most junior developers.

title: Squad on Large Codebases
heading: Squad on Large Codebases
text:
# Squad on Large Codebases
Try this to understand before changing:
Try this to set up domain routing:
Squad handles scale: each agent gets their own 200K context window, routing ensures only the right agent looks at relevant code. No loading everything — just what's needed.

title: Squad on Large Codebases
heading: 1. The Scale Problem
text:
How Squad handles scale. Agents work in their own 200K context windows. Routing ensures only the right agent looks at code.
Large codebases overwhelm most AI tools:
500K+ lines of code — can't fit in a single context window
Hundreds of files — loading everything is slow and expensive
Deep module trees — agents get lost in the structure
Multiple services — monorepos with 10+ services
Squad doesn't try to load everything. Instead, each agent gets its own 200K token context window, and routing ensures only the right agent works on relevant code.

title: Squad on Large Codebases
heading: 2. Each Agent Has Its Own Context Window
text:
When you give a task:
Squad spawns multiple agents in parallel, each with their own context:
Each agent's context is isolated. Trinity doesn't load backend code. Morpheus doesn't load frontend components. They only read what's relevant to their task.
Your codebase size doesn't matter — agents aren't loading all 500K lines.

title: Squad on Large Codebases
heading: 3. Routing Directs Work to the Right Agent
text:
Routing rules in  keep agents focused:
When you say:
The coordinator routes to Trinity. Only Trinity looks at frontend code. Morpheus and Tank don't load any of it.
When you say:
The coordinator routes to Morpheus. Only Morpheus looks at backend code.
This is how Squad scales — selective loading, not brute-force context.

title: Squad on Large Codebases
heading: 4. History Summarization Keeps Memories Manageable
text:
After 10 sessions, agent histories can grow large. The Scribe archives old learnings to keep histories focused:
Agents don't forget — they just move old details to the archive. Generic knowledge becomes skills, and specific session logs are archived.

title: Squad on Large Codebases
heading: 5. Skills Encode Patterns — No Rediscovery
text:
After an agent solves a problem, it writes a skill file:
:
Next time any agent handles Stripe webhooks, they read this skill first. No rediscovery. No re-exploration.
Over time, your  directory becomes a compressed knowledge base — 50 skill files instead of 10,000 lines of history.

title: Squad on Large Codebases
heading: 6. Be Explicit About Scope on Huge Repos
text:
On truly massive codebases (monorepos with 10+ services), help agents focus:
Or route by service in :
Each agent becomes a service owner.

title: Squad on Large Codebases
heading: 7. Sample Prompts for Large Codebases
text:
Initial exploration:
Scoped task assignment:
Routing-based work:
Agent memory check:
If Morpheus's answer is stale or incomplete:

title: Squad on Large Codebases
heading: 8. Workaround: Use File Paths in Prompts
text:
If agents are getting lost in a huge codebase, give explicit file paths:
Agents load only the specified file and its immediate dependencies.

title: Squad on Large Codebases
heading: Tips
text:
Each agent has 200K tokens. Even a 1M-line codebase isn't a problem — agents only load what's relevant.
Routing prevents over-exploration. Route frontend work to frontend agents, backend to backend agents. Don't let everyone explore everything.
Skills are compressed knowledge. After 5 sessions, your team has 30 skill files that encode months of learning. Agents read skills in seconds.
History archiving happens automatically. The Scribe moves old session logs to the archive, keeping current histories lean.
Be explicit on monorepos. In a 10-service monorepo, tell agents which service they're working on. Don't let them wander.
Agent context is isolated. Frontend agents don't load backend code. Backend agents don't load frontend code. This is the key to scaling.

Start with exploration, not implementation. Let agents map the codebase before giving tasks. They'll make fewer mistakes.

Use the Lead to audit existing code. Michael can review code that was written before Squad was added.
Skills accumulate fast. After 2–3 sessions, agents know your patterns. By session 5, they're as good as a developer who's been on the project for weeks.
Never too late. Squad works on 3-week projects and 3-year projects. Agents adapt to what's there.

text:
Some patterns apply across all services:

title: Squad in Monorepos

title: Squad in Monorepos
heading: 8. One Squad, Not Eight
text:
Why one squad instead of one per service?
Shared knowledge. Patterns that span services (logging, auth, error handling) are encoded once in skills.
Cross-service coordination. Michael (the Lead) sees the whole monorepo, not just one service.
Fewer exports/imports. You don't have to export/import squads between services.
Consistent conventions. All services follow the same patterns because the same agents work on them.
If you had 8 separate squads, they'd diverge. One squad keeps the monorepo aligned.

title: Squad in Monorepos
heading: Tips
text:
One squad per monorepo. Add specialists per service, keep them in one team.
Routing rules are critical. Route work to the right specialist so agents don't wander.
Skills that span services are gold. Logging, error handling, API conventions — document once, use everywhere.
Shared code needs careful handling. Changes to  affect multiple services — route to the Lead.
Cross-service features need coordination. Use the Lead to decompose, then route to specialists.

title: Multi-Codespace Setup with Squad SubSquads
heading: Multi-Codespace Setup with Squad SubSquads
text:
# Multi-Codespace Setup with Squad SubSquads
End-to-end walkthrough of running multiple Squad instances across Codespaces.

title: Multi-Codespace Setup with Squad SubSquads
heading: Background: The Tetris Experiment
text:
We validated Squad SubSquads by building a multiplayer Tetris game using 3 Codespaces, each running a separate SubSquad:
  Codespace   SubSquad   Label   Focus  
 ----------- -------- ------- ------- 
  CS-1         React game board, piece rendering, animations  
  CS-2         WebSocket server, game state, matchmaking  
  CS-3         CI/CD, Docker, deployment  
All three Codespaces shared the same repository. Each Squad instance only picked up issues matching its SubSquad's label.

title: Multi-Codespace Setup with Squad SubSquads
heading: 1. Create the SubSquads config
text: In your repository, create :

title: Multi-Codespace Setup with Squad SubSquads
heading: 2. Configure each Codespace
text:
In , set the  env var. For multiple configs, use devcontainer features or separate devcontainer folders:
Option A: Separate devcontainer configs
Option B: Set env var after launch

title: Multi-Codespace Setup with Squad SubSquads
heading: 3. Label your issues
text: Create GitHub issues with the appropriate team labels:

title: Multi-Codespace Setup with Squad SubSquads
heading: 4. Launch Squad in each Codespace
text: Each Codespace runs  normally. The SubSquad context is detected automatically:

title: Multi-Codespace Setup with Squad SubSquads
heading: 5. Monitor across SubSquads
text: Use the CLI from any Codespace to see all SubSquads:

title: Multi-Codespace Setup with Squad SubSquads
heading: What Worked
text:
Clear separation: Each SubSquad had well-defined boundaries, minimizing merge conflicts
Parallel velocity: 3x throughput vs. single-squad mode for independent work
Label-based routing: Simple, uses existing GitHub infrastructure

title: Multi-Codespace Setup with Squad SubSquads
heading: What Didn't Work (Yet)
text:
Cross-SubSquad dependencies: When the UI team needed a backend API change, manual coordination was required
Shared files: , , and other root files caused occasional conflicts
No meta-coordinator: No automated way to coordinate across SubSquads (future work)

title: Multi-Codespace Setup with Squad SubSquads
heading: Lessons Learned
text:
Keep SubSquads independent — design folder boundaries to minimize shared files
Use branch-per-issue — direct commits across SubSquads cause merge hell
Label everything — unlabeled issues get lost between SubSquads
Start with 2 SubSquads — add more once the team finds its rhythm

title: Combining Knowledge from Multiple Squads

title: Combining Knowledge from Multiple Squads
heading: 1. The Reality: One Full Squad at a Time
text:
You have 3 repos with trained squads. You're starting a new project and want the best of all of them.
Squad's import system brings in a complete team — roster, charters, histories, skills, decisions. You can only import one full squad cleanly.
But you can cherry-pick skills and knowledge manually.
Want continuous coordination instead? If you need squads on different machines to see each other's live state (not just import once), see Distributed Mesh — it syncs remote squad state via git and HTTP.

text:

title: Combining Knowledge from Multiple Squads

title: Combining Knowledge from Multiple Squads

text:

title: Combining Knowledge from Multiple Squads
heading: 7. Alternative: Use --force for Full Reimport (Destructive)
text:

title: Combining Knowledge from Multiple Squads
heading: Tips
text:
One full import, then cherry-pick. Import the squad with the closest domain match, then manually copy skills from the others.
Skills are modular. Each skill file is independent. Copy the ones you need, ignore the rest.
Histories are context-heavy. Don't import histories from unrelated projects — they contain project-specific details that will confuse agents.
Decisions can be manually merged. If another squad made architectural decisions you want to preserve, copy them into  as new entries.
Skill files are the cleanest transfer. They're generic, portable, and immediately useful across projects.

title: Squad on Private Repositories — Privacy & Security
heading: 1. Where Squad Runs
text: Squad runs entirely in your Copilot session. Nothing leaves your machine except what Copilot already does.

title: Squad on Private Repositories — Privacy & Security
heading: 1. Where Squad Runs
text:
Squad is a local agent that runs in your GitHub Copilot CLI session. It doesn't phone home. It doesn't send data to a Squad server. It doesn't use external APIs.
When you run:
And select Squad from the  list (CLI) or  (VS Code), Squad runs on your machine using Copilot's infrastructure.
What this means:
Your code stays local
Squad sees what Copilot already sees (your repo, your files)
No additional data transmission beyond Copilot's standard operation
No Squad-specific telemetry or tracking

title: Squad on Private Repositories — Privacy & Security
heading: 2. What's Stored in `.ai-team/`
text:
Squad writes everything to  in your repository:
You control what's committed. If you don't want agent histories in your repo, add them to :
Now histories stay local. Charters, skills, and decisions are still committed.

title: Squad on Private Repositories — Privacy & Security
heading: 3. Agent Histories May Contain Project-Specific Info
text:
Agent histories () log what agents learned during sessions:
`markdown

title: Squad on Private Repositories — Privacy & Security
heading: What I learned
text:
The users table has columns: id, email, createdat, role
Auth middleware is in /server/middleware/auth.ts
Passwords are hashed with bcrypt, salt rounds = 12
`
This is project-specific knowledge. If your repo is private and you want to keep it that way, review histories before sharing exports.

title: Squad on Private Repositories — Privacy & Security
heading: 4. Skills Are Generic — Safe to Share
text:
Skill files in  are intentionally generic:
:
No project-specific details. No code references. No file paths.
Skills are safe to share publicly. They're best practices, not secrets.

title: Squad on Private Repositories — Privacy & Security
heading: 5. Exporting Your Squad — What's Included
text:
The export includes:
✅ Roster (agent names, roles)  
✅ Charters (agent role definitions)  
✅ Skills (generic best practices)  
✅ Decisions (architectural reasoning)  
✅ Histories — with project-specific details removed  
✅ Casting state (agent names, universe)
Squad strips project-specific info from histories during export. What remains is portable knowledge:
"Always validate input with Zod" ✅
"The API is at /server/routes/api.ts" ❌ (removed)
The export is safe to share with other projects or teammates, but review it first if you're paranoid.

title: Squad on Private Repositories — Privacy & Security
heading: 6. Labels and Workflows Use Standard GitHub APIs
text:
Squad's GitHub Actions workflows (Ralph heartbeat, auto-assign) use GitHub's public APIs:
Issue labeling — uses the GitHub Issues API with your repo's auth token
PR creation — uses the GitHub Pull Requests API
Workflow dispatch — uses the GitHub Actions API
No third-party services. No external webhooks. Just GitHub talking to GitHub.
Your GitHub token permissions matter. If your repo is private, make sure the token used by Actions has the appropriate permissions (, ).

title: Squad on Private Repositories — Privacy & Security
heading: 7. What Squad DOESN'T Do
text:
❌ Send code to a Squad server  
❌ Use external APIs for processing  
❌ Transmit telemetry or usage data  
❌ Store your team's knowledge in a cloud service  
❌ Require an account or API key (beyond your GitHub Copilot license)
Squad is entirely local. It's a GitHub Copilot agent, not a standalone service.

title: Squad on Private Repositories — Privacy & Security
heading: 8. Private Repo Checklist
text:
If your repository is private and you're security-conscious:
[ ] Review  — make sure no secrets or sensitive details are logged
[ ] Add  to  if you don't want histories committed
[ ] Review exports before sharing — check  for project-specific details
[ ] Audit  — remove any decisions that reference internal systems or secrets
[ ] Use GitHub token permissions wisely — don't give Actions more permissions than needed
[ ] Skills are public-safe — feel free to share  publicly

Squad runs in Copilot, not on a remote server. Your code stays on your machine.

Skills are generic by design. They're safe to share publicly.

GitHub Actions workflows use your repo's auth. Make sure token permissions are scoped correctly.

title: Remote Q&A with Squad
heading: Remote Q&A with Squad
text:
# Remote Q&A with Squad

⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.


Try this:


You don't always have the repo cloned locally. Sometimes you want to ask your Squad a question from the browser, the GitHub CLI, or a mobile device — without pulling code.

title: Remote Q&A with Squad
heading: Current options
text: Squad already supports several remote interaction paths. Each trades off convenience, depth, and setup effort.

title: Remote Q&A with Squad
heading: 1. Copilot Chat with squad.agent.md
text:
If the repo has , GitHub Copilot Chat reads it automatically when you ask questions about the repo.

How it works:
Open the repo in GitHub.com
Use Copilot Chat in the browser
Copilot reads the agent file and answers using your team's context

Good for: Quick questions about architecture, team structure, and project conventions.

Limitation: Copilot reads the default branch only. You can't point it at a feature branch.

title: Remote Q&A with Squad
heading: 2. Assign an issue to @copilot
text:
Create a GitHub issue and assign it to . If the repo has Squad's issue-assign workflow (), the coding agent picks up the issue and works it using your Squad configuration.

How it works:
Create an issue describing the question or task
Assign it to 
The workflow triggers and Squad processes it

Good for: Tasks that need code changes, research across files, or multi-step investigation.

Limitation: Designed for work items, not conversational Q&A. The workflow runs against the default branch.

title: Remote Q&A with Squad
heading: 3. Use `squad:` labels on issues
text:
Add a  label to any issue, and Squad routes it to the right team member.

How it works:
Create or label an issue with  (or any member name)
The triage workflow assigns it to the appropriate agent
Work proceeds through the normal Squad flow

Good for: Routing specific work to specific team members without cloning.

Limitation: Requires label setup on the repo. Routes work, not questions.

title: Remote Q&A with Squad
heading: What's not supported yet
text: These features don't exist today but would make remote Q&A more powerful:

title: Remote Q&A with Squad
heading: Branch-aware queries
text:
All current remote paths read the default branch. You can't ask "How does auth work on the  branch?" and get branch-specific answers.

Workaround: Mention the branch in your question and ask the agent to check out that branch during investigation.

title: Remote Q&A with Squad
heading: GitHub Discussions integration
text: A Discussions-based Q&A channel where Squad monitors and answers questions would make remote interaction feel conversational. This would need a new workflow trigger on  events.

title: Remote Q&A with Squad
heading: Issue comment commands
text: A  command in issue comments that triggers Squad to respond inline would enable threaded Q&A without creating new issues.

title: Remote Q&A with Squad
heading: Tips
text:
Start with Copilot Chat. It's the lowest-effort path and works today for repos with .
Use issues for anything that needs code. Copilot Chat answers questions; issues drive work.
Include context in your question. Remote paths don't have your local state. Be specific about which files, features, or branches you mean.
Check the default branch. All remote paths currently read  (or whatever the repo's default branch is). If you're asking about unreleased work, mention the branch explicitly.

title: Scaling with SubSquads
heading: Scaling with SubSquads
text:
# Scaling with SubSquads
Partition your repo's work across multiple Squad instances for horizontal scaling.

title: Scaling with SubSquads
heading: The Problem
text:
A single Squad instance handles all issues in a repo. For large projects, this creates bottlenecks:
Too many issues overwhelm a single team
Agents step on each other's toes in shared code
No workflow enforcement (agents commit directly to main)
No way to monitor multiple teams centrally

title: Scaling with SubSquads
heading: The Solution: SubSquads
text: SubSquads partition a repo's issues into labeled subsets. Each Codespace (or machine) runs one SubSquad, scoped to its slice of work.

title: Scaling with SubSquads
heading: 2. Label your issues
text: Each issue gets a  label matching a SubSquad. Ralph will only pick up issues matching the active SubSquad's label.

title: Scaling with SubSquads
heading: 3. Activate a SubSquad
text:
Option A — Environment variable (Codespaces):
Set  in the Codespace's environment. Squad auto-detects it on session start.
Option B — CLI activation (local):
This writes a  file (gitignored — local to your machine).
Option C — Single SubSquad auto-select:
If  defines only one SubSquad, it's auto-selected.

title: Scaling with SubSquads
heading: 4. Run Squad normally
text: Ralph will only scan for issues with the  label. Agents will only pick up matching work.

title: Scaling with SubSquads
heading: CLI Commands
text: Note:  and  are deprecated aliases for .

title: Scaling with SubSquads
heading: folderScope is Advisory
text: tells agents which directories to focus on — but it's not a hard lock. Agents can modify shared packages (like ) when needed, and will call out when working outside their scope.

title: Scaling with SubSquads
heading: Workflow Enforcement
text:
Each SubSquad specifies a  (default: ). When active, agents:
Create a branch for every issue ()
Open a PR when work is ready
Never commit directly to main

title: Scaling with SubSquads
heading: Single-Machine Multi-SubSquad
text: You don't need multiple Codespaces to test. Use  to switch between SubSquads sequentially on a single machine.

title: Scaling with SubSquads
heading: Resolution Chain
text:
Squad resolves the active SubSquad in this order:
 environment variable
 file (written by )
Auto-select if exactly one SubSquad is defined
No SubSquad → single-squad mode (backward compatible)

title: Scaling with SubSquads
heading: Monitoring
text: Use  to see all SubSquads' activity:
