title: Squad for Solo Developers
heading: Squad for Solo Developers
text:
# Squad for Solo Developers
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this:
Squad gives solo devs what they're missing: code review, test coverage, and architectural second opinions. Start with 3 agents instead of 5.

title: Squad for Solo Developers
heading: 1. Why Squad Works for Solo Devs
text:
When you're working alone:
No code reviews. Bugs land in production because no one else looked.
No testing discipline. You skip tests when you're in a hurry.
No second opinion. Architectural decisions go unchallenged.
Squad gives you a Lead who reviews everything, a tester who catches edge cases, and specialists who know their domain. You're one person, but you have a team that remembers everything.

title: Squad for Solo Developers
heading: 2. Start Small: 3 Agents Instead of 5
text:
You don't need a full 5-agent team as a solo dev. Customize the roster:
Lean team. Just what you need.

title: Squad for Solo Developers
heading: 3. The Reviewer Protocol Catches Bugs
text:
When you give a task to an agent:
Fredo builds it. Then Michael (the Lead) automatically reviews the code.
This is the safety net you don't have as a solo dev. Michael catches issues before they land.

title: Squad for Solo Developers
heading: 4. Skills Accumulate — You Don't Repeat Mistakes
text:
After Michael rejects Fredo's code for missing rate limiting, it gets encoded as a skill:
:
Next time any agent builds an auth feature, they read this skill first. The mistake doesn't happen again.

title: Squad for Solo Developers
heading: 5. You're Solo, But Your Team Remembers Everything
text:
As a solo dev, you juggle dozens of context switches. Squad doesn't forget:
Decisions are logged. "Why did I use PostgreSQL instead of MongoDB?" — check .
Skills capture patterns. "How do I structure FastAPI routes?" — check .
Histories track what each agent learned. Fredo knows your database schema after one session.
You get back to a project after 3 weeks, and the team is still up to speed.

title: Squad for Solo Developers
heading: 6. Workflow: Solo Dev + Squad
text:
Fredo builds it. Michael reviews. Kay writes tests.
All three work in parallel. You give one prompt, get three agents.
When it's done:
You're a solo dev, but you shipped like a team.

title: Squad for Solo Developers
heading: 7. Use the Lead as a Sounding Board
text: Michael's not always right, but having a second opinion is invaluable.

title: Squad for Solo Developers
heading: Tips
text:
Start with 3–4 agents. You don't need a frontend and backend specialist if you're only building one layer.
The Lead is your reviewer. Use them to review your own code before committing.
Skills are YOUR documentation. After 10 sessions,  is a custom knowledge base.
Agents work while you're thinking. Give a vague task and agents research in parallel.
You get testing discipline. The Tester writes tests you'd skip. Coverage goes up without extra effort.

title: Switching AI Models — Budget vs Quality
heading: Switching AI Models — Budget vs Quality
text:
# Switching AI Models — Budget vs Quality
Try this to reduce costs temporarily:
Try this to maximize quality:
Try this to restore defaults:
Squad supports 16 models across three tiers. Switch the entire team, set per-agent preferences, or let Squad auto-route based on task type.

title: Switching AI Models — Budget vs Quality
heading: 1. The Default: Cost-First Strategy
text:
Model selection strategies for different needs. Squad supports 16 models with fallback chains.
Squad's default model configuration prioritizes cost-effectiveness:
Non-code tasks (planning, triage, decisions) →  (fast, cheap)
Code tasks (implementation, refactoring) →  (balanced quality/cost)
Code review (critical analysis) → 
This is optimized for everyday use — good quality without burning your API budget.

title: Switching AI Models — Budget vs Quality
heading: 2. Budget Mode: Always Use Haiku
text:
If you're cost-sensitive or working on a small project:
This is written to :
Now every agent uses Haiku for everything. Faster responses, lower cost, slightly less nuanced reasoning.

title: Switching AI Models — Budget vs Quality
heading: 3. Quality Mode: Use Opus for Critical Work
text:
If you're working on architecture, security, or high-stakes features:
This is written to :
Neo gets the premium model for code reviews and decisions. Everyone else stays on Sonnet.

title: Switching AI Models — Budget vs Quality
heading: 4. Per-Agent Overrides
text:
You can set different models for different roles:
Tank doesn't need Opus to write tests. Neo does need it for code reviews. Balanced spend.

title: Switching AI Models — Budget vs Quality
heading: 5. The 16-Model Catalog
text:
Squad supports these models (as of v0.2.0):
Premium tier (highest quality, highest cost):
 — latest Opus, best reasoning
 — Opus with faster response time
 — previous Opus generation
Standard tier (balanced quality/cost):
 — current default for code tasks
 — previous Sonnet generation
 — Google's latest
 — OpenAI Codex, code-specialized
Fast/cheap tier (fast responses, lower cost):
 — current default for non-code tasks
 — smaller Codex model
Note: Model availability depends on your GitHub Copilot subscription tier.

title: Switching AI Models — Budget vs Quality
heading: 6. Fallback Chains
text:
If a model is unavailable, Squad falls back to the next tier:
If Opus is unavailable (rate limit, quota), Squad automatically uses Sonnet. If Sonnet is unavailable, it falls back to Haiku.
You don't have to configure this — it's automatic.

title: Switching AI Models — Budget vs Quality
heading: 7. When to Use Which Model
text:
Use Haiku () when:
Writing tests
Running triage or planning tasks
Generating boilerplate code
Refactoring (simple renames, restructuring)
You're on a budget and speed matters more than depth
Use Sonnet () when:
Writing feature code
Implementing APIs or UI components
Refactoring with logic changes
Most everyday development tasks
Use Opus () when:
Code review (the Lead should catch subtle bugs)
Architectural decisions
Security-sensitive code
Complex debugging
Critical features where quality trumps cost

title: Switching AI Models — Budget vs Quality
heading: 8. Sample Prompts for Model Configuration
text:
Check current model configuration:
Switch everyone to budget mode:
Use premium for the Lead only:
Temporary override for a specific task:
Reset to defaults:

title: Switching AI Models — Budget vs Quality
heading: Tips
text:
Default is fine for most projects. Haiku for planning, Sonnet for code. You don't need to change it.
Use Opus for the Lead. Code reviews benefit most from premium reasoning. Opus catches edge cases Sonnet misses.
Haiku is underrated for tests. Test writing doesn't require deep reasoning — Haiku is fast and accurate enough.
Per-agent overrides are cheap. Put Opus on the Lead, Haiku on the Tester, Sonnet on everyone else. Balanced budget.
Model config is in . Commit it so your team uses the same models.

title: Squad Alongside a Team of Human Developers
heading: Squad Alongside a Team of Human Developers
text:
# Squad Alongside a Team of Human Developers
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Try this:
Mixed AI + human teams: AI agents handle implementation, humans review and make critical decisions. Route work to humans just like AI agents.

text:
Tell Squad about your human team members:

text:

`

title: Squad Alongside a Team of Human Developers
heading: 2. Routing Rules for Mixed Teams
text:
Edit  to route work to humans:
Now when you give a task:
The coordinator sees "architectural change" and routes to humans:
The AI agents don't start implementation until the humans decide.

title: Squad Alongside a Team of Human Developers
heading: 3. Humans Decide, AI Implements
text:
Sarah and Jamal discuss and decide: WebSockets via Socket.IO.
You relay the decision:
Scribe logs the decision:
`markdown

title: Squad Alongside a Team of Human Developers
heading: 2025-07-15: Real-time notifications via WebSockets
text:
By: Sarah, Jamal
What: Use Socket.IO for real-time notifications
Why: Need bi-directional communication, Socket.IO has better mobile client support than raw WebSockets
`
AI agents read this before starting. They know the humans already decided.

title: Squad Alongside a Team of Human Developers
heading: 4. AI Agents Work, Humans Review PRs
text:
Agents create pull requests. Humans review them.
Sarah and Jamal review the PR on GitHub. If they request changes, relay that back:
Morpheus pushes a fix. The humans approve. Merge.

title: Squad Alongside a Team of Human Developers
heading: 5. Pausing the Coordinator
text:
When work routes to a human, the coordinator pauses — it doesn't guess what the human will decide.
You ask Sarah. She says no, stick with REST.
`
📋 Scribe — logged decision

title: Squad Alongside a Team of Human Developers
heading: 2025-07-15: Staying with REST
text:
By: Sarah
What: Not migrating to GraphQL
Why: Current REST API works well, migration cost isn't justified
`

title: Squad Alongside a Team of Human Developers
heading: 6. Use Agents for the Tedious Work
text:
Humans handle high-level decisions and code review. Agents handle implementation, testing, and grunt work.
Tank finds a broken test, fixes it, commits. Sarah doesn't have to.

title: Squad Alongside a Team of Human Developers
heading: 7. Sample Prompts for Mixed Teams
text:
Route a decision to a human:
Delegate implementation after human approval:
Have an agent assist a human:
Agent-led PR, human review:

title: Squad Alongside a Team of Human Developers
heading: Tips
text:
Humans in the roster, not as agents. Humans don't get charters or histories, but they appear in routing rules.
Use routing rules to protect critical paths. Route payment logic, security changes, and architectural decisions to humans.
Agents don't guess. If a task routes to a human, the coordinator pauses until you relay the human's decision.
Agents make PRs, humans review. Preserve your team's code review culture — agents submit work for approval, not direct commits.
Agents handle the tedious stuff. Test writing, linting fixes, refactoring — offload it to agents so humans focus on high-value work.

# Moving a Team Between Repos
Try this to save your team:
Try this to transfer trained agents:
Export trained agents from one repo, import into another. They bring skills and portable knowledge — project-specific learnings stay tagged so they don't bleed.

title: Moving a Team Between Repos
heading: 1. Export from the Source Repo
text:
Export a trained team from one repo and import it into another. Agents carry their skills and portable knowledge — project-specific learnings stay tagged so they don't bleed into the new project.
In the repo where your team currently lives:
This creates  — a portable snapshot containing agents, casting state, skills, and accumulated knowledge.
You can also specify a custom output path:

title: Moving a Team Between Repos
heading: 2. Initialize Squad in the Target Repo
text: The target repo needs Squad installed before importing:

title: Moving a Team Between Repos
heading: 4. Handle Collisions
text:
If  already exists (e.g., this repo already had a team), import will fail:
Use  to archive the existing team and replace it:
The existing  is moved to . Nothing is deleted.

title: Moving a Team Between Repos
heading: 5. Tell the Team About the New Project
text:
Open Copilot and introduce the team to their new context:
Agents adapt. Their portable knowledge (general patterns, coding practices, team dynamics) carries over. Project-specific learnings from the old project are preserved in their histories but tagged:

title: Moving a Team Between Repos
heading: 6. History Splitting
text:
During import, agent histories are automatically split:
Portable knowledge — general learnings that transfer across projects (coding patterns, architectural principles, team conventions). These carry over normally.
Project-specific learnings — file paths, sprint details, PR references, session logs from the old project. These are preserved under a  section so agents can reference them but don't confuse old project details with the new one.

title: Moving a Team Between Repos
heading: Tips
text:
Review histories before sharing. Agent histories may contain project-specific information — file paths, API keys mentioned in context, internal architecture details. Review  before sending it to someone outside your organization.
Import starts fresh decisions. The imported team gets an empty . Old decisions lived in the source project's context. Tell agents your conventions for the new project — they'll capture them.
Casting universe is preserved. Agents keep their names and the fictional universe they were drawn from. Danny is still Danny.
Archives are cheap insurance. When using , the old team is archived, not deleted. If the import doesn't work out, rename the archive back to .
Skills carry over. Earned skills (with confidence levels) transfer with the team. Agents don't lose expertise when they move.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
text:
# Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
Your  directory contains everything—team rosters, skills, decisions, agent histories. The question isn't whether to track it, but how and where to track it. Here are the real options, with honest tradeoffs.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: 1. Committed to Main (The Default)
text: What it is:  is tracked in git, committed alongside your code. Anyone who clones the repo gets the full team with all accumulated knowledge.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Pros
text:
Simplest setup. No configuration, no branching strategy.
Portable. Clone the repo anywhere, and the team knowledge travels with it.
Shared context. Every collaborator sees the same team definitions, skills, and decisions.
Git history. You can trace how decisions evolved, view old skills, recover deleted files.
GitHub Actions work out of the box. Workflows (heartbeat, triage, label sync) access  immediately.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Cons
text:
PR noise. Every team change—new skill, updated decision, agent history—shows up in PR diffs. Some people find this distracting.
 grows. Over time, your decisions file accumulates hundreds of entries. Git history is there, but the current file gets long.
Some orgs don't allow it. Enterprise policies sometimes forbid AI artifacts in source repositories. Check before you commit.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: When to Use This
text:
Solo dev or small team, private repo.
Open source project—your contributors should see how the team works.
You want maximum portability and zero configuration.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: 2. Gitignored (Local-Only)
text: What it is: Add  to . Team state lives locally on each dev machine, never committed.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Pros
text:
Zero repo noise. No PR diffs, no git history clutter.
No policy concerns. Enterprise orgs with AI artifact policies sleep easy.
Clean main branch. Code and team are completely separated.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Cons
text:
Team knowledge is not portable. If you delete , it's gone. No git history to recover it.
Collaborators don't share state. Your teammate clones the repo and gets a fresh, empty . Their team doesn't match yours.
No git history for recovery. You can't  to find an old decision or see when a skill was added.
⚠️ GitHub Actions workflows can't access . Actions only see committed files. Triage routing rules in  won't work in CI/CD. (Label sync and other API-based workflows still function, but the team-based routing logic is silent.)

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: When to Use This
text:
Team doesn't need shared state (unlikely for Squad).
Enterprise policy strictly forbids AI artifacts in repos.
You're experimenting and don't want to commit yet.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: 3. Separate Branch (e.g., `squad-state`)
text: What it is: Keep  on a dedicated branch (, , etc.), not on . Use  to mount it locally.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Pros
text:
Clean main branch.  never appears in  or in PR diffs.
Full git history. The  branch has complete history of all team changes.
Shareable with collaborators. They can check out  and pull your team setup.
GitHub Actions can access it. Workflows can check out both  and  if needed.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Cons
text:
Complex setup. Requires knowledge of  and branch management.
Merge conflicts. If multiple people work on  simultaneously, conflicts happen.
Worktree management overhead. You need to remember to update the worktree, and it can get stale.
Collaborators must set up the worktree. They can't just clone; they need to run the setup commands.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: When to Use This
text:
Team that values clean main branch but wants shared team state.
You want full git history of team evolution but don't want PR noise.
You're already comfortable with  or branching strategies.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: 4. Git Submodule
text: What it is:  as a separate Git repository, added as a submodule to your main repo.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Setup
text:
Collaborators clone with:
Or after cloning normally:

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Pros
text:
Completely separate history. The submodule repo has its own git log, independent of your main project.
Shareable across repos. Use the same submodule in multiple projects.
Clean main branch.  is external; no PR diffs in your project.
Full git features. Submodule repo has branches, tags, and full history.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Cons
text:
Submodules are complex. Widely disliked by the git community. Conflicts, merge issues, and confusion are common.
Everyone must remember . Collaborators who forget get an empty  directory.
CI/CD needs extra setup. Your workflows must initialize submodules explicitly:
  
Updating the submodule can cause conflicts. If two people push to the submodule simultaneously, merging back is painful.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: When to Use This
text:
You're already using submodules elsewhere in your org (they're familiar with the pain).
You want to share the same squad configuration across 3+ repositories.
Your team is comfortable with advanced git workflows.
Honest take: Submodules work, but the git community almost universally dislikes them. They're powerful tools for specific use cases, but most teams regret using them. Only reach for submodules if you truly need them.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: 5. Symlink to External Directory
text: What it is: Keep  somewhere else on your filesystem (e.g., ), then symlink it into your repo.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Setup
text:
On macOS/Linux:
On Windows (requires admin or Developer Mode):
Add  to :

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Pros
text:
Share state across repos. Point multiple projects to the same squad directory.
No git noise. The symlink itself isn't tracked;  is ignored.
Maximum flexibility. You can move the squad, reorganize it, or swap it out.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Cons
text:
Not portable. Symlinks are machine-specific. Collaborators need the exact same filesystem layout or the symlink breaks.
Windows compatibility is fragile. Symlinks on Windows require admin privileges or Developer Mode; many orgs disable this.
Easy to break. If the external directory is deleted, the symlink points to nothing.
No git history. Team state changes aren't tracked in your project repo; you're on your own for backups.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: When to Use This
text:
You maintain multiple repositories with the same squad.
Everyone on your team has the same filesystem layout (rare in practice).
You're on macOS/Linux and control your development environment.
Caveat: This breaks for most teams sharing code. Collaborators' symlinks will be broken, external contractors can't participate, and CI/CD usually fails.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: 6. Dev Branch Only (The Squad Project's Own Approach)
text: What it is:  is committed, but only on dev/feature branches. On , it's gitignored. When you create a feature branch, you remove  from  so the team travels with your work.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Setup
text:
On :
When you start a feature branch:
Agents work with the full  context while you develop. When you merge back to , the PR shows the  changes, but  stays clean.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Pros
text:
Clean main branch.  is pure code, no squad artifacts.
Full context on feature branches. Agents have the team history while you work.
Git history preserved. Team changes are committed on feature branches and visible in git log.
Collaborators get team state. Anyone checking out your feature branch gets .
GitHub Actions can work both ways. On , workflows use GitHub API (label sync, heartbeat). On feature branches, they can use team-based routing if needed.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Cons
text:
Merge conflicts when syncing branches. If  has  gitignored but your branch commits it, merging is messy.
Easy to forget the pattern. Developers forget to remove  from  when creating feature branches (or forget to add it back when switching back to ).
PR diffs include team changes. PRs from feature branches show all  modifications, which some teams find noisy.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: When to Use This
text:
Small team that's aware of the pattern.
You want clean main but team context on feature branches.
You're OK with remembering to toggle  per branch.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Decision Matrix
text:
Scenario   Option   Why  
 ---------- -------- ----- 
  Solo dev, private repo   1. Committed   Simplest, portable, full history  
  Team, shared state, no PR concerns   1. Committed   Everyone gets same team  
  Team, clean main, no Actions workflows   2. Gitignored   No policy issues, no PR noise  
  Team, clean main, need Actions workflows   3. Separate Branch   Full history, shared state, Actions can access it  
  Multiple repos, same squad   4. Submodule or 5. Symlink   Submodule if you need git; symlink if portable  
  Enterprise, AI artifact policy   2. Gitignored or 4. Submodule   Keep AI stuff out of main repo  
  Open source   1. Committed   Contributors should see how the team works

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Tips
text:
GitHub Actions and Gitignored : If you choose option 2 (gitignore), remember that Actions workflows see committed files only. Label sync and heartbeat workflows (which use GitHub API) still work. But  triage rules won't see  during automated runs. Workaround: Copy critical decisions to a committed file or pass them as workflow env vars.
Merge conflicts on : If multiple people are committing to  at the same time,  and agent histories conflict frequently. Use the  rules that Squad sets up. Check the file after merge to ensure it looks reasonable.
Backup your team. If you're gitignoring , make sure you have backups. A deleted  directory with no git history is gone forever.
Communicate the pattern to your team. Whatever you choose, document it. Add a line to your  or  explaining where the squad lives and how to interact with it.
Start simple, migrate later. Commit  initially (option 1). If PR noise becomes a real problem, migrate to option 2 or 3. Changing strategies later is possible but requires care.

title: Keeping Your Squad Where You Want It
heading: Sample Prompts
text:
Use these prompts with Squad to implement specific strategies:
"Keep .ai-team/ out of my main branch."
  - Directs you toward option 3 (separate branch) or option 6 (dev-only).
"I want to share my squad across three repos without duplicating the team state."
  - Points to option 4 (submodule) or option 5 (symlink).
"Add .ai-team to .gitignore but make sure GitHub Actions can still route based on team.md."
  - Hybrid: gitignore but keep a committed  that Actions reads.
"My enterprise doesn't allow AI artifacts in the main repository."
  - Option 2 (gitignore) or option 4 (submodule in a separate org-controlled repo).
"I deleted .ai-team by accident. How do I recover it?"
  - If committed:  (restore from history).
  - If gitignored: No recovery from git. Restore from backup or rebuild the team.

title: Troubleshooting
heading: `npx github:bradygaster/squad` appears to hang
text:
Problem: Running the install command shows a frozen npm spinner. Nothing happens.
Cause: npm resolves  package specifiers via . If no SSH agent is running (or your key isn't loaded), git prompts for your passphrase on the TTY — but npm's progress spinner overwrites the prompt, making it invisible. This is an npm TTY handling issue, not a Squad bug.
Fix (choose one):
Start your SSH agent first (recommended):
   
   Then re-run .
Disable npm's progress spinner to reveal the prompt:
   
Use HTTPS instead of SSH by configuring git:
   
Reference: #30

title: Troubleshooting
heading: Authentication fails on cross-org repos
text:
Problem: Squad agents hit authentication errors when working with repositories across personal GitHub and GitHub Enterprise Managed Users (EMU) organizations.
Cause: The  CLI and git credentials are tied to one account at a time. When you switch contexts between personal and EMU repos, the active account may not have access to the target repository.
Fix:
Use  to toggle between authenticated accounts:
   
Add account mappings to  so Squad agents know which account to use for which repos.
Configure git credential helpers per host or organization.
See Cross-organization authentication for detailed setup instructions.

title: Upgrading Squad
heading: Upgrading Squad
text:
# Upgrading Squad
Update Squad-owned files to the latest version without touching your team state.

title: Upgrading Squad
heading: 1. Run the Upgrade
text:
From your repo root:
Squad detects your installed version, updates Squad-owned files, and runs any needed migrations:
That's it.

title: Upgrading Squad
heading: What Gets Upgraded
text:
File   Updated?   Notes  
 ------ ---------- ------- 
     ✅ Yes   Overwritten with latest coordinator logic  
     ✅ Yes   Overwritten with latest templates  
     ✅ Yes   Overwritten with latest squad workflows  
     ⚡ Conditional   Updated only if @copilot is enabled on the team  
     ❌ Never   Your team's knowledge, decisions, casting state, skills  
Squad-owned files ( and ) are replaced entirely. Don't put custom changes in them — they'll be lost on upgrade.
Your team state in  is never touched. Agent charters, histories, decisions, casting state, skills, and session logs are all safe.

title: Upgrading Squad
heading: Migrations
text:
Some upgrades require additive changes to your team state directory — like creating a new subdirectory that didn't exist in older versions.
Migrations are:
Additive — they only create new files or directories, never modify existing ones
Idempotent — safe to re-run; if the change already exists, it's skipped
Example: upgrading to v0.2.0 creates  if it doesn't already exist.

title: Upgrading Squad
heading: Migrating .ai-team/ → .squad/ (v0.5.0+)
text:
In Squad v0.5.0, the team state directory was renamed from  to . Existing repos continue to work — Squad detects both. If you're still on , you'll see a deprecation warning.
To migrate your repo:
Then commit:
What the migration does:
Renames  → 
Updates  and  references
Scrubs email addresses from migrated files (PII cleanup)
Timeline:  support continues through v0.6.0. Migration becomes required in v1.0.0.
Full details: See the Migration Guide.

title: Upgrading Squad
heading: Version Stamping
text: is version-stamped on install and upgrade. The version appears in two places:

text:
The version is displayed in the agent picker across all Copilot hosts (VS Code, CLI, Visual Studio):
When you select agents in Copilot, you'll see "Squad (vX.Y.Z)" in the dropdown — making it immediately clear which version you're running.

title: Upgrading Squad
heading: 2. Version Field (For Reference)
text: The frontmatter also includes a standalone version field:

title: Upgrading Squad

text:
You can also check your installed version from the command line:
The output will show your installed version (e.g., ).

title: Upgrading Squad
heading: Already Up to Date
text:
If you're already on the latest version:
Squad still runs any missing migrations in case a prior upgrade was interrupted.

title: Upgrading Squad
heading: 2. Commit the Upgrade
text: No changes to  — the diff is limited to Squad-owned files.

title: Upgrading Squad
heading: Tips
text:
Upgrade is safe. It only overwrites files that Squad owns. Your team state is never modified.
Don't customize . Any changes you make will be overwritten on the next upgrade. If you need custom behavior, use directives in  instead.
Re-running upgrade is harmless. If you're not sure whether an upgrade completed, run it again. It's idempotent.

title: SDK-First Squad Mode
heading: SDK-First Squad Mode
text:
# SDK-First Squad Mode
Phase 1 — Type-safe team configuration with builder functions.
Squad now supports SDK-First Mode: define your team in TypeScript with full type safety, runtime validation, and editor autocomplete. Instead of manually maintaining markdown files in , you write clean TypeScript, and  generates the governance markdown.

title: SDK-First Squad Mode
heading: What gets generated
text:
Running  generates these files:
  File   Condition   Contains  
 ------ ----------- ---------- 
     Always   Team roster, member list, project context  
     If  defined   Routing rules and default agent  
     For each agent   Agent role, model, tools, capabilities  
     If  defined   Ceremony schedule and agenda  
Protected files (never overwritten):
 /

title: SDK-First Squad Mode
heading: What is SDK-First Mode?
text:
In SDK-First Mode:
You write a  (or ) with builder functions
Squad validates your config at runtime with type guards

You version control only your TypeScript source — markdown is generated
This replaces manual , , and agent charters with a single source of truth in code.
When to use SDK mode: For a comparison of SDK-first mode versus CLI mode, see the Getting started guide.

title: SDK-First Squad Mode
heading: Common types reference
text: All builders share these core patterns:

title: SDK-First Squad Mode
heading: `defineTeam(config)`
text:
Define team metadata, project context, and member roster.
  Field   Type   Required   Notes  
 ------- ------ ---------- ------- 
     string   ✅   Team name (non-empty)  
     string   ❌   One-liner  
     string   ❌   Injected into agent prompts; describe the codebase, tech stack, conventions  
     string[]   ✅   Agent refs ( or ); order matters for routing fallback

heading: `defineAgent(config)`
text:
Define a single agent with role, charter, model preference, tools, and capability profile.
  Field   Type   Required   Notes  
 ------- ------ ---------- ------- 
     string   ✅   Unique identifier (kebab-case, no )  
     string   ✅   Human-readable title  
     string   ❌   Character description or link to charter  
     string   ❌   Model preference (e.g., , )  
     string[]   ❌   Allowed tools (e.g., )  
        ❌   Capability list with proficiency levels (see Common Types)  
        ❌   Lifecycle:  (default), ,

title: SDK-First Squad Mode
heading: `defineRouting(config)`
text:
Define typed routing rules with pattern matching, priority, and tier.
  Field   Type   Required   Notes  
 ------- ------ ---------- ------- 
        ✅   Pattern-based routing rules (see Common Types)  
     string   ❌   Fallback agent ref  
        ❌   , , or   
Routing tiers:  (skip ceremony),  (quick handoff),  (normal workflow),  (maximum governance).

title: SDK-First Squad Mode
heading: `defineCeremony(config)`
text:
Define ceremonies (standups, retros, etc.) with schedule, participants, and agenda.
  Field   Type   Required   Notes  
 ------- ------ ---------- ------- 
     string   ✅   Ceremony name  
     string   ❌   Event that triggers ceremony (e.g., , )  
     string   ❌   Cron expression or human-readable frequency  
     string[]   ❌   Agent refs  
     string   ❌   Freeform agenda or template

title: SDK-First Squad Mode
heading: `defineHooks(config)`

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
text:
# Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
Quick Start Prompts:  
  
  
  
Real patterns from using Squad effectively. These are techniques that work.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 1. Be Specific About Scope
text:
Good prompts describe the boundary, not just the task.
The second one tells the team: what's in, what's out, what's coming. Agents don't have to guess or ask.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 2. Name the Team Roster in Your Spec
text:
If you need specialized agents, say so in the prompt.
This creates exactly the team you need instead of defaulting to the generic roster.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 3. Use "Team" When Parallel Work Matters
text:
This spawns frontend (UI), backend (endpoints), tester (test cases), and lead (architecture) — all at once. They divide the work naturally.
For sequential work, name the agent:

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 4. Stack Decisions in Your Prompt
text:
Decisions made early prevent agents from asking questions later.
These go into  automatically. Future agents read them before working.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 5. Use Bullet Points for Multi-Part Tasks
text: Agents process lists better than paragraphs.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Use Direct Commands (Name an Agent)
text:
When the work is sequential or highly specialized.
  Use Case   Example   Why  
 ---------- --------- ----- 
  Code review   "Keaton, review the auth endpoints"   Only the lead does design review  
  Specialized skill   "Felix, optimize the database queries"   The performance expert works alone  
  Fix a specific mistake   "Dallas, fix the button styling"   Don't spawn the whole team for one file  
  Unblock someone   "Kane, help Lambert debug the test failure"   Point conversation between two agents

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Use Team Requests (Say "Team")
text:
When the work is parallel or cross-functional.
  Use Case   Example   Why  
 ---------- --------- ----- 
  New feature   "Team, build the search feature"   Frontend, backend, tests all start together  
  Sprint planning   "Team, plan the next two weeks"   Lead scopes, backend estimates, tester defines test cases  
  Problem-solving   "Team, we have a performance problem — investigate"   Frontend measures, backend profiles, infra checks caching  
  Iteration round   "Team, fix the feedback from the design review"   Multiple people can tackle different issues in parallel

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Use General Requests (No Name)
text:
When you don't care who handles it, or when it's context-dependent.
Squad routes this intelligently. Could be backend, could be ops, depends on team.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 1. Wait for Work to Complete Before Following Up
text:
Squad agents chain their own work. When you give a task, don't interrupt.
If you jump in after 2 minutes with "Did you test the form submission?", you break the chain. Let it finish.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 2. Check the Work Log, Not the Output
text:
When agents finish a batch, read the logs, not the code.
This asks Scribe to summarize. You'll see:
What was built
What decisions were made
What's left to do
What surprised them
Much faster than reading 5 agent outputs.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 3. Run Ralph When the Board is Full
text:
If you have a backlog of issues or PRs, let Ralph process them.
Ralph will:
Triage untriaged issues
Assign to team members
Spawn agents to work through them
Report progress every 3-5 rounds
Keep going until the board is clear
You can keep using the team for urgent work while Ralph grinds through the backlog.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 4. Use Parallel Decision-Making
text:
Agents can write decisions in parallel (they go to ). Scribe merges them.
This prevents "we decided different things" surprises.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 1. Activate Ralph When You Have Backlog
text:
Ralph is most useful when you have open issues.
Ralph will:
Check GitHub Issues for untriaged work
Ask the Lead to triage
Assign issues to team members
Spawn agents to work through them
Report every 3 rounds

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 2. Give Ralph a Scope If Needed
text:
By default, Ralph monitors issues, PRs, and CI.
Useful when:
You're in the middle of a PR and don't want Ralph to merge it yet
You only care about triaging issues, not closing them
You want to focus on one type of work

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 3. Ralph Reports Automatically Every 3-5 Rounds
text:
Don't ask for status — Ralph tells you.
When you see this, you can:
Let Ralph keep working (he will)
Say "Ralph, idle" to stop
Jump in with a different task
Check a specific issue

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 4. Use Ralph Between Sessions
text:
The  workflow runs Ralph on event-based triggers (issue close, PR merge, manual dispatch). For persistent polling when you're away from the keyboard, use  locally.
Ralph will:
Triage new issues
Assign them to team members
Trigger  if you have the coding agent enabled
This means your squad works even when you're not at the keyboard.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 5. Check Ralph's Status Before Wrapping Up
text:
Ralph does one check and reports:
If the board is clean, you can wrap up. If there's work, start Ralph for the next session.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 1. Set Permanent Rules Early
text:
The first or second session, establish conventions.
These go to . Every agent reads them before working. You only have to say them once.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 2. Use User Directives for "Never Again" Lessons
text:
When an agent makes a mistake, turn it into a directive.
These get stored as directives and agents follow them automatically in future sessions.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 3. Check decisions.md When Agents Disagree
text:
If Frontend does something one way and Backend does it another way, the decision is usually missing.
Now it's in the shared brain. Next agent to work on components will see this.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 4. Archive Outdated Decisions
text:
When a decision no longer applies, move it to a "Superseded" section.
You can edit  directly:
`markdown

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Superseded Decisions
text:
File naming (v1): "All files kebab-case" — SUPERSEDED by PascalCase convention in v2
API versioning (v1): "Use URL paths for versioning" — SUPERSEDED by headers-based versioning

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Active Decisions
text:
...
`
Agents know to ignore "Superseded" sections.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 5. Let Scribe Handle Decision Merging
text:
Agents write decisions to , Scribe merges them into .
You don't have to manually merge. Just ask:
Scribe will:
Read all files in 
Merge them into the canonical 
Deduplicate overlaps
Clean up the inbox
This happens automatically in mature teams, but you can force it anytime.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: 6. Personal History Files Build Over Time
text:
Each agent's  grows with every session. Check it when an agent seems lost.
If an agent keeps asking "where are the components?", their history might not have the right info. Edit it directly or remind them:

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Pitfall 1: Vague Scope = Agents Ask Questions Instead of Building
text:
Problem: "Build the API" — unclear what endpoints, what data model, what auth.
Solution: Be specific. Agents will ask if unclear, but clarity upfront saves rounds.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Pitfall 2: Interrupting Parallel Work
text:
Problem: You give a task to the team, then jump in after 2 minutes with a follow-up question.
Solution: Let parallel work finish. Squad agents chain automatically. Your interruption breaks the chain.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Pitfall 3: Forgetting That Decisions Persist
text:
Problem: You set a rule in session 1, forget about it, contradict it in session 5.
Solution: Read  at the start of every session, or ask Scribe:

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Pitfall 4: Not Using Ralph on a Full Backlog
text:
Problem: You have 10 open issues, but you keep working on small tasks manually.
Solution: Use Ralph for the backlog, stay focused on urgent work.
Ralph handles the backlog, you handle the critical path.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Pitfall 5: Too Many Agents at Once
text:
Problem: You spawn a huge team and context gets confusing.
Solution: Start small. 4-5 agents is a good team. Add specialists only when needed.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Pitfall 6: Lost Work Because You Didn't Commit `.ai-team/`
text:
Problem: You deleted the repo and lost all your team knowledge.
Solution: Commit  to git. It's permanent team memory.
Now anyone who clones the repo gets your team with all their learned knowledge.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Pitfall 7: Agents Stuck on the Same Mistake
text:
Problem: An agent keeps making the same error even though you fixed it in session 3.
Solution: The decision might not be in . Add it.
This goes to . Next time that agent works, they'll read it.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Pitfall 8: Ralph Running Out of Work Too Quietly
text:
Problem: Ralph finishes all the work but doesn't tell you, so you think he's still working.
Solution: Ralph reports every 3-5 rounds. If you don't see a report in a while, ask:
Ralph will check once and report. If the board is empty, you know you're done.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Pattern 1: Decision First, Implementation Second
text:
Before any agent writes code, the team agrees on the design.
This prevents "we built different things" surprises.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Pattern 2: Run Two Parallel Teams on One Repo
text:
If you have a large project, you can run one team on one feature, another team on another.
Requires good routing rules and clear ownership, but it works.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Pattern 3: Spike → Decision → Build
text:
For hard problems, do a spike first.
This prevents agents from building the wrong thing.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Pattern 4: Post-Mortem Decisions
text:
When something goes wrong, capture the lesson.
This prevents it from happening again.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Session Flow Template
text:
A typical high-performing session:
Start: Open Copilot, say "Team" or name an agent
Set context: Describe the work (scope, decisions, rules)
Parallel execution: Let agents work (don't interrupt)
Check logs: Ask Scribe what happened while you were reading code
Next round: Based on what Scribe told you, give follow-up work or start Ralph
Wrap up: Ask Ralph for status, commit , go home
Time to productive work: usually < 2 minutes.

title: Tips and Tricks for Managing Your Squad
heading: Reference: Who Does What
text:
When you're unsure who to ask:
  Task   Ask   Why  
 ------ ----- ----- 
  Architecture review   Lead (Keaton)   Design decisions are the lead's job  
  Fix a feature   The assigned agent   They know the context  
  Debug a test   Tester + Backend   Usually a logic error or missing setup  
  Design decision   Team (parallel)   All perspectives needed  
  Code review   Lead   Final arbiter  
  What happened last session?   Scribe   Scribe tracks everything  
  What's on the backlog?   Ralph   Ralph monitors the board  
  New decision   Any agent can propose, Scribe merges   Decisions are shared  
  Edit decisions.md   You or Scribe   Plain markdown, editable anytime

By: Hicks

title: First Session Walkthrough

title: First Session Walkthrough

`
Every agent reads these decisions before working. As the list grows, the team self-aligns — agents respect each other's architectural choices without you repeating them.

title: First Session Walkthrough
heading: 8. Set a Directive
text:
You notice something you want to standardize:
Squad captures this as a directive — no agent spawn needed:
This is written to  via the inbox. Every agent will see it in their next spawn.

title: First Session Walkthrough
heading: 9. Export Your Squad
text:
After a productive session, export your team for portability:
This snapshot contains everything — charters, histories, casting state, skills, and decisions. You can import it into another repo later:

title: First Session Walkthrough
heading: What to Try Next
text:
Add a team member: 
Run a ceremony:

Connect to issues:  (see GitHub Issues Walkthrough)

title: First Session Walkthrough
heading: Tips
text:
First session is the slowest. Agents have no history yet. After 2–3 sessions, they know your conventions and stop asking questions they've answered before.
Commit . It's your team's brain. Anyone who clones gets the team with all their knowledge.
Say "team" for big tasks. The word "team" triggers parallel fan-out across multiple agents.
Name an agent for focused work.  sends work to one specific agent.
Directives are sticky. Once captured, they persist across all future sessions.

title: GitHub Issues Walkthrough
heading: GitHub Issues Walkthrough
text:
# GitHub Issues Walkthrough
A step-by-step tour of Squad's GitHub Issues workflow. This connects your team to a real repository's issue tracker so agents can pick up issues, create branches, open PRs, and handle review feedback.
Prerequisite: The  CLI must be installed and authenticated ().

title: GitHub Issues Walkthrough
heading: 1. Connect to a Repository
text:
Tell Squad which repo to track:
Squad stores the issue source in team state:
From now on, Squad can read issues from that repo and create branches and PRs against it.

title: GitHub Issues Walkthrough
heading: 2. View the Backlog
text:
Ask to see open issues:
Squad pulls open issues via  and displays them:

title: GitHub Issues Walkthrough
heading: 3. Work on an Issue
text:
Pick an issue for an agent to work on:
Squad reads the issue details, routes it to the right agent, and kicks off the workflow:
Behind the scenes, the agent:
Creates a branch — named descriptively based on the issue (e.g., )
Does the implementation work — writes code, tests, whatever the issue requires
Opens a PR — linked back to issue #12, with a description of what was done
When the agent finishes:

title: GitHub Issues Walkthrough
heading: 4. Multiple Issues in Parallel
text:
You can assign multiple issues at once:
Each agent creates its own branch and works independently. If your repo supports worktrees, Squad can work on multiple branches simultaneously.

title: GitHub Issues Walkthrough
heading: 5. Handle Review Feedback
text:
After a PR is open, reviewers may leave comments. When you see feedback:
Squad routes the review to the agent who opened the PR:
The agent reads the review comments, makes the requested changes, and pushes new commits to the same branch:

title: GitHub Issues Walkthrough
heading: 6. Merge Completed Work
text:
When the PR is approved and ready:
The issue is closed automatically when the PR merges (if the PR body includes ).

title: GitHub Issues Walkthrough
heading: 7. Check Remaining Work
text:
After merging, see what's left:
Squad refreshes the backlog:

title: GitHub Issues Walkthrough
heading: Tips
text:
You don't pick the agent. Squad routes the issue to the agent whose expertise matches the issue's domain. A bug in the API goes to the backend agent. A UI issue goes to the frontend agent.
Agents name branches sensibly. Branch names include the issue number and a slugified title, so they're easy to find in .
PRs link to issues. The PR description includes a  reference so merging automatically closes the issue.
Review feedback is incremental. When you tell Squad about review feedback, the agent pushes new commits to the existing branch — no force-pushes, no new PRs.
Check  after issue work. Agents often record decisions while working on issues (e.g., "chose cursor pagination" or "added text index for search"). These decisions carry forward to future issues.

title: What's New

# What's New
⚠️ Experimental — Squad is alpha software. APIs, commands, and behavior may change between releases.
Full release history for Squad — from beta through the v1 TypeScript replatform. Jump to the version you're looking for, or read top-down to see how the project evolved.

title: What's New
heading: v0.9.1 — Current Release
text:
Shell agent name extraction — Robust multi-pattern fallback for extracting agent names from shell transcripts (#577)
Init scaffolding —  now scaffolds typed casting files; silences remote-lookup warnings (#579)
Personal squad global mode —  auto-discovers  (#576)
Release hardening — CI playbook rewrite, publish policy linting, docs consistency checks (#564, #557)
Doctor improvements — Actionable warnings and  existence checks (#565, #533)

title: What's New
heading: v0.9.0 — Major Feature Release
text:
Governance & Personal Squads
Personal Squad concept — Isolated developer workspace with own team.md, routing.md, and roster (#508)
Ambient discovery — Auto-detect personal squad at  via environment variables
Personal squad CLI — Commands: , , ,  (#508)
Governance isolation — Hooks, ceremonies, telemetry scoped per personal squad (#508)
Worktree Spawning & Distributed Work
Worktree creation — Coordinator spawns managed worktrees for parallel agent work (#529)
Cross-squad orchestration — Agents coordinate across multiple squads and worktrees (#446)
Persistent Ralph — Long-running daemon with watch + heartbeat health monitoring (#443)
Worktree .git guard — Regression detection for file vs directory confusion (#521)
Capability Discovery & Routing
Machine capability inference — Auto-detect available tools, models, hardware specs at session start (#514)
 label routing — Agents self-route based on discovered capabilities (#514)
Rate Limiting & Cost Control
Cooperative rate limiting — Predictive circuit breaker with token budget forecasting (#515)
Economy Mode — Automatic cheaper-model selection when quality thresholds permit (#500)
Token usage tracking — Per-agent cost visibility in session UI (#453)
Rate limit recovery — Actionable error messages for quota pressure (#464)
Ralph circuit breaker — Graceful degradation under model quota limits (#451)
Telemetry & Infrastructure
Auto-wire telemetry —  now self-configures, no manual wiring (#281)
OpenTelemetry propagation — Automatic context flow across squad sessions
Issue lifecycle template — Standardized workflow (creation → triage → assignment → completion) (#527)
KEDA autoscaling template — Kubernetes-based horizontal scaling for agent work (#516, #519)
GAP analysis verification — After-work checklist ensures all requirements met before completion (#473)
Session recovery skill — Find and resume lost sessions without restart (#442)
GitHub auth isolation skill — Multi-account GitHub workflows (#470)
Docs, Stability & Distribution
Astro site enhancements — Search tuning, section badges, coverage indicators (#524)
Autonomous agents guide — Comprehensive SDK guide for building agents (#492)
CLI terminal rendering — Fixed scroll flicker, reduced re-render churn, stabilized component keys
Upgrade hardening — Context-aware footers, EPERM handling, gitignore coverage (#544, #549)
ESM compatibility — Node 22/24 dual-layer fix, Node 24+ hard-fail with guidance (#449, #502)
Signal handling — SIGINT/SIGTERM graceful shutdown with 22+ regression tests (#486)
npm-only distribution — Removed GitHub-native channel; standard npm registry install

title: What's New
heading: v0.8.2

title: What's New
heading: v0.6.0 — The TypeScript Replatform
text:
The big rewrite. Everything moved to TypeScript with a clean SDK + CLI split.
Full rewrite — JavaScript → TypeScript with strict mode, ESM modules, Node.js ≥20
SDK + CLI split — Two npm packages:  (runtime, adapter, resolution) and  (commands, shell, REPL)
npm workspace — Monorepo with  and 
Interactive shell —  with no args launches rich REPL with streaming, welcome banner, session registry
OpenTelemetry integration — 3-layer API (low-level otel.ts, bridge otel-bridge.ts, init otel-init.ts), SquadObserver file watcher, Aspire dashboard support
Adapter layer —  bridging  to Squad's session interface
Constants extraction — , ,  centralized in constants.ts
Security —  with array args replacing  template strings (CWE-78 fix)
Wave-based development — 3 waves of parallel fan-out:
  - Wave 1: OTel, Aspire, SquadObserver, upstream docs
  - Wave 2: REPL polish, CWE-78 fix, config extraction, 119 new tests, Aspire E2E
  - Wave 3: Docs migration, site engine, 5 guides
CLI entry point — Moved from  to 
CRLF normalization — All 8 parsers normalize line endings; Windows users with  work correctly
